ci: Stories are shared in different ways.
lo: Imagining stories together; Ways stories are shared; Connecting with stories

s.d: Students can use iPads to photograph the physical puppets or Lego characters they build during developmental play, import them into Puppet Pals, and collaboratively record their voices to narrate a digital puppet show, addressing the 'Ways stories are shared' line of inquiry.

To capture their narrative writing and oral language, students can use Book Creator to design digital storybooks. They can upload photos of their playdough or physical story settings, draw digital illustrations, and use the audio recording feature to orally share the beginning, middle, and end of their stories.

Instead of Chatterpix, students use the 'Animate from Audio' feature. They select a character that matches their narratives from 'Mystery Storytellers' and record their voice to narrate the beginning, middle, and end of their story, focusing on storytelling vocabulary.

Connecting to their developmental play with Lego and playdough, students can work in small groups to take sequential photographs of their physical creations. This introduces them to stop-motion animation as an alternative medium for storytelling, reinforcing the concepts of sequencing and plot progression.

Students can explore digital storytelling by coding simple sequences in ScratchJR. By selecting characters and backgrounds, and using movement and speech blocks to act out a narrative, they develop computational thinking while demonstrating their understanding of story structure, settings, and character actions.

plannerText: In this Term 4 Prep unit under the theme 'How We Express Ourselves', students explore the central idea that 'Stories are shared in different ways.' Key activities include engaging in developmental play with costumes, finger puppets, Lego, and playdough to imagine and construct narratives. Students learn story structures (beginning, middle, end, characters, and settings) through oral retellings, simple narrative writing, and a 'Mystery Storytellers' program. An excursion to the Cranbourne Botanical Gardens introduces Indigenous storytelling perspectives. Assessment tasks involve observing students' collaborative story creation, narrative writing samples, and oral retells. The unit develops communication skills and vocabulary around creativity, audience, and movement, encouraging students to express their imagination through multiple multimodal forms of storytelling.

ci: Everyone has a role to play in the community.
lo: My communities; Contributing to the local community; Connections in my community

s.d: In the 'Wonder Wall' phase, students use 'Animate from Audio' with a character matching a community helper (e.g., a doctor or librarian) to record their initial questions about school roles.

For the Week 4 activity where students draw and label what a teacher's job involves, utilize the drawing and microphone tools in Seesaw. Prep students can use the digital canvas to draw the teacher, and instead of struggling with the cognitive load of writing complex labels, they can use the microphone to orally explain the different parts of the job. Teachers can also provide pre-typed, draggable sight-word labels for students to match to their drawings.

Connect the Week 5 school tour and the Mathematics focus on 'Directional Language - Map of school' using Beebots. Create a large floor grid featuring photos of the different school spaces visited on the tour (library, office, canteen). Students practice directional language (forwards, backwards, turn) by programming the Beebot to travel from the 'classroom' square to visit different community helpers on the map, reinforcing spatial awareness and community connections.

Enhance the Week 6 role-play activity ('What if I were the principal?') by setting up a Green Screen station. As students dress up and act out the roles of key staff (cleaner, groundskeeper, office staff), record them using the iPad camera or iMovie and replace the background with an actual photo of that staff member's workspace. This highly scaffolded, play-based integration makes the role-play immersive and visually contextualizes the community roles.

To consolidate the Week 7 staff interviews and prepare for the Week 8 assembly, create a collaborative digital class book in Book Creator. Small groups can add a photo of the staff member they interviewed, and use the audio or video recording tool to capture the staff member's answers or the students' own oral reflections on what they learned. This creates a multimodal, accessible artifact that can be easily presented to the school community.

plannerText: This Prep unit, under the theme 'How We Organise Ourselves', explores the central idea that everyone has a role to play in the community. The inquiry focuses on identifying school community members, understanding their contributions, and exploring connections. Key activities include guided school walks to observe different areas (library, canteen, office), creating mind maps, and generating questions on a Wonder Wall. Students engage in role-playing different staff roles (e.g., 'What if I were the principal?') and conduct authentic small-group interviews with key school personnel. In Mathematics, students explore directional language by mapping the school. Assessment and reflection culminate in a collaborative presentation at the school assembly, where students share videos and oral reflections about the different roles and responsibilities that keep their school community functioning. Vocabulary focuses on community, roles, responsibilities, and specific staff titles.

ci: Scientists follow a method to discover new things.
lo: The scientific process; Observing my world; Drawing conclusions

s.d: During the Week 4 'Nature Detective Walk', Prep students use iPad cameras to take macro photos of the plants and insects they find. Back in class, they upload their best photo to Seesaw and use the microphone tool to orally record their observations (e.g., 'I observe this bug has six legs'). This scaffolds the 'Observe' and 'Record' steps of the scientific method, allowing them to capture data without relying heavily on early writing skills.

For the Week 5 'Weather Watchers' activity, create a collaborative class journal in Book Creator. Each morning, the teacher scaffolds students to use the drawing tool or select emojis to record the daily weather (sun, rain, wind) on a new page. At the end of the week, the class flips through the pages on the interactive whiteboard to visually identify weather patterns, supporting the 'Analyse patterns' step of their inquiry.

Enhance the Week 6 'Ice Melting Challenge' by setting up an iPad on a tripod at the experiment station. As students test their different methods (salt, warm water, spoon), the teacher helps them take a photo every few minutes using Stop Motion Studio. Students can then watch the playback to visually 'Observe' the melting process sped up, helping them draw concrete conclusions about which method was fastest.

Integrate Sphero indi into the Week 7 'Rolling Ramp Investigation' to explore how objects move. After testing physical objects like balls and toy cars on wooden ramps, students use the indi colour tiles to build a path. They predict how the indi car will react to specific colour tiles (e.g., green for fast, red for stop) and test their predictions, reinforcing the 'Predict -> Test -> Observe' scientific process through play-based robotics.

Following the physical 'Sink or Float' water tub experiment outlined in the Mathematics inquiry, students complete a highly scaffolded digital sorting activity on Seesaw. Using a pre-made template with a T-chart, students use their fine motor skills to drag and drop digital stickers (pictures of the tested items like coins, corks, and blocks) into 'Sink' or 'Float' columns, reinforcing data recording and early graphing concepts.

plannerText: This Prep unit, under the theme 'How the World Works', explores the central idea that 'Scientists follow a method to discover new things.' The inquiry focuses on the scientific process (observe, predict, test, record, conclude) through hands-on, play-based experiments. Key activities include a 'Nature Detective Walk' to observe living things, a week-long 'Weather Watchers' pattern tracking activity, an 'Ice Melting Challenge', a rolling ramp physics investigation, and a sensory 'Mystery Materials Bag' exploration. The unit integrates Mathematics through a sink-or-float prediction and pictograph task. Assessment is primarily observational, capturing students' emerging conceptual understandings through drawings, oral retells, and simple charts. Vocabulary emphasizes scientific steps (predict, observe, conclude) and physical properties. The goal is to build students' confidence in identifying as scientists while developing their communication, thinking, and self-management skills.

ci: Living things share our world and have an important role in nature
lo: The different living things around us; The roles living things play in nature; How humans and other living things share space

s.d: During the Week 4 'Nature Detective Walk', students use iPads to take macro photos of plants and insects. Back in class, they use a pre-made Seesaw template to sort their photos into groups (e.g., by colour or number of legs). They can use the microphone tool to verbally record their scientific observations, reducing the cognitive load of writing while documenting their findings.

For the Week 5 'Weather Watchers' routine, create a digital weather journal in Book Creator. Each morning, Prep students use the drawing tool to sketch the weather and the camera to snap a photo of the sky. When comparing local weather to another location via webcam, students can easily add an emoji or a quick voice note to their page to communicate the patterns they notice.

During the Week 6 'Ice Melting Challenge', set up an iPad on a small tripod using Stop Motion Studio to capture a time-lapse of the ice cubes melting under different conditions (salt, warm water, etc.). Playing back the time-lapse allows students to visually and clearly compare the speed of the melting process, helping them draw concrete conclusions about their predictions.

Extend the Week 7 'Rolling Ramp Investigation' by introducing Sphero indi cars. After testing unpowered objects like balls and cylinders on wooden ramps, students use indi's colour tiles to direct the car down the ramps. This allows them to observe and verbally compare how a powered object moves differently on an incline compared to the gravity-driven objects they previously tested.

In the Week 8 'Mystery Materials Bag' lesson, after students identify the hidden items, they take a photo of their favourite material using Chatterpix Kids. They draw a mouth on the object and record a short voiceover pretending to be the material, describing its properties (e.g., 'I am a sponge, I am squishy and light!'). This provides a highly engaging, age-appropriate way to communicate scientific conclusions.

plannerText: The 'How the World Works' unit for Prep focuses on the central idea that 'Scientists follow a method to discover new things.' Over nine weeks, students engage in hands-on, inquiry-based learning to explore the scientific process, including predicting, testing, observing, and recording. Key learning experiences include a 'Nature Detective Walk' to observe and group living things, a 'Weather Watchers' routine to identify daily weather patterns, and an 'Ice Melting Challenge' to understand changes in materials. Students also investigate physical sciences by testing how different objects move on rolling ramps, and explore chemical sciences by identifying the properties of hidden objects in a 'Mystery Materials Bag.' The unit emphasizes developing thinking and research skills, encouraging students to view themselves as capable scientists who can gather data and confidently communicate their findings.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Discovering our past helps us know more about ourselves today.
lo: Our personal stories and histories; Reflecting on and recording our past; Our ancestry and families

s.d: During the Week 1-3 Provocation exploring the role of a scientist, students draw a picture of themselves as a scientist. They use iPads to take a photo of their drawing, draw a line for the mouth, and record their voice saying, 'I am a scientist and I discover...' This highly engaging, low-barrier activity builds oral language skills and helps Prep students form a personal connection to the unit's central idea.

For the Week 4 'Nature Detective Walk', students use iPads to take macro photos of the plants and insects they find. Back in the classroom, they upload these to a Seesaw activity to sort them into groups (e.g., by color or number of legs). Using the microphone and drawing tools, they circle key features and verbally record their observations, allowing them to demonstrate scientific grouping without the cognitive load of writing.

During the Week 5 'Weather Watchers' sequence, create a collaborative digital class journal. Each morning, a small group of students takes a photo of the sky, adds it to the day's page, uses the pen tool to draw the weather (sun, clouds, rain), and uses the audio button to record their observation. This multimodal approach helps Prep students sequence events and identify patterns over the week.

Enhance the Week 6 'Ice Melting Challenge' by setting up an iPad on a tripod with Stop Motion Studio. As students test different methods to melt the ice (salt, warm water, spoon), they take turns tapping the screen to capture frames. Playing the sequence back at high speed makes a slow physical change immediately visible and concrete, helping young learners easily compare results and draw conclusions.

Integrate robotics into the Week 7 'Rolling Ramp Investigation'. After predicting and testing how far physical objects (balls, toy cars) roll down cardboard ramps, introduce the Sphero indi. Students use the screen-free, tactile color tiles to program indi's speed and direction across the floor or down gentle inclines. This hands-on, play-based approach reinforces physical science concepts of movement, cause-and-effect, and testing predictions.

plannerText: This Prep unit, under the transdisciplinary theme 'How the World Works', explores the central idea that scientists follow a method to discover new things. Over nine weeks, students engage in hands-on, inquiry-based learning to understand the scientific process, observe their world, and draw conclusions. Key learning experiences include a 'Nature Detective Walk' to observe and group living things, a 'Weather Watchers' daily observation routine, an 'Ice Melting Challenge' to predict and test material changes, and a 'Rolling Ramp Investigation' to explore physical sciences and movement. The unit also integrates mathematics through sink-or-float predictions and pictographs. By explicitly practicing the steps of observing, predicting, testing, recording, and sharing findings, students develop critical thinking and communication skills while building their identity as young scientists.

ci: Relationships impact our wellbeing.
lo: Developing relationships; How those around us impact our behaviours and interactions; Keeping ourselves healthy and safe

s.d: During Lesson 3 ('Understanding feelings in friendships'), after reading 'The Feelings Book', students use the drawing canvas in Seesaw to illustrate a time a friend helped them feel better. They then use the microphone tool to record a short audio explanation of their drawing. This reduces cognitive load for Prep students who are still developing writing skills, allowing them to verbally articulate their understanding of emotional support.

For Lesson 6 ('Introducing LP attributes'), students work in small groups using iPads to take photos of each other role-playing Learner Profile attributes, such as sharing a toy to show being 'Caring'. They import these photos into PicCollage to create a simple visual poster. This makes abstract Learner Profile concepts concrete and leverages Prep students' natural inclination for dramatic play.

Following the Lesson 4 read-aloud of 'Have You Filled a Bucket Today?', students draw a picture of a kind action. They take a photo of their drawing using Chatterpix Kids, draw a mouth, and record their voice stating one 'bucket-filling' behavior (e.g., 'I play with everyone'). This highly engaging app requires minimal fine motor skills and perfectly captures their oral brainstorming.

To practice the sharing and turn-taking goals of Lesson 5, students work in pairs with a single Sphero indi and a shared set of color tiles. They must take turns placing one tile at a time to build a continuous path for the indi to travel between them. This reinforces cooperative play and negotiation in a tactile, screen-free coding environment ideal for early years.

Connecting to the Visual Art lessons on facial features and emotions, students use Sketchbook on iPads to draw emotional self-portraits. Teachers can provide a basic blank face template as a background layer. Students use different digital brushes to draw facial features representing specific emotions (happy, sad, angry). The digital format encourages risk-taking via the undo button and allows easy exploration of color.

plannerText: This Prep unit, under the theme 'Who We Are,' explores the central idea that relationships impact our wellbeing. The inquiry focuses on developing friendships, understanding how our behaviors affect others, and recognizing emotional balance. Throughout the unit, students engage with picture books like 'How Do Dinosaurs Play with Their Friends?' and 'Have You Filled a Bucket Today?' to unpack concepts of kindness, sharing, and empathy. Key learning experiences include co-constructing an essential agreement, orally brainstorming bucket-filling behaviors, and practicing turn-taking through collaborative block-building. Students also explore emotions in Visual Art by creating self-portraits that depict different feelings. The unit emphasizes social and emotional learning, helping early years students navigate classroom and playground dynamics while developing Learner Profile attributes such as being caring and communicative.

ci: Emotions can be expressed in different ways.
lo: Recognising our own and others' emotions; Responding to and interpreting our emotions; Different ways we express emotions

s.d: Connecting to the Visual Art lesson where students create individual masks to show an emotion, students can use the iPad camera to photograph themselves wearing their masks. They then upload the photo to Seesaw and use the microphone tool to orally explain why they chose specific colours and facial features to represent that feeling. This provides an accessible way for Year 1 students to document their artistic choices and practice expressive oral language without the cognitive load of extensive writing.

Following the reading of 'The Colour Monster' or 'My Many Coloured Days', students can collaboratively build a digital class book. Each student is assigned a page to draw their 'creative feelings people' using the in-app pen tool, selecting colours that match their chosen emotion. They can then use the audio recording feature to add a short voiceover explaining what makes them feel that colour, creating a multimodal text that supports early literacy and emotional intelligence.

To support the Music integration where students create a soundscape to accompany a homeroom book, Year 1s can use Garageband on iPads. Using the Smart Instruments or Live Loops, students can safely experiment with tempo and pitch to compose short audio clips that sound 'happy', 'sad', or 'scary'. This hands-on digital exploration makes abstract musical concepts concrete and allows them to easily export their emotional soundscapes to share with the class.

During the 'Tune In' phase where students work in groups to record their thinking about the word 'feelings', the teacher can project a Padlet board with columns for different emotions. Using iPads, students can capture their group's thinking by taking photos of their hand-drawn pictures or using Padlet's built-in drawing tool to add their visualisations directly to the board. This creates a dynamic, digital 'Wonder Wall' that makes the whole class's thinking visible and easily referable throughout the unit.

Replacing Chatterpix, students use Adobe Express to animate a character representing an emotion from 'The Colour Monster'. They focus on using an expressive tone and volume to communicate that feeling, demonstrating how the voice is a tool for expression.

plannerText: This Year 1 unit under the theme 'How We Express Ourselves' centers on the idea that emotions can be expressed in different ways. Students inquire into recognising, interpreting, and communicating their own and others' emotions. The unit integrates heavily with English through picture books like 'The Colour Monster' and 'My Many Coloured Days,' prompting students to explore how colours and illustrations depict feelings. In Visual Arts, students create emotion masks and group collages, focusing on facial expressions and colour associations. Music lessons involve experimenting with tempo and pitch to create emotional soundscapes, while PSPE focuses on teamwork challenges to practice emotional regulation and respectful communication. Formative assessments include 'See, Think, Wonder' routines and creating 'feelings people' to demonstrate their understanding of how body language, tone of voice, and artistic choices convey different emotional states.

ci: Services are established to help the needs of people in the community.
lo: Local services; Why we need local services in our community; How services meet personal and community needs

s.d: During the 'Moving to Mars' provocation where students design a new community, they can use Seesaw's drawing canvas instead of butcher's paper. Year 1 students can draw essential services (like hospitals and schools) and use the microphone tool to verbally explain why their Mars community needs these specific services, capturing their reasoning for formative assessment.

For the 'Learning walk' around the school community, students can use iPads to take photographs of different service locations or school helpers (e.g., the library, the nurse). Back in the classroom, they can import these photos into PicCollage to create a visual map or poster, adding simple text labels or emojis to identify each service.

When students choose a community helper to write an information report about, they can publish their work digitally using Book Creator. They can use the pen tool to illustrate their helper, type simple sentences, and use the audio recording feature to read their report aloud, supporting both literacy development and oral presentation skills.

To elevate the 'School community interviews' and guest speaker visits (like Nurse Simone), set up the Rodecaster and microphones to record a 'Community Helpers Radio Show'. Year 1 students can take turns speaking into professional microphones to ask their pre-prepared questions, making the interview process highly engaging and authentic.

As part of the 'Reflect and Act' phase where students write letters to local heroes (like Police Officer Jack or the Royal Flying Doctor Service), they can draw a picture of the helper, photograph it in Chatterpix Kids, draw a mouth, and record a 30-second verbal 'thank you' message to share alongside their written letters.

plannerText: This Year 1 unit under the theme 'How We Organise Ourselves' explores the central idea that services are established to help the needs of people in the community. Students inquire into local services, why they are necessary, and how they meet personal and community needs. The unit begins with an engaging 'Moving to Mars' provocation, prompting students to consider essential community services. Throughout the inquiry, students participate in learning walks around the school to identify local helpers, interview guest speakers like nurses and doctors, and research the Royal Flying Doctor Service. Learning experiences integrate literacy and social studies as students read information texts, sort needs versus wants, and write information reports on chosen community helpers. The unit culminates in a student-led presentation and a meaningful action component where students write letters of appreciation to local community heroes, demonstrating their understanding of interconnected community systems.

ci: Patterns help us to interpret the world around us.
lo: Patterns and cycles in our lives; Different patterns around us; How patterns can be created, interpreted and extended

s.d: Connecting to the 'butterfly journal observing changes to chrysalises' activity, students use Book Creator on iPads to maintain a digital observation log. They can use the iPad camera to snap daily macro photos of the classroom chrysalis and use the audio recording tool to verbally describe the changes, reducing the cognitive load of writing while capturing the scientific pattern of the life cycle.

During the local learning walk 'exploring our school grounds taking photos of objects... and observing patterns', students use their iPads to capture images of natural and man-made patterns (like leaves or bricks). They then import these into PicCollage, using the drawing tool to trace over the repeating shapes, explicitly highlighting the visual patterns they discovered in their environment.

To elevate the 'Design Technologies - making a model of a butterfly's lifecycle' task, students use ScratchJR to code a simple, animated life cycle. Using block coding appropriate for Year 1, they can sequence sprites (egg, caterpillar, chrysalis, butterfly) using 'timer' and 'hide/show' blocks to demonstrate the continuous, cyclical pattern of change over time.

Following the task where students 'use Get Epic to choose a cycle of interest' for their home learning, students use the Seesaw drawing canvas to create a cyclical diagram of their chosen animal. They can draw circular arrows to represent the continuous pattern and use the microphone tool to explain each stage, sharing this directly with families to celebrate their independent research.

Connecting to the 'Maths: Measurement - Time: Seasons' focus, students use Stop Motion Studio and tactile craft materials (like playdough or paper) to animate the cyclical pattern of a tree through the four seasons. Taking photos as they add blossoms, green leaves, falling orange leaves, and bare branches helps them visualize the repeating pattern of seasonal change.

plannerText: In this Year 1 unit under the theme 'How the World Works,' students inquire into the central idea that patterns help us interpret the world around us. The inquiry focuses heavily on natural cycles, specifically animal life cycles, seasons, and plant cycles. Day-to-day learning experiences include observing a live butterfly chrysalis in the classroom and keeping a daily observation journal to track its changes. Students also conduct a local learning walk around the school grounds using iPads to photograph physical patterns in nature and architecture. Furthermore, students utilize the Epic app to research a specific life cycle of their choice, creating models and responses to demonstrate their understanding of how natural patterns are created, interpreted, and extended over time.

ci: Plants are a life sustaining resource.
lo: Products derived from plants; Plants all around us; The interconnectedness of plants

s.d: To support the daily chrysalis observation, students use iPads to take photos of the chrysalis and add them to a digital Book Creator journal. Because Year 1 students are still developing writing stamina, they can use the audio recording tool to verbally describe the daily changes they observe, documenting the life cycle progression with low cognitive load.

During the local community pattern hunt, students use iPad cameras to photograph natural patterns in the school grounds. They import these images into PicCollage and use the drawing tool to trace over the repeating shapes (like leaf veins or brickwork), helping them visually isolate and identify the mathematical and natural patterns they discovered.

Connecting to the Design Technologies task of making a butterfly life cycle model, students use playdough or craft materials to build the four stages. Using Stop Motion Studio on iPads, they capture a sequence of photos to animate the caterpillar eating, forming a chrysalis, and emerging as a butterfly, reinforcing the concept of 'change' over time.

After using the Epic app to research a life cycle of their choice, students draw the adult animal or plant. They photograph their drawing using Chatterpix Kids, draw a digital mouth, and record their voice explaining the steps of its life cycle. This provides an engaging, age-appropriate way to present their research findings without heavy writing.

To consolidate their understanding of different cycles (like the seasons or the water cycle), teachers place image cards of the cycle stages on a Beebot grid mat. Students must program the Beebot to travel to the pictures in the correct chronological order, physically demonstrating their understanding of cyclical patterns while developing early sequencing and coding skills.

plannerText: This Year 1 unit, under the transdisciplinary theme 'How the world works', explores the central idea that patterns help us interpret the world around us. The inquiry focuses on life cycles of living things, the water cycle, and the seasons, emphasizing the key concepts of change, function, and perspective. Day-to-day learning experiences are highly interactive, beginning with a classroom provocation of a live butterfly chrysalis, which students observe daily. Students engage in local nature walks using iPads to photograph natural patterns, research various life cycles using the Epic app, and create physical models of a butterfly's life cycle. The unit integrates English through daily read-alouds and journal writing, Mathematics through measuring time and seasons, and Design Technologies through model making. Ultimately, students develop their critical thinking and communication skills by identifying, interpreting, and explaining the continuous cycles present in their natural environment.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Homes reflect our culture and environment.
lo: Different types of homes; Characteristics of a home; The importance of sustainable living spaces

s.d: During the 'Tune in' phase where students write about what they love about their home, they can draw a picture of their house, take a photo of it, and use Chatterpix Kids to make the house 'talk'. This allows Year 1 students to orally express their personal connections and preferences, reducing the cognitive load of writing while still capturing their understanding.

For the 'Find out' lesson exploring information texts about global homes (Gunyas, igloos, yurts, etc.), use Google Earth on iPads to virtually travel to the specific environments where these homes are found (e.g., the Mongolian steppe or the Arctic). This visual context helps Year 1s concretely connect the 'environment' aspect of the central idea to the physical 'form' of the houses they are reading about.

To support the 'Sort out' lesson where students create a Venn-diagram comparing two types of homes, use a Seesaw activity template. Instead of writing, students can drag and drop images of the homes into the circles and use the microphone tool to verbally explain the similarities and differences, making the comparative thinking process much more accessible for early writers.

Before the 'Go further' activity of building mini houses, provide students with ClassVR Headsets to experience immersive 360-degree views of diverse cultural homes. Experiencing the scale and interior of a yurt or a stilt house in virtual reality will spark creative thinking and provide rich, visual inspiration for their own physical mini-house designs.

For the final 'Learning Showcase', have students use the paid online version of Book Creator to document their journey. Each student can design a page featuring a photo of their physical mini-house, an embedded audio recording of the fact they researched on Epic, and digital drawings. This creates a multimodal, accessible portfolio piece to share with families.

plannerText: In this Year 1 unit under the theme 'Where We Are in Place and Time', students inquire into the central idea that homes reflect our culture and environment. The inquiry focuses on exploring different types of homes globally (such as gunyas, houseboats, igloos, and yurts), understanding the unique characteristics of a home, and introducing the importance of sustainability. Key learning experiences include reading information texts on Epic, comparing and contrasting two types of homes using Venn diagrams, and engaging in hands-on creation by building mini houses. Students also connect to the unit through Visual Arts by doing observational drawings of house patterns, and they share their personal perspectives by writing about what they love about their own homes. The unit culminates in a Learning Showcase where students present their findings and mini-house creations, demonstrating their developing communication and thinking skills.

ci: The actions we take influence our safety and wellbeing.
lo: Safe environments; How our actions affect ourselves and others; Keeping our body and mind healthy

s.d: Students use 'Animate from Audio' to make their paper organs 'talk,' explaining their function and how specific healthy habits keep the heart, lungs, or stomach safe.

At the 'Healthy Habits Inquiry Stations' focusing on hygiene, students can use iPads and Stop Motion Studio to animate a healthy routine, such as the sequential steps of brushing teeth or washing hands. Using small props or Play-Doh, students physically manipulate the objects frame-by-frame, which helps solidify their understanding of sequencing and the 'causation' concept of how these specific actions prevent illness.

For the 'Building Informative Writing' task where students draft and publish 'My Human Body Book', Book Creator provides an accessible digital publishing platform. Year 1 students can take photos of their physical illustrations, use the pen tool to add digital labels or headings, and utilize the audio recording feature to read their informative text aloud. This multimodal approach supports early writers by allowing them to capture their full knowledge verbally alongside their developing written skills.

For the final 'Healthy Habits presentations' shared at Student-Led Conferences, students can use Canva to design a digital poster or visual booklet. Using Canva's intuitive drag-and-drop interface, students can search for and arrange icons representing their personal healthy choices (e.g., specific fruits, water bottles, sleep routines). This provides a highly engaging, multi-modal element that gives students agency in how they visually communicate their learning to their families.

To elevate the 'Quiz Book for Student-Led Conferences' activity, teachers can help students input their generated multiple-choice and true/false questions into a class Kahoot! game. During the conferences, students can take on the role of 'quizmaster', hosting the game on a laptop or iPad and challenging their parents to answer questions about body systems and healthy living. This transforms a standard recall task into an interactive, gamified celebration of learning.

plannerText: This Year 1 unit explores the central idea that the actions we take influence our safety and wellbeing. Students investigate how the human body grows and changes, ways to keep the body and mind healthy, and how to maintain safe environments. Key learning experiences include creating life-size body models with paper organs, participating in healthy habits inquiry stations (focusing on nutrition, exercise, hygiene, and sleep), and drafting an informative 'My Human Body Book'. The unit authentically integrates Mathematics through data collection on daily wellbeing habits and safety choices. For their summative assessment, students design a quiz book and present their personal healthy habits using multi-modal elements during Student-Led Conferences.

ci: Artistic appreciation and expressions inspire our imagination and creativity.
lo: Forms of expression; Expressing thoughts and ideas; Understanding and appreciating art

s.d: Connecting to the 'I am' poem and watercolor activity, students can use iPads to photograph their physical paintings and import them onto a Book Creator page. They can then type their poem alongside the image and use the audio recording tool to read their poem aloud. This directly supports the literacy goal of 'reading with expression' while accommodating Year 2 fine motor and typing skills.

To support the 'Learner Profile comic book creating' engagement, students can use Canva's education templates to design a simple 3-panel comic strip. They can drag and drop characters and use speech bubbles to illustrate a Learner Profile attribute like 'Risk-Taker' or 'Balanced'. This visual approach reduces cognitive load while allowing them to explore digital layout and visual storytelling.

Students record thoughts from the perspective of their 'pet portraits' or fairy tale characters. This supports the literacy goal of 'reading with expression' and helps students answer the prompt 'What was the character thinking?' using a polished animation.

For the 'data collectors' activity exploring Art and Music forms, teachers can push a pre-made Frayer model or table template via Seesaw. As students listen to instruments or view artworks, they can use the drawing, labeling, and microphone tools to record their emotional responses and observations. This multimodal approach ensures data collection is accessible for Year 2 students who may find extensive handwriting challenging.

After writing their 'Haiku chalk poems' in the Music Courtyard, students can use iPads to capture photos of their ephemeral outdoor art. They can bring these photos into PicCollage to add digital text overlays of their poem or select digital stickers that reflect the mood and emotion of their words, beautifully blending physical outdoor creation with digital enhancement.

plannerText: This Year 2 unit under the theme 'How We Express Ourselves' centers on the idea that artistic appreciation and expressions inspire imagination and creativity. Students explore diverse forms of expression, including poetry, fairy tales, visual arts, and music. Key learning experiences involve creating 'I am' poems, designing pet portraits, and writing Haiku chalk poems in the school courtyard. Students act as 'data collectors' to analyze different art and music forms using graphic organizers like Frayer models. The unit integrates literacy by examining character thoughts and reading with expression, while also fostering social-emotional skills through 'Be Balanced' choice boards and Learner Profile comic books. Throughout the inquiry, learners are encouraged to reflect on how different forms of art make them feel and to communicate their own thoughts, feelings, and ideas using various creative mediums.

ci: Marketplaces reflect the needs and wants of communities.
lo: Needs and wants; The changing needs of communities; The design process

s.d: Enhance the 'Shipwrecked Provocation' and 'Needs vs. Wants Sorting Game' by creating a drag-and-drop Seesaw activity. Students can sort digital images of items (e.g., water, shelter, video games) into 'Need' and 'Want' columns. Crucially, they can use the microphone tool to verbally justify their choices, which reduces the cognitive load of writing for Year 2s while capturing their critical thinking and understanding of basic needs versus luxury wants.

Modernise the 'Need or Want Collage' activity by having students use PicCollage on their iPads. Instead of cutting physical magazines, students can search for safe digital images or use stickers to represent basic needs and luxury wants. This develops their digital literacy and fine motor skills as they resize, rotate, and categorise images onto a digital poster, which can then be easily shared to their Seesaw journals.

Elevate the 'I Want, I Need' song or chant activity by integrating Garageband. Students can record their vocal chants about needs and wants using the iPad microphone, and then use Smart Instruments or Live Loops to layer a simple, steady beat behind their voices. This multimodal approach reinforces the vocabulary in a highly engaging, memorable way while introducing basic audio production skills appropriate for their age.

During the 'Tinker Time in the Maker Space' and prototyping phase, students can use Lego Spike Essential kits to build a functional prototype of their community product. For example, if designing for wheelchair accessibility, they can build a motorized ramp or automatic door. Using the intuitive, icon-based block coding, Year 2 students can program simple movements, bringing their design thinking solutions to life.

For the culminating 'Share Fair', students can use Book Creator to document their entire Design Thinking journey. They can take photos of their physical prototypes, use the digital pen tool to label key features, and embed audio or video recordings explaining how their product meets a specific community need. This creates an interactive, accessible digital portfolio that empowers Year 2 students to confidently share their innovations with peers and parents.

plannerText: This Year 2 unit under the transdisciplinary theme 'How We Organise Ourselves' explores how marketplaces reflect the needs and wants of communities. The inquiry focuses on distinguishing between basic needs and luxury wants, understanding how community needs change over time, and applying the design thinking process to solve real-world problems. Key learning engagements include a 'Shipwrecked' provocation, needs versus wants sorting games, role-playing packing scenarios, and creating visual collages. The unit culminates in an action-oriented design thinking project where students empathise with community members (such as wheelchair users), ideate, and prototype a physical product to meet a specific local need. Finally, students present their community-focused innovations and prototypes at a collaborative Share Fair.

ci: Forces and motion are all around us.
lo: Forces around us; Scientific laws of motion; Innovations in technology

s.d: Connecting to the 'Gravity' lesson where students drop objects with different viscosities/weights, students can use the native iPad camera's slow-motion feature to record their drop tests. They then upload these videos to Seesaw and use the microphone tool to verbally explain their observations about how gravity pulls the objects to the ground, reducing the cognitive load of writing while practicing scientific vocabulary.

During the 'Friction' experiments where students test toy cars on different surfaces, introduce Lego Spike Essential. Students can build a simple motorized rover and code it to move forward. By testing their rover on smooth floors versus rough carpets, they can observe and discuss how friction impacts the motor's ability to move the vehicle, providing a hands-on introduction to mechanical forces and basic block coding.

Linked to the 'Magnet maze creation' activity, students can use Stop Motion Studio on iPads to bring their mazes to life. After drawing their maze, they can take frame-by-frame photos of a paperclip moving through the path. This allows Year 2 students to visually represent and explain the invisible 'pulling' force of magnetism in a creative, sequential way.

For the 'Writing to inform' lesson where students create information reports on Simple Machines, Book Creator is an ideal platform. Students can walk around the school with iPads to photograph real-world simple machines (like ramps or door wedges), insert them into their digital book, use the pen tool to draw arrows showing the direction of the force, and use the audio tool to record themselves reading their facts.

Students will leverage Seesaw's native video and audio recording capabilities to document their hands-on inquiry and experimental processes throughout the unit. They can record themselves conducting guided experiments on forces like magnetism, gravity, and friction, explaining their observations and conclusions. During the Maker Space immersion and when tinkering with simple machines, students can create short videos demonstrating how these machines make work easier. For the culminating Rube Goldberg machine assessment, students can record a video of their collaborative creation in action, narrating its design, the simple machines incorporated, and how it applies their understanding of pushes, pulls, and motion. This provides an authentic platform for students to demonstrate, explain, and reflect on their conceptual understanding and practical application of scientific principles.

plannerText: This Year 2 IB PYP unit, under the theme 'How the World Works,' investigates the central idea that forces and motion are all around us. Students explore the scientific laws of motion and technological innovations through highly hands-on, inquiry-based learning. The unit begins with a provocation at Twisted Science and a Maker Space immersion. Throughout the unit, students conduct guided experiments to understand specific forces, including magnetism, gravity, and friction. They tinker with simple machines like wedges, inclined planes, wheels, and axles to see how they make work easier. Learning is integrated across subjects, with students writing informational reports on simple machines in English and measuring distance, time, and mass during motion experiments in Mathematics. The unit culminates in a summative assessment where students apply their conceptual understanding of pushes, pulls, and simple machines to collaboratively design, build, and explain their own physical Rube Goldberg machines.

ci: Taking care of our Earth starts with the choices we make.
lo: Earth's resources; How our actions affect the environment; Ways we can care for our planet

s.d: During the 'Lunchbox waste collection' activity, students use iPads to take daily photos of their lunchbox waste. They use Seesaw's drawing and labelling tools to sort the items digitally into 'compost', 'recyclable', and 'general waste' categories, recording a short audio reflection on how they can reduce their packaging.

To explore 'Earth's resources', students use the Freeform app on iPads to create a visual sorting board. They can drag and drop images (or use the camera to take photos around the school) of resources like plants, water, wood, and rocks, categorising them into 'For Food', 'For Shelter', and 'For Living Things'.
Instead of Chatterpix, students animate a piece of rubbish (like a plastic bottle) to explain which of the '5Rs' it belongs to and why it should be kept out of landfill.

When students prepare their 'persuasive speech to convince school leaders', they can record their speeches using Green Screen Kits and iPads. In iMovie, they can replace the background with images of the specific school environments they want to improve (e.g., the playground or lunch area), making their call to action more visually impactful.

For the 'waste timeline' activity, students use Book Creator to design an interactive digital timeline. They can photograph different waste items (e.g., apple core, plastic wrapper) and place them on a timeline page, adding voice notes or short text to explain how many weeks or years each item takes to break down in landfill.

plannerText: This Year 2 'Sharing the Planet' unit focuses on the central idea that taking care of our Earth starts with the choices we make. Students inquire into Earth's finite resources, how human actions impact the environment, and practical ways to care for the planet. Key learning experiences include a provocation excursion to CERES to learn about zero waste, and a week-long lunchbox waste audit to categorize rubbish into compost, recycling, and landfill. Students investigate the '5Rs' (reduce, reuse, recycle, rot, refuse) and explore how long different materials take to break down by creating a waste timeline. As a summative action, students engage in a personalised inquiry project where they write and present persuasive speeches to school leaders and the parent community, advocating for positive environmental changes within the school environment.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Landmarks and places of significance tell stories about our history.
lo: The significance of places to different people and cultures; The connection between landmarks and identity; How landmarks and symbols represent our history

s.d: During the campus walking tour to identify local Wesley landmarks, students use iPads to capture photos of significant buildings. Returning to class, they upload these to Seesaw and use the drawing tool to annotate the images with simple map symbols, directly supporting the Mathematics focus on location and transformation.

Connecting to the Mathematics mapping and directions activities, teachers use Canva to design and print simple grid maps featuring the Australian landmarks students researched. Students then program Beebots to navigate between these sites, physically practicing positional language and interpreting map symbols.

After unpacking the 'Dreaming Dictionary' and creating short stories using Aboriginal art symbols, students use Stop Motion Studio to animate their narratives. Using cut-out paper symbols or playdough, they capture frame-by-frame movements, allowing them to communicate their understanding of Indigenous storytelling in a highly engaging, age-appropriate medium.

To enhance the 'virtual holiday' learning experience, students use Google Earth to explore 3D views of natural and built Australian landmarks. They take screenshots of their chosen locations and add them to a collaborative Book Creator, using the audio tool to verbally record facts about the site's significance, accommodating developing Year 2 writing skills.

When students co-create and practice their Acknowledgement of Country for assembly, they record their presentation using Green Screen Kits. With teacher support in iMovie, they overlay their performance onto images of local Boonwurrung land or natural landmarks they have studied, deepening their visual connection to the concept of place and heritage.

plannerText: This Year 2 unit, under the theme 'Where We Are in Place and Time,' explores how landmarks and places of significance tell stories about our history. Students investigate the connection between place and story, focusing on natural and built Australian landmarks, as well as local Wesley College sites. Key learning experiences include campus walking tours, virtual holidays using maps, and researching significant sites like Uluru and the Great Barrier Reef. A major focus is placed on Indigenous Australian perspectives, where students learn about the Boonwurrung people, explore Dreaming stories, decode Aboriginal art symbols, and co-create a class Acknowledgement of Country. The unit integrates Mathematics through mapping, directions, and symbols, while Literacy is supported through mentor texts, data collection, and writing short narratives.

ci: Our relationships with others impact our lives.
lo: Importance of relationships; Developing and maintaining healthy relationships; The impact of relationships on wellbeing

s.d: After the Toolbox Education incursion, Year 2 students can use Book Creator to design their own interactive 'Calm Down Toolbox' manual. They can use the iPad camera to take photos of themselves demonstrating the 5 tools (e.g., moving their body, doing a body scan, mindful breathing) and use the audio recording feature to explain how each tool helps them when their emotions get 'shaken up.' This supports literacy development while providing a personalized wellbeing resource.

To explore the question 'What character strengths make me a great friend?', students can use Canva to design a digital 'Great Friend' poster. Using Canva's kid-friendly templates and elements, Year 2 learners can drag and drop icons that represent positive friendship traits (e.g., sharing, listening, taking turns) and type short, descriptive sentences. This connects directly to the social thinking focus on interpersonal relationships and empathy.

Connecting to the UR Strong 'Friendology' program, students can use Puppet Pals to animate a scenario where a 'friendship fire' occurs. Working in pairs, they can select characters and record their voices role-playing the 'talk-it out' strategy to resolve the conflict. This provides a safe, low-stakes digital environment for 7-8 year olds to practice conflict resolution and courteous communication before applying it in real-life playground situations.

Following the 'How to apologize' mentor text focus, students can draw a picture of a time they had to say sorry, or photograph a classroom object (like a toy that was fought over). Using Chatterpix Kids, they draw a mouth on the image and record a 30-second audio clip practicing a genuine, structured apology. This is highly engaging, developmentally appropriate for Year 2, and reinforces the oral language component of repairing relationships.

To capture the PSPE portfolio task on teamwork and fairness, teachers can record short video clips of students playing cooperative games. Students then use Seesaw to annotate their specific video clip using the microphone and drawing tools, circling moments where they demonstrated 'inclusive behaviour' or 'fairness' and verbally reflecting on how their actions impacted the group's wellbeing and shared success.

plannerText: This year-long Year 2 'Who We Are' unit explores identity and belonging through the central idea that our relationships with others impact our lives. The inquiry focuses on the importance of developing and maintaining healthy relationships, understanding the changeable nature of these connections, and recognizing body-mind connections. Key learning experiences include the Toolbox Education incursion, where students learn five practical tools (like mindful breathing and body scans) to regulate their emotions when they feel 'shaken up.' Students also engage with the UR Strong Friendology program to navigate 'friendship fires' using the talk-it-out strategy. Literacy and wellbeing are integrated through mentor texts exploring how to apologize and what makes a great friend. Additionally, PSPE lessons emphasize fundamental movement skills, teamwork, and fairness, allowing students to reflect on how their inclusive behaviors and cooperative choices positively affect their peers and their own emotional intelligence.

ci: Celebrations and traditions shape the culture of a community.
lo: The ways personal and cultural events are celebrated; Our appreciation of the diversity of cultures; How celebrations shape community identity

s.d: To enhance the Week 2 'Message to families' activity where students map their family origins, use Padlet's 'Map' format. Instead of just using physical string on a paper map, students can drop a digital pin on their family's country of origin. They can upload a family photo or a picture of a cultural artifact, and type a short sentence about their heritage. This creates an interactive, collaborative class map that visually represents the diversity of the room.

For the Week 1 activity distinguishing between a 'celebration' and a 'commemoration' (like ANZAC Day), create a digital drag-and-drop sorting activity in Seesaw. Students can drag images of various events (e.g., birthdays, weddings, ANZAC Day wreaths, Remembrance Day poppies) into the correct columns. To reduce writing load and assess understanding, require students to use the microphone tool to verbally justify why they placed one specific event in the commemoration column.

During the 'What does it mean to be Australian?' research task, where the class splits into 12 groups to research topics like traditional food, flags, and tourist destinations, have the class collaboratively author a digital book using Book Creator. Each group takes ownership of one chapter/page to document their findings using text, images, and voice recordings. This provides a highly engaging, multimodal alternative to a standard PowerPoint or paper poster.

To support the 'Step in, Step Out, Step Back' thinking routine after reading 'My Two Blankets', students can use Chatterpix Kids to demonstrate empathy and perspective-taking. Students take a photo of the main character, Cartwheel, draw a digital mouth on her, and record a 30-second voiceover speaking from her perspective about how it feels to navigate a new culture and language. This is a highly engaging, low-barrier way for Year 3s to express complex emotional insights.

For the final Personal Project where students present their researched cultural events, foods, and traditions to the class, elevate the presentation using Green Screen Kits and iPads (via iMovie). Students can select an image of their culture's famous landmark or festival to use as their digital background. They can then film themselves acting as a 'cultural tour guide' sharing their research, building oral communication skills and adding an exciting multimedia element to their summative task.

plannerText: This Year 3 unit under the theme 'How We Express Ourselves' explores the central idea that celebrations and traditions shape a community's culture. Students begin by investigating their own family origins, mapping where their ancestors came from to highlight classroom diversity. They delve into what it means to be Australian by researching cultural symbols, traditional foods, flags, and significant events like ANZAC Day, learning to distinguish between celebrations and commemorations. Through literature like 'My Two Blankets,' students develop empathy and explore different cultural perspectives. The unit culminates in a personal project where students research and present their own cultural heritage, including significant events, customs, and tourist destinations. Finally, students reflect on their learning by collaboratively writing a verse for the song 'I Am Australian' and creating a cultural quilt, celebrating their shared identity and diverse backgrounds.

ci: The rights and responsibilities of citizens are determined by the rules and laws of a community.
lo: The differences between rules and laws; People responsible for enforcing rules and laws; Rights and responsibilities of citizens; The consequences when rules, laws and rights are not met

s.d: Book Creator is an excellent alternative, allowing students to compile their learning into interactive digital books. For the 'invent and play card games' provocation, students can create a comprehensive digital rulebook, incorporating text, images of their game components, and even embedded videos demonstrating gameplay and explaining the necessity of rules. When writing persuasive texts about new school rules, students can dedicate a chapter to their arguments, using multimedia to enhance their message. Research on local government roles and historical laws can be presented in dedicated sections, combining text, images, and audio narration. This tool fosters a deep, organized, and multimedia-rich exploration of the unit's themes, culminating in a shareable artifact that showcases their understanding of rules, laws, and responsible citizenship.

For the English connection focusing on persuasive texts about school rules or new laws, students can use Microsoft Word to draft their arguments. Year 3 students can utilize the 'Dictate' tool if they struggle with typing speed or spelling, allowing their persuasive ideas to flow freely. Afterwards, they can use the 'Read Aloud' feature to listen back to their writing, helping them self-edit and ensure their arguments about rights and responsibilities make sense to their audience.

To support the Social Studies inquiry into local government and community rules, students can use Canva to design a public service announcement (PSA) poster. Using age-appropriate, drag-and-drop templates and icons, students can visually communicate a specific local law (e.g., wearing bicycle helmets or school zone speed limits), highlighting who enforces the law and the consequences of not following it to keep the community safe.

To connect the Maths focus on 'rules for order' (measurement and place value) to the central idea, students can build and program a simple motorized vehicle using Lego Spike Essential. The teacher challenges them to program the vehicle to travel a specific distance. Students must measure the distance using informal units versus formal standard units (centimetres), discovering firsthand that without the 'rule' of standard measurement, instructions and outcomes become chaotic.

To address the student-generated inquiry questions (e.g., 'What is the weirdest law?', 'What laws are in place for safety?'), the teacher can set up a collaborative Padlet board with categorized columns. As students research using Epic or teacher-curated resources, they post their findings to the board. Year 3 students can easily add text, images, and safely comment on peers' posts, creating a shared digital gallery that highlights the diversity and purpose of different laws.

plannerText: This Year 3 unit under the theme 'How We Organise Ourselves' investigates how the rights and responsibilities of citizens are determined by the rules and laws of a community. Students explore the fundamental differences between rules (guidelines for home or school) and laws (government-enforced regulations). The inquiry delves into who is responsible for making and enforcing these laws, and the consequences when they are broken. Learning experiences include a hands-on provocation where students invent and play card games to understand the necessity of rules for fairness and order. Cross-curricular connections are strong, with students writing persuasive texts in English about new school rules, and exploring the 'rules of order' in Mathematics through standard measurement and place value. Students also research local government roles, historical laws, and the democratic decision-making process, ultimately understanding their own roles as responsible citizens.

ci: Humans use their understanding of scientific principles to take action.
lo: The work of scientists; The process of learning; How science brings about new understandings and changes

s.d: During the PROE (Predict, Reason, Observe, Explain) provocation stations, such as the bicarb and vinegar experiment, students can use Seesaw's multimodal tools to document their learning. By taking a photo of the reaction, using the drawing tool to annotate the variables, and recording their voice to explain the chemical change, Year 3 students can demonstrate their understanding without the cognitive load of extensive writing.

To consolidate their understanding of energy transference (conduction, convection, and radiation), students can import photos of their hands-on experiments (like the melting chocolate chips or boiling water) into Explain Everything. They can use the digital whiteboard tools to draw arrows showing the direction of heat transfer and particle movement, narrating the scientific principles at play to make their invisible thinking visible.

Following the physical activity of building atoms and molecules with marshmallows and toothpicks, students can transition to digital modeling using Tinkercad. They can manipulate basic 3D spheres and cylinders to design H2O or CO2 molecules. These digital models can then be exported and printed on the school's 3D Printers, providing a tangible, student-created artifact of their chemical science inquiry.

Aligning directly with the Science Fair portfolio task, students can use Canva to design an infographic biography of their chosen famous scientist. Using Year 3-appropriate templates, students will organize their researched fact fragments, highlighting the scientist's motivations, their specific field of science, and the impact their discoveries had on society, thereby developing their visual communication and digital design skills.

For the 'Nude Food' action project, students can use Microsoft Excel to input their daily lunchbox plastic data. Teachers can scaffold this by providing a shared spreadsheet where students enter their tallies. Students will then learn to highlight their data and use the 'Insert Chart' feature to generate simple column graphs, visually tracking their progress in reducing plastic waste and meeting ACARA data representation standards.

plannerText: This Year 3 unit under the theme 'How the World Works' explores the central idea that humans use their understanding of scientific principles to take action. Students investigate the work of scientists, the scientific method, and how science brings about new understandings. The inquiry begins with hands-on provocation stations where students observe chemical and physical changes, utilizing the PROE (Predict, Reason, Observe, Explain) framework. They delve into physical and chemical sciences, specifically focusing on energy transference (conduction, convection, radiation) and the states of matter, including building models of atoms and molecules. As a major project, students research a famous scientist to create a biography and infographic, culminating in a Science Fair where they conduct and present their own experiments. Finally, students take action through a 'Nude Food' project, collecting data on lunchbox waste to promote sustainable choices.

ci: Adaptation and sustainability ensure the survival of living things in changing environments.
lo: Adaptations and structural features of living things; Roles of living things in an ecosystem; Actions that benefit or harm ecosystems

s.d: During the 'Missing Link' assessment where students invent an animal with four specific adaptations, they can use Tinkercad on laptops to design a 3D model of their creature. This requires them to manipulate basic 3D shapes to intentionally represent the structural adaptations they chose, reinforcing the connection between physical form and environmental function.

For the summative documentary project on endangered animals, students can use Green Screen Kits and iPads to superimpose themselves directly into their assigned ecosystem (e.g., the Great Barrier Reef or the Amazon Forest). They can then use iMovie to edit their footage, add their researched voiceovers, and sequence their storyboards.

When investigating mimicry and camouflage, students can import a photograph of their chosen animal's natural habitat into Explain Everything. Using the drawing tools, they can sketch their animal blending into the background, and then use the screencasting feature to record an audio explanation of how this adaptation ensures survival.

During the Animal Classification Jigsaw activity, expert groups can use Canva to collaboratively design an educational infographic about their assigned category (e.g., amphibians or reptiles). They can utilize Canva's image library and text tools to visually summarize key characteristics before presenting their findings to the rest of the class.

To demonstrate their understanding of food webs and ecosystem destruction, students can use ScratchJR on iPads to code a simple, animated food chain. After programming the normal flow of energy between plants, prey, and predators, they can duplicate the project and remove a vital food source to code the negative cascading effects on the ecosystem.

plannerText: This Year 3 'Sharing the Planet' unit explores how adaptation and sustainability ensure the survival of living things in changing environments. Students begin by investigating the characteristics of living versus non-living things and classifying animals into distinct groups. They delve into the physical and behavioral adaptations of various animals, exploring concepts like hibernation, migration, camouflage, and mimicry. The inquiry progresses to understanding ecosystems, food webs, and the delicate balance required for survival. Students examine human impacts on the environment, both positive and negative, connecting their learning to UN Sustainable Development Goals 14 (Life Below Water) and 15 (Life on Land). For their summative assessment, students synthesize their knowledge by designing a 'missing link' animal with specific adaptations and collaboratively producing an educational documentary about an endangered animal within a specific global ecosystem.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploration leads to new discoveries that can shape and change society.
lo: The factors that motivate exploration (Causation); How people reflect on discoveries from the past to inform change (Change); How exploration brings about change and new understandings for people, places and society (Change)

s.d: Connecting to the 'Convict Love Tokens' activity (Page 45-46), students can elevate their cardboard designs by using Tinkercad on their laptops to digitally model their tokens. Year 3 students can use basic 3D shapes and text tools to engrave their researched convict's initials, crime, and sentence length. Printing these on the school's 3D Printers provides a tangible, lasting artifact that deepens their historical empathy and connection to the First Fleet database research.

For the 'First Fleet Voyage Map' lesson (Page 46), students can use Google Earth to trace the actual route taken by the First Fleet from Portsmouth to Botany Bay. They can use the 'Measure' tool to comprehend the vast distances. Students can take screenshots of key historical stops (e.g., Rio de Janeiro, Cape of Good Hope) and upload them into a Seesaw map template, using the microphone tool to record their summaries of what life was like aboard the ships at each specific location.

Replacing the Green Screen activity, students use 'Animate from Audio' to take on the persona of a First Fleet convict or an Indigenous Australian. They record their 'First Contact' scripts, focusing on the different perspectives of colonization.

For the 'Explorer Project' (Page 50), students can synthesize their research by authoring an interactive multimedia biography using Book Creator. They can dedicate pages to the explorer's 'Fact File', 'Timeline', and 'Learner Profile' attributes. Year 3 students can easily embed images, draw exploration routes using the pen tool, and use the audio recording feature to narrate the 'push and pull' factors that motivated their specific explorer.

Students can create interactive digital books to deeply explore and present their understanding of historical events and multiple perspectives. They can craft narratives from the viewpoint of a First Nations Australian, a convict, or an explorer, incorporating text, images, audio recordings (e.g., personal reflections, interviews), and video clips to bring their research to life. This allows for a rich, multimedia exploration of the First Fleet's journey, the impact of colonization, and concepts of Reconciliation. The culminating Mars colony project can also be presented as a detailed digital book, showcasing their design, research, and application of the IB Learner Profile and UN Sustainable Development Goals in a comprehensive and engaging format.

plannerText: This Year 3 unit under the theme 'Where We Are in Place and Time' explores how exploration leads to new discoveries that shape society. Students investigate the push and pull factors motivating historical exploration, with a significant focus on the First Fleet, convicts, and Captain Cook's voyages. Crucially, the unit examines these historical events through multiple perspectives, particularly highlighting the impact of colonization on First Nations Australians and exploring concepts of Reconciliation and 'invasion'. Learning experiences include analyzing convict databases, creating convict love tokens, mapping the First Fleet's journey, and researching famous explorers. The unit culminates in a forward-looking project where students apply their understanding of exploration, the IB Learner Profile, and UN Sustainable Development Goals to design a sustainable human colony on Mars.

ci: An inquiry into the nature of the self; beliefs and values; personal, physical, mental, social and spiritual health; human relationships.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded

s.d: To elevate the 'How do people see me?' lesson, students can use Canva to create a digital 'Mirror' poster. Using iPads, they take a selfie, use the background remover tool, and place their image in the center of a mirror template. They then surround their portrait with text boxes and digital stickers representing their personal strengths and positive attributes, reinforcing their self-identity in a visually engaging way.
Students animate the 'Brain Animals' (Guard Dog, Wise Owl) to explain their role in emotional regulation, focusing on using a voice that matches the animal's temperament.

Students can create interactive digital books to document their learning journey throughout the unit. For 'fixed vs. growth mindset', they can write and illustrate stories, create character profiles, or embed short video reflections on their own mindset shifts. To explore emotional regulation and the 'Grow Your Mind' program, students can design pages dedicated to each 'brain animal', explaining triggers and strategies, incorporating audio recordings of their explanations or short video demonstrations. For 'Friendship Fires' and interpersonal relationships, students can create narrative books depicting scenarios, embedding short role-play videos of conflict resolution, and writing reflections on choices and outcomes. The books can serve as a portfolio of their resilience strategies, self-reflections, and understanding of healthy relationships, allowing for a multi-modal demonstration of learning and a personalized resource they can revisit.

Directly supporting the planner's suggestion for the 'Building Resilience' lesson, divide students into 6 groups and assign each a specific resilience strategy. Using laptops or iPads, each group collaboratively designs a 5-question Kahoot quiz to test their peers' understanding of their assigned strategy. This shifts students from consumers to creators and reinforces their understanding of resilience tactics.

To explore the URStrong 'Friendship Fires' and 'Mean on Purpose' scenario cards, students can work in pairs using Puppet Pals. They select digital characters and a setting to animate a given friendship conflict. They record the dialogue to demonstrate how to safely and respectfully resolve the 'fire' using the URStrong strategies taught in class, eventually exporting the final video to Seesaw for teacher feedback.

plannerText: This Year 3/4 'Who We Are' unit focuses on the central idea that individuals use different strategies to overcome challenges and keep themselves safe. Throughout the year-long inquiry, students explore the concepts of perspective, change, and responsibility. Key learning engagements involve unpacking the difference between a fixed and growth mindset through picture books, hands-on challenge stations, and self-reflection. Students learn about emotional regulation using the 'Grow Your Mind' program, identifying different parts of the brain as animals (like the Guard Dog) to understand their emotional triggers. They also develop practical resilience strategies to manage setbacks. In the second term, the focus shifts to interpersonal relationships using the URStrong program, where students role-play 'Friendship Fires' and learn how to navigate conflicts, make informed choices, and maintain healthy, respectful friendships both face-to-face and online.

ci: Technology transforms how we create and share ideas.
lo: How technology expands creative possibilities; The evolution of communication tools; The impact of technology on expression

s.d: In Lesson 6 (Surveying Teachers) and Lesson 8 (Analysing Teacher Survey Results), students use Microsoft Forms to collaboratively design and distribute their survey about classroom technology use. Once the data is collected, they export the results to Microsoft Excel to generate bar charts and pie graphs, identifying patterns in the most popular apps used across different year levels.

For Lesson 10 (Cybersafety Posters for Younger Students), students use Canva's education suite to design their kid-friendly cybersafety posters for Prep-3 learners. They can utilize Canva's extensive library of age-appropriate icons, shapes, and templates to visually communicate concepts like safe passwords and online kindness without relying on heavy text.

During Lesson 4 (Evolution of Communication), students use a collaborative Padlet board set to the 'Timeline' layout for their research carousel. As they rotate through stations exploring historical tools like typewriters and radios, they post the strengths and limitations of each tool, co-constructing a shared, interactive digital timeline of communication history.

Microsoft Teams provides a robust platform for Year 4 students to design, organize, and facilitate their 'Teach Meet' technology workshops. Leveraging its native video tools, students can record engaging presentations and tutorials on cybersafety and creative app usage for younger Prep-3 students, directly replacing the video sharing functionality of 'flip'. Teams channels can be used to foster collaborative group work, allowing students to share resources (including their creations from Canva, Book Creator, and Stop Motion Studio), co-author presentations, and manage their project timelines. The platform's meeting features enable students to conduct live workshop sessions, practicing their communication and self-management skills in a real-world context. This approach not only supports the creation and sharing of ideas but also reinforces digital citizenship by operating within a secure, collaborative online environment, perfectly aligning with the unit's focus on responsible and creative technology use.

In Lessons 17 and 18 (Tech Workshops), Year 4 learners use Stop Motion Studio as one of the core creative tools to teach the Prep-3 students. Acting as digital leaders, the Year 4s guide the younger students step-by-step to create a simple animation, demonstrating patience, clear verbal communication, and safe iPad handling.

plannerText: This Year 4 unit, under the theme 'How We Express Ourselves,' investigates the central idea that 'Technology transforms how we create and share ideas.' Throughout the inquiry, students explore the evolution of communication tools, from historical methods to modern digital platforms, analyzing their strengths and limitations. A significant focus is placed on digital citizenship and cybersafety, with students evaluating ethical scenarios and learning to make principled, safe choices online. As a summative action, Year 4 learners take on a leadership role by designing and facilitating a 'Teach Meet' technology workshop for younger Prep-3 students. They gather data via teacher surveys, explore creative apps like Canva, Book Creator, and Stop Motion Studio, and develop age-appropriate cybersafety resources.
