This hands-on process develops their communication, self-management, and research skills while fostering a deep understanding of responsible and creative technology use in their community.

ci: Systems connect us.
lo: Human systems; The interconnections that exist within and across systems; The influence of systems on communities

s.d: During the 'Systems around us' school walk in Session 3, students use iPads to take photos of everyday school systems (e.g., recycling bins, library scanners). They import these images into Explain Everything to create an annotated interactive diagram, using digital drawing tools and voice recordings to explain the 'parts', 'purpose', and 'connections' of each system before exporting their work to Seesaw.

To document the 'Mini Water Cycle in a Bag' experiment in Session 4, students use iPads to capture a daily photo of their zip-lock bag using Stop Motion Studio. Over the course of the week, they compile these images into a short time-lapse animation, adding voiceovers to explain the scientific processes of evaporation, condensation, and precipitation occurring within their closed natural system.

Following the 'Web of Life' string activity in Session 5, students use Canva to design a digital food web or ecosystem flow map for a local environment like Elsternwick Park. They use Canva's graphics library to represent different organisms and draw connecting arrows to visually demonstrate how the removal of one element (like frogs or water) impacts the balance of the entire ecosystem.

Students will leverage Microsoft Teams' integrated video capabilities to demonstrate their understanding of systems. They can record short video tours identifying everyday systems within the school, narrate their hands-on experiments like the mini water cycle, or present their findings on waste management and recycled paper. These videos can be uploaded to dedicated Teams channels or assignments, fostering peer feedback and discussion through comments and reactions, thereby deepening their inquiry into the form, function, and interconnectedness of human and natural systems.

To synthesize their learning from the 'Landfill vs Compost' videos, students use Microsoft PowerPoint to create a digital Venn diagram. They insert overlapping circle shapes, use text boxes to categorize waste types and processes, and add digital icons to represent decomposers, helping them visually organize the similarities and differences between the two waste management systems.

plannerText: This Year 4 unit under the theme 'How We Organise Ourselves' explores the central idea that 'Systems connect us.' Students investigate the form, function, and interconnectedness of various human-made and natural systems. The inquiry begins by identifying everyday systems within the school environment, such as the library or canteen, breaking them down into their parts, purposes, and connections. Learning then shifts to natural systems, where students conduct hands-on experiments like creating a mini water cycle in a bag and participating in a 'Web of Life' string activity to understand ecosystems. Finally, the unit connects human and natural systems by examining waste management. Students explore the sustainability benefits of recycling by making their own recycled paper and compare composting systems to landfills, ultimately understanding how human actions and systems impact the broader environment and community.

ci: Changes to the Earth impact environment and communities.
lo: The causes of geological change over time; How extreme environments cause natural disasters; The impact of natural events on communities

s.d: During the 'Earthquake Tower Experiment' (Session 1), students can attach a Micro:bit to their LEGO or wooden block structures. Using MakeCode, Year 4 students can program the Micro:bit's accelerometer to act as a seismograph, measuring the numerical intensity of the vibrations when they shake their 'tectonic plates'. They can record this data in their UOI books to mathematically compare which structural designs best withstood the simulated earthquake.

For the 'BTN-style news broadcast' about an imaginary bushfire, students can film their scripts using iPads and Green Screen Kits. Using iMovie, they can replace the green background with relevant images or video clips of the specific Australian environmental conditions they researched (e.g., dry grasslands, strong winds). This elevates their multimodal communication skills before uploading the final video to Seesaw.

Instead of a paper template for the 'Compare Two Earthquakes' lesson (Session 3), students can use Canva to collaboratively design a digital Venn diagram. Working in pairs on their laptops, they can insert text boxes, icons, and images to categorize the environmental and community effects of the 2018 Lombok and 2011 Japan earthquakes, practicing digital layout and data organization skills.

During the 'Visual Source Analysis and Mapping' activity for floods, or the 'Mapping Natural Disasters' math extension, students can use Google Earth to plot the locations of major historical disasters. They can use the digital measurement tool to calculate the distance of these events from their local community in Victoria, helping them visualize geographical patterns and the scale of extreme environments.

For their Summative Research Project, students can use Book Creator to design an interactive digital portfolio about their chosen natural disaster. They can embed photos of their handwritten research logs, record audio clips explaining the causes of geological change, and use the drawing tool to illustrate how the disaster impacts the environment, creating a rich, accessible presentation to share with their peers.

plannerText: This Year 4 unit under the theme 'How the World Works' investigates how changes to the Earth impact environments and communities. Students explore the causes of geological changes and extreme environments, focusing specifically on natural disasters like bushfires, earthquakes, and floods. Through hands-on experiments (like building earthquake-resistant towers), jigsaw research activities, and analyzing real-world events (such as the 2011 Japan earthquake or Australian Black Summer fires), learners examine both the scientific processes behind these events and the human response. The unit integrates literacy through BTN-style news broadcasts and explanation texts, and mathematics through mapping and measurement. It culminates in an independent research project where students investigate a specific historical natural disaster, demonstrating their understanding of its causes, environmental impacts, and the community's recovery efforts.

ci: Country, language and community are significant to the lives of First Nations people.
lo: The diversity of First Nations people; The importance of community, family and kinship; The importance of Country to First Nations people

s.d: In Session 6, where students create a visual dictionary of the Woi Wurrung language, they can use Book Creator to design an interactive multimedia dictionary. Students can type the English and Woi Wurrung words, use the pen tool to draw or import images representing the vocabulary, and use the audio recording feature to practice and embed their pronunciation of the Woi Wurrung words.

Microsoft Teams provides a robust platform for students to engage with the unit's content through its integrated video and collaboration tools. Students can utilize the native video recording features to present their Woi Wurrung visual dictionaries, share their research on First Nations individuals, and record themselves delivering their meaningful Acknowledgement of Country. This allows for authentic expression and practice, directly replacing the video functionality of 'flip'. Teams channels can facilitate rich discussions around Dreaming stories, Songlines, and the nuances between Welcome to Country and Acknowledgement of Country, fostering deeper understanding and peer learning. Furthermore, students can collaborate on mapping language groups, share digital resources, and present their totem designs, using the platform as a central hub for all unit activities and culminating reflections.

During Session 13, when students create a short acting presentation of the Bundjil and Waa Dreaming story, they can perform their play in front of a Green Screen. Using iPads and iMovie, they can edit their performance by replacing the background with appropriate Australian landscapes or their own digital artwork, and overlay traditional soundscapes to enhance their storytelling.

To physically represent the concept of Songlines explored in Sessions 14 and 15, students can program a Sphero BOLT using block coding to navigate across a large physical map of Australia. They can code the Sphero to change colours, pause, or play specific sounds when it reaches significant cultural landmarks or crosses different Indigenous language group boundaries.

In Sessions 17-18, instead of using a paper hand template to profile Adam Goodes, students can use Canva to design a digital infographic. They can utilise Canva's elements to create a hand graphic, add text boxes for their 5 researched facts, and embed safe images or icons that visually demonstrate his leadership and deep connection to Country, language, and community.

plannerText: This Year 4 Sharing the Planet unit explores the central idea that Country, language, and community are deeply significant to the lives of First Nations people. Students investigate the diversity of Indigenous groups globally and locally, focusing heavily on Australian Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander cultures. Key learning experiences include mapping language groups, creating Woi Wurrung visual dictionaries, and understanding the profound differences between a Welcome to Country and an Acknowledgement of Country. Students delve into traditional modes of communication and storytelling by exploring Dreaming stories, Songlines, and artistic expressions like rock art and dance. The unit also highlights the contributions of famous First Nations individuals, such as Adam Goodes and Indigenous ANZAC veterans, examining their leadership and connection to culture. The inquiry culminates in students writing their own meaningful Acknowledgement of Country and creating totem designs that reflect their understanding of community and legacy.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Human migration is a response to challenges and opportunities
lo: The reasons people migrate; Migration throughout history; Effects of migration on communities, cultures and individuals

s.d: During the Session 2/3 'BTN News Broadcasters' activity where students script and film a report on an imaginary bushfire, elevate the task using Green Screen Kits and iPads. Students can use iMovie to replace the green background with authentic visuals, such as Australian bushland or weather maps they have sourced. This provides a highly engaging, real-world media experience that develops their communication skills while remaining developmentally appropriate for Year 4.

For the 'Earthquake Tower Experiment' STEM task, replace manual shaking with Lego Spike Essential kits. Students can build and code a motorized 'shake table' to test their LEGO brick towers, programming different motor speeds to simulate varying intensities on the Richter scale. They can record their structural tests using iPads in slow-motion and upload the videos to Seesaw, adding a voiceover reflection on why their building succeeded or failed.

In the Session 3 'Compare Two Earthquakes' research task, students can use Canva to digitally construct their Venn Diagrams comparing the 2018 Lombok Earthquake and the 2011 Japan Earthquake. Canva's real-time collaboration allows pairs to work simultaneously on the same design. They can search for and insert specific icons or images to represent environmental and community effects, reducing the fine-motor fatigue of drawing and allowing for a highly visual, organized comparison.

To support the 'Mapping Natural Disasters' Mathematics integration, students use Google Earth to locate recent global disasters. They can utilize the digital measurement tool to calculate the exact distance in kilometers from their school in Victoria to the disaster epicenters. Students can take screenshots of their measurements and paste them into a collaborative Microsoft OneNote Class Notebook, using digital ink to annotate the maps and identify geographical patterns.

For the final Summative Research Project where students present their findings on a specific historical natural disaster, Book Creator offers an intuitive, multimodal platform. Year 4 students can easily synthesize their research by typing facts, using the pen tool to draw diagrams of geological causes (like tectonic plate boundaries), and embedding audio recordings explaining the community's response. This allows for rich, universally designed expression without overwhelming their cognitive load.

plannerText: This Year 4 IB PYP unit under the theme 'How the World Works' investigates how natural disasters and geological changes impact the Earth and human communities. The central idea focuses on the causes of extreme environmental events?such as bushfires, earthquakes, floods, and cyclones?and how communities prepare for, respond to, and recover from them. Throughout the 6-week planner, students engage in jigsaw research activities, hands-on STEM experiments like building earthquake-resistant towers, and interactive simulations. They explore the science behind these events, including tectonic plate movement and the fire triangle. The unit culminates in an independent research project where students investigate a specific historical natural disaster, analyzing its environmental and social impacts, and presenting their findings to demonstrate their understanding of geological change and community resilience.

ci: Leaders shape communities.
lo: Roles and responsibilities of effective leadership; The purpose of leadership in communities; Qualities of influential leaders

s.d: Connecting to Lesson 1 (Breaking down the central idea), students use Canva to design a digital poster of their personal 2025 leadership quote. Instead of just writing it on a strip of paper, they learn basic graphic design principles, selecting typography and imagery that reflect the tone of their leadership goal. These can be printed for the classroom display or compiled into a shared digital class book.

During Lesson 2 (Our class personalities / Compass points), students input adjectives that describe their identified personality type and leadership strengths into Word Clouds by ABCya. This provides Year 4 students with a concrete visual representation of abstract character traits. They can then export this image to their Seesaw portfolio and add an audio recording explaining how these traits will help them lead.

To explore the 'Boss vs. Leader' scenario, students animate characters to demonstrate the different communication styles. They focus on the vocabulary and tone used by effective leaders compared to dictatorial 'bosses'.

During the famous leader case studies in Lessons 7-9 (e.g., Adam Goodes, Albert Einstein), students use the podcasting equipment to record a short 'radio interview'. One student acts as the journalist asking about the leader's roles and community impact, while the other role-plays the leader. This highly engaging task develops the 'Communicator' ATL and oral language skills aligned with Australian curriculum standards.

For the final summative task in Lessons 10-15 (Leadership Applications), students use PowerPoint to design a professional pitch deck for their desired Year 4 role (e.g., STEM Leader, SRC). They utilize the 'Record' feature to narrate their slides, clearly articulating their learner profile attributes and how they plan to shape the Junior School community, providing a fantastic opportunity to practice formal digital presentation skills.

plannerText: This Year 4 'Who We Are' unit explores the central idea that 'Leaders shape communities.' Throughout the inquiry, students investigate the roles, responsibilities, and qualities of effective leadership. Early lessons focus on self-discovery, using compass point routines to help students identify their unique personality traits and how these translate into leadership styles. Students analyze the distinct differences between being a 'boss' and a 'leader' through role-play and discussion. The unit incorporates detailed case studies of influential figures like Martin Luther King Jr., Adam Goodes, and Albert Einstein to demonstrate how leaders impact their communities. Ultimately, the unit culminates in a practical application process where students reflect on their own strengths, set goals, and formally apply for specific Year 4 leadership roles (such as SRC, STEM, or Arts Leader) within the Junior School community.

ci: People can create or manipulate messages to target specific audiences.
lo: The devices used to target audiences; Critical evaluation of messages; How we know we have been influenced

s.d: In Week 4, Lesson 6, students write an alternative ending to 'Click Clack Moo: Cows That Type'. Using Adobe Express character animations, students can record their persuasive dialogue as Farmer Brown or the cows. The tool automatically syncs their voice to an animated character, allowing Year 5 students to focus on their persuasive tone, expression, and vocabulary without the cognitive load of complex video editing.

For the Week 5, Lesson 5 activity based on Margaret Wild's 'Fox', students must break the text into a 6-frame visualisation. Students can use Canva's comic strip templates to digitally construct these frames. They can search for specific graphics, adjust colours to match the mood of the rich vocabulary, and add text boxes to demonstrate their reading comprehension and visual literacy.

Students can leverage Canva to design and create their own persuasive media, such as 'Australian advertising campaigns,' 'protest posters,' or short animated videos, directly applying their understanding of persuasive techniques like humour, emotional appeal, and visual storytelling. This allows them to actively 'create and manipulate messages to target specific audiences,' a core unit goal. They can also use Canva to visually deconstruct mentor texts or existing media, highlighting persuasive devices. For the 'Game of Persuasion,' students can design the visual 'messages' to demonstrate their mastery of audience targeting and persuasive communication, fostering both critical analysis and creative production of media.

In Week 8, Lessons 4 and 5, students learn to plan a persuasive argument map (e.g., 'Would you rather be a mouse or a tiger?'). Using OneNote, students can utilize digital inking to draw their argument maps, connecting their thesis to supporting arguments and conclusions. The infinite canvas allows for expansive brainstorming, and teachers can easily distribute templates and provide real-time feedback.

When analyzing the 'Advertising Campaigns with an Australian Context' (like 'Dumb Ways to Die' or 'Slip, Slop, Slap'), teachers can set up a Padlet with columns for each campaign. Students can collaboratively post their critical evaluations of the persuasive devices used (e.g., humour, fear, music) under each column, creating a shared digital wall that makes their thinking visible and encourages peer-to-peer discussion.

plannerText: This Year 5 unit under the theme 'How We Express Ourselves' investigates how people create and manipulate messages to target specific audiences. The inquiry focuses on the devices used in media, critical evaluation of messages, and understanding how we are influenced. Students explore these concepts through rich mentor texts like 'Click, Clack, Moo' and 'Fox', analyzing how authors use narrative structure, vocabulary, and literary devices to persuade. They also deconstruct Australian advertising campaigns, political speeches, and protest songs to identify persuasive techniques such as humour, emotional appeal, and visual storytelling. The unit culminates in students applying these skills to their own writing, developing structured persuasive texts and engaging in a 'Game of Persuasion' to practically demonstrate their understanding of audience targeting and persuasive communication.

ci: Entrepreneurs have a responsibility to develop, market and sell sustainable products and services.
lo: The power of giving back; How our environment is impacted by the products we create; Sustainable business practices

s.d: Connecting to the planner's requirement that 'Students record thoughts/actions of each stage of the design process in their learning journal', students can use a OneNote Class Notebook as their digital design portfolio. They can paste their ChatGPT market research transcripts, use digital inking to draw their 'Crazy 8' ideation sketches, and insert audio recordings of the feedback they receive during the Week 5 testing phase.

During Week 3, the planner asks students to 'Analyse the data from the JS. Create a data visual.' Students can use Canva to design professional infographics that represent the survey data collected from the Junior School students. This helps them visually synthesize market trends and user preferences to define their sustainable product idea.

For the Week 4 & 5 'prototype' phase where students must 'build real, tactile representations', students can translate their 2D product designs into 3D CAD models using Tinkercad. They can then use the school's 3D Printers to manufacture physical, sustainable prototypes to present to their users for the Week 5 feedback loop.

Instead of iMovie, students use the podcasting tool to create a professional radio commercial for their sustainable product. They can layer their persuasive pitches with royalty-free music and sound effects to target their market stall audience.

Integrating the Financial Maths (budgeting, expenses, and income) and English components, students can create a formal business pitch deck in PowerPoint. They can embed their financial tables, outline their corporate social responsibility goals, and present their product's sustainability features to their peers and teachers prior to the final market day.

plannerText: This Year 5 unit under the theme 'How We Organise Ourselves' explores the central idea that entrepreneurs have a responsibility to develop, market, and sell sustainable products and services. The inquiry follows a comprehensive design thinking process over seven weeks. Students begin by empathising with their target market, using ChatGPT to generate survey questions for Junior School students to understand their interests. They then define product opportunities, ideate using 'Crazy 8' sketches, and build tactile prototypes. Financial mathematics is integrated as students develop business budgets, calculating expenses and income. English skills are developed through formal email writing and persuasive advertising. The unit culminates in a real-world Market Stall in Week 7, where students manufacture their products in bulk, set up stalls, and advertise their sustainable goods to the school community, demonstrating their understanding of corporate social responsibility and sustainable consumer choices.

ci: Design processes enable people to respond creatively to challenges in the natural world.
lo: The nature and impact of natural hazards (Causation); How design processes are used to generate and test solutions (Function); The ethical and practical considerations in human responses to natural challenges (Responsibility)

s.d: During the Week 4 mapping tasks, students use Google Earth to investigate the geographical features of the Newcastle and Meckering earthquake sites or map local bushfire-prone zones. They can use the measurement tools to calculate distances between vulnerable infrastructure and hazard zones, applying their grid reference and mapping skills from the Maths integration.

For the Week 4 'Sorting Out' phase, students use Canva's collaborative whiteboard features to design a visual systems thinking model (inputs, processes, outputs, feedback) analyzing a specific natural hazard. They can also use it to create a digital Venn diagram comparing modern hazard management with traditional Indigenous cool burning practices.

During the Week 5 earthquake-resistant structure design challenge, students use Tinkercad to design 3D models of their building's structural supports, such as cross-bracing or base isolators. They can then 3D print these specific components to test their load capacity and stability when subjected to simulated seismic movement.

In the Week 6 Prototyping phase, students integrate Micro:bits into their physical models to create functional early warning systems. Year 5 students can code the Micro:bit's temperature or accelerometer sensors to trigger an LED alert or sound an alarm when simulating a bushfire's heat or an earthquake's tremors, adding computational thinking to their design.

To prepare for the Week 7 UNICEF pitch presentations, students use Green Screen Kits and iMovie to record their persuasive pitches. They can overlay their video in front of digital backgrounds showing the natural hazard they are addressing (e.g., Lismore floods) and insert footage of their prototype testing, creating a compelling advocacy video for the School Showcase.

plannerText: This Year 5 unit under the theme 'How the World Works' investigates how design processes enable people to creatively respond to natural hazards. Students explore the causes and impacts of extreme weather and geological events like bushfires, floods, droughts, and earthquakes. Through a partnership with UNICEF, students engage deeply in the design thinking cycle. They research traditional and modern land management (including Indigenous cool burning), analyze data and mapping, and use systems thinking to understand vulnerabilities. The unit culminates in a hands-on prototyping phase where students design, build, and test tangible solutions?such as earthquake-resistant structures or early warning systems?and deliver persuasive pitches to advocate for their designs at a community showcase.

ci: Finding peaceful solutions to conflict can make a difference in the world.
lo: Types of conflict; Causes and impacts of conflict; Conflict resolution

s.d: During the Colour-Symbol-Image (CSI) thinking routine about 'Conflict' and 'Power', students use Canva to digitally construct their posters. They can search for specific symbolic graphics, apply colour palettes that evoke the emotions of conflict or peace, and use text boxes to justify how their chosen visual elements represent their conceptual understanding.

Students can utilize Microsoft Teams' native video capabilities within a dedicated channel for the unit. They can record and share video reflections on mentor texts like 'Old Turtle' and 'The Rabbits', discussing their understanding of conflict and peaceful solutions. For the Physical Education integration, students can record short video clips demonstrating 'I statements' or role-playing conflict resolution scenarios, then share these for peer feedback and self-reflection. They can also use video to present their generated inquiry questions for the research project, fostering a dynamic and interactive learning environment that directly replaces the video discussion functionality of 'flip' while supporting the unit's pedagogical goals of reflection, perspective-taking, and communication.

For the 'Old Turtle' reading comprehension activity, teachers distribute a digital 'Double Entry Journal' template via OneNote Class Notebook. Students extract quotes or imagery from the text on the left side of the table, and use the typing or audio recording tools on the right side to document their personal connections regarding interconnectedness and ecology.

Directly supporting the Science Fair Project question generation lesson, students access a shared Padlet board via a QR code. After selecting their high-interest topics, they post their ideas and use the comment feature to collaboratively generate, sort, and categorize inquiry questions under key concepts (like causation or perspective), mirroring the avocado seed modeling activity.

To consolidate their PSPE learning about navigating disagreements during team games, students use Stop Motion Studio to animate a common sports conflict using classroom materials or Lego. They script and record voiceovers for their characters, explicitly demonstrating the use of 'I statements' and respectful language to successfully de-escalate and resolve the simulated playground dispute.

plannerText: This Year 5 PYP unit, under the theme 'Sharing the Planet,' investigates how finding peaceful solutions to conflict can make a difference in the world. Students explore the types, causes, and impacts of conflict, alongside strategies for conflict resolution. The inquiry is driven by rich mentor texts, including picture books like 'Old Turtle' and 'The Rabbits', and the analysis of protest songs such as 'From Little Things Big Things Grow' and 'Imagine'. Learning experiences involve utilizing thinking routines like Colour-Symbol-Image, completing double-entry journals, and generating inquiry questions for a research project. Furthermore, the unit authentically integrates with Physical Education, where students experience and reflect on real-time conflict resolution, teamwork, and the use of 'I statements' during gameplay, ultimately developing their understanding of perspective, connection, and responsibility.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Colonisation has shaped society.
lo: The impact of colonisation; Colonisation stories; The effects of colonisation ? past, present and future

s.d: Connecting to the Social Studies focus on 'Cartography ? mapping of the East coast of Australia' and Lesson 5's 'Mapping of Massacres', students use Google Earth to spatially visualize Captain Cook's voyage and the subsequent frontier conflicts. By dropping pins and measuring distances between historical settlement sites and the locations from the Colonial Frontier Massacres map, Year 5 students develop spatial reasoning and a deeper, contextualized empathy for the geographical scale of colonisation's impact on First Nations peoples.

Linked to the English lessons using Shaun Tan's 'The Rabbits' and the 'Creative writing task about the colonisation of Mars', students use Book Creator to author and illustrate their own allegorical multimodal texts. They can combine digital drawings, imported images, and recorded voiceovers to tell the story of Mars' colonisation, demonstrating their understanding of how colonisers impact new environments and indigenous ecosystems, mirroring the themes explored in their mentor text.

During Lesson 3 from the 'Unsettled' sequence, where students 'analyse an artwork for visual storytelling and examine what it tells us about the impact of terra nullius', they can use Canva to create an analytical digital poster. Students upload an image of the artwork and use Canva's annotation tools, shapes, and text boxes to highlight and decode specific symbols, demonstrating their visual literacy and understanding of historical perspectives.

To elevate the 'Comparing and Contrasting colonization stories from around the world' lesson, students replace a static paper Venn diagram with a collaborative Padlet using the 'Canvas' format. Students can post text, historical images, and links about different global colonies, then draw digital connecting lines between posts to visually map out similarities (e.g., perceived advantages) and differences, fostering collaborative synthesis and visible thinking.

Connecting to Lesson 2 from 'Unsettled', where students 'explore different means of communication [and] learn about signal fires', students program Micro:bits to send and receive radio signals across the classroom. By coding specific LED patterns to represent different messages or warnings, students engage in computational thinking while practically exploring how distant communication systems operate, bridging traditional First Nations communication methods with modern digital systems.

plannerText: This Year 5 unit, under the transdisciplinary theme 'Where we are in place and time', investigates the central idea that colonisation has shaped society. Students explore the historical and ongoing impacts of colonisation, focusing heavily on the Australian context and First Nations perspectives through resources like the Australian Museum's 'Unsettled' exhibition. Key learning experiences include analyzing Captain Cook's voyage, mapping colonial frontier massacres, and examining the devastating concept of terra nullius. Students also engage with powerful literature like Shaun Tan's 'The Rabbits' and write creative pieces on the hypothetical colonisation of Mars. Cross-curricular connections involve mapping the East Coast in Social Studies, exploring the exponential growth of invasive species in Maths, and analyzing visual storytelling to understand diverse historical perspectives.

ci: People's choices are influenced by their beliefs and values and can impact others.
lo: How values and beliefs impact choices; How people's choices and actions impact others; Responsible decision-making

s.d: Students will leverage Microsoft Teams' native video recording and sharing features to capture and articulate their reflections across all unit components. In Physical Education, students can record short video reflections detailing their in-game choices and perspective-taking, submitting these as part of their digital portfolio. For Visual Arts, students can create video journals explaining how their self-perception and external influences shaped their creative decisions. Music lessons can incorporate video reflections where students discuss their individual practice habits and their impact on ensemble dynamics. Furthermore, classroom inquiries into self-efficacy and positive self-talk can be supported by students recording themselves practicing positive affirmations or discussing how their internal dialogue and core values drive their actions and influence their broader community. These video reflections can be shared within dedicated channels for peer feedback and discussion, fostering a deeper understanding of beliefs, values, and their subsequent impact on self and others, perfectly aligning with the unit's focus on personal growth, relationships, and responsible decision-making.

To demonstrate their understanding of positive self-talk and self-efficacy, students use the 'Animate from Audio' feature in Adobe Express. They record their own voice acting out a scenario where a character shifts from negative to positive self-talk when facing a learning challenge, illustrating how beliefs influence actions.

During their Visual Arts unit, students maintain a digital 'Art Process Journal' using Book Creator. They use digital cameras or iPads to document their artwork from start to finish, adding text or audio recordings to explicitly reflect on how their personal thoughts, beliefs, and peer influences shaped their creative choices.

To support the Music reflection on IMP instrumental groups, the teacher creates a Padlet board. Students post short written reflections detailing how their personal actions, such as home practice and rehearsal behavior, have impacted their small group's relationships and overall performance, fostering a discussion on collective responsibility.

Using the Collaboration Space in Microsoft OneNote, students engage in a digital 'Chalk Talk' to unpack the central idea. The teacher sets up pages with different choice-based scenarios; students collaboratively type their perspectives on what underlying values drive those choices and how they might impact others.

plannerText: This Year 5 'Who We Are' unit investigates how people's choices are influenced by their beliefs and values, and how these decisions subsequently impact others. The inquiry focuses on physical, emotional, and spiritual well-being, alongside relationships, belonging, and personal growth. Key learning experiences are integrated across specialist subjects. In Physical Education, students engage in small-sided games to explore responsible decision-making and perspective-taking, culminating in a portfolio reflection on their in-game choices. In Visual Arts, students examine how their self-perception and external influences shape their creative decisions. Music lessons prompt students to reflect on how their individual practice habits and behaviors affect their instrumental ensemble's dynamics. Additionally, classroom inquiries delve into the concepts of self-efficacy and positive self-talk, empowering students to understand how their internal dialogue and core values drive their actions and influence their broader community.

ci: An inquiry into the ways in which we discover and express ideas, feelings, nature, culture, beliefs and values.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded

s.d: Use Microsoft OneNote Class Notebook as the primary digital 'PYPEX Journal'. For Lesson 1, distribute a custom template where students can embed their VIA Character Strengths survey results. They can use the audio recording tool in OneNote to verbally reflect on how their top strengths align with their personal passions, reducing cognitive load for students who struggle with extensive typing while capturing authentic Year 6 student voice.

During Lessons 3 and 4, utilize Padlet to facilitate the collaborative creation of group Essential Agreements. Students can post their proposed group norms and expectations on a shared board, using the voting feature to democratically select the most important agreements. This provides a visual, interactive space for Year 6 students to negotiate their shared issue and establish a respectful working environment for their PYP Exhibition group.

In Lessons 11 and 12, students use Canva to design their Unit of Inquiry wall displays. Leveraging Canva's collaborative features, group members can simultaneously design their posters, integrating their co-constructed Central Idea, Lines of Inquiry, and Learner Profile attributes. The extensive library of templates and graphics allows Year 6 students to focus on visual hierarchy and effective communication of their PYPEX framework without getting bogged down in complex graphic design mechanics.

For the Lesson 13 'Making' phase, have students use Explain Everything to deconstruct and analyze existing media campaigns. Students can import images or video clips of real-world campaigns into the whiteboard space, using the digital pen and voiceover tools to annotate persuasive techniques, target audience cues, and media elements. This multimodal approach helps Year 6 students critically evaluate media literacy concepts before planning their own campaigns.

During Lessons 14 to 18, students can utilize the Adobe Express suite to produce diverse digital assets for their media campaign. Depending on their target audience, students can use the 'Animate from Audio' feature to create engaging character animations explaining their issue, or use the webpage designer to build a central hub for their advocacy. This provides Year 6 students with professional-grade, yet intuitive, creation tools to express their PYPEX findings powerfully.

plannerText: This Year 6 'How We Express Ourselves' unit serves as the framework for the PYP Exhibition (PYPEX). The central idea and lines of inquiry are entirely student-led, focusing on how individuals discover and express ideas, feelings, and beliefs. The planner outlines a highly structured, step-by-step inquiry process where students first identify their personal passions and transition these into actionable global or local problems. Throughout the tuning-in and finding-out phases, students collaboratively form groups, draft essential agreements, unpack key vocabulary, and co-construct their own central ideas and lines of inquiry. In the making and sorting-out phases, students engage in deep research and synthesize their findings by designing and producing a comprehensive media campaign. This campaign acts as their primary vehicle for expression and advocacy, culminating in a public exhibition where they communicate their learning and proposed solutions to the broader school community.

ci: Student-led exhibition (PYPEX) ? Students develop and present their own units of inquiry.
lo: Student-led transdisciplinary themes; Approaches to learning; Research and presentation skills

s.d: During Lessons 1 and 2, where students identify passions and brainstorm surrounding positive and negative elements, use Padlet in a 'Canvas' or 'Map' layout. Year 6 students can visually map out their interests, link them to real-world problems, and cluster similar ideas collaboratively. This visual brainstorming will help them organically form their exhibition groups based on shared global issues.

For Lesson 12, where students finalize their unit of inquiry wall display, leverage Canva's collaborative whiteboard and presentation features. Groups can collaboratively design their interactive UOI displays, embedding custom graphics, text, and visual representations of their central idea and lines of inquiry, ensuring their final product is visually engaging for the PYPEX audience.

In Lesson 13, as students prepare for mentor meetings and complete 360-degree assessments, utilize Microsoft Teams to set up private channels for each exhibition group and their mentor. Students can use a shared OneNote Class Notebook within the channel to document their assessments, draft professional emails, and maintain an ongoing digital journal of their mentor meeting minutes.

During Lessons 13 and 14 (Making Conclusions), where students analyze and create a media campaign consisting of 30+ items, employ the Adobe Express suite. Year 6 students can design professional-grade persuasive media, including social media graphics, short promotional videos, and scrolling web pages, applying their understanding of target audiences to drive action for their chosen issue.

As part of the Lesson 14 media campaign creation, groups can use the school's Podcast Equipment and Garageband to script, record, and edit a short podcast episode or radio PSA. This provides a dynamic audio medium for students to articulate their research findings, interview experts, and advocate for their cause, developing both their oral communication and technical editing skills.

plannerText: The Year 6 PYP Exhibition (PYPEX) unit is a student-led inquiry where learners develop and present their own comprehensive units of inquiry. Serving as the culminating PYP experience, students begin by identifying personal passions and connecting them to real-world problems to form collaborative groups. Throughout the process, they co-construct their central ideas, lines of inquiry, and key concepts. The learning experiences guide students through unpacking vocabulary, formulating research questions, and designing a unit of inquiry wall display. A significant focus is placed on action and media representation; students analyze existing media campaigns and subsequently design their own extensive campaign comprising over 30 media items to persuade and inform their audience. The unit emphasizes self-management, research, and communication skills, supported by regular 360-degree assessments, reflective journaling, and ongoing collaboration with adult mentors to prepare for their final public exhibition.

ci: Energy may be converted, transformed, and used to support human progress.
lo: Different forms of energy and how they are used; The impact of different energy sources on the planet; Sustainable energy practices

s.d: During Activity 2 (Investigating Energy Transformations), students use Lego Spike Prime kits to build and code their own physical chain-reaction machines. They must document the 'Initial Energy' (e.g., a coded motor providing mechanical energy) and the 'Final Energy' (e.g., kinetic movement, light, or sound), demonstrating tangible energy transformations.

To elevate the 'Explanation Game' thinking routine in Activity 5 (William's Dynamo), students use Explain Everything on iPads. They import a diagram of the dynamo, then use the digital whiteboard tools to draw arrows and record their voice explaining how electromagnetic induction converts mechanical energy into electrical energy.

As a tuning-in activity before constructing their Solar Buddy lights in Activity 9/10, students use Makey Makey kits connected to laptops to explore open and closed circuits. They test various classroom materials for conductivity, deepening their understanding of how electrical current flows through a system before building their solar lamps.

For the Activity 4 'weird and wacky blackout scenarios', students write and record a short radio drama or emergency news bulletin using the Rodecaster and mics. They must articulate the catastrophic consequences of losing electrical energy, focusing on how modern human progress relies heavily on this specific energy form.

In Activity 8 (State Energy Graphs), students input the state energy data into Microsoft Excel to generate their own comparative bar charts. They then export these into a collaborative Microsoft PowerPoint deck, using SmartArt tools to build a digital Venn diagram comparing the renewable vs. non-renewable energy sources of two Australian states.

plannerText: This Year 6 How the World Works unit focuses on the central idea that energy may be converted, transformed, and used to support human progress. Students investigate diverse forms of energy, including renewable and non-renewable sources, and analyze their advantages and disadvantages. Through hands-on learning experiences like building Solar Buddy lights, analyzing Rube Goldberg machines, and studying William Kamkwamba's windmill, students explore how energy transfers and transforms within systems. The unit also delves into the environmental impact of different energy sources, comparing state-by-state electricity generation and the effects of fossil fuels. Ultimately, students develop an understanding of sustainable energy practices and their individual and collective responsibilities in conserving energy and reducing waste for a more sustainable future.

ci: Communities interact with the environment in different ways.
lo: How community values shape the management of the natural environment; The need for sustainable practices; Taking action for environmental stewardship

s.d: During the Lesson 1 Provocation, instead of just watching a standard video clip, students use ClassVR headsets to immerse themselves in 360-degree environments such as coral reefs, dense forests, or heavily polluted urban areas. This immersive experience lowers the cognitive barrier to understanding complex ecosystems and deeply engages their curiosity before they record their observations and questions in their KWL charts.

For the 'Find out' Environmental Case Studies activity, students use a collaborative Padlet board set up with columns for each case study (e.g., Aboriginal land management, urban pollution, Amazon conservation). As groups rotate, they post their analysis (Who, What environment, Impact, Stewardship) using text, images, and voice notes. This creates a dynamic, accessible whole-class repository of research that students can easily reference.

In the 'Sort out' phase focusing on Indigenous stewardship, students use Canva to design their comparison chart of Traditional Gunaikurnai practices versus Modern land management. Year 6 students can leverage Canva's extensive library of icons, shapes, and templates to visually organize their deep dive research, reducing the cognitive load of formatting while enhancing the visual communication of their comparative analysis.

During the 'Go further' action phase, groups that choose to create an awareness video to promote environmental stewardship can utilize Green Screen Kits and iPads with iMovie. Students can superimpose themselves over footage of the environments they are advocating for (like a regenerating forest or a polluted waterway), allowing them to deliver a compelling, professional-style news report or documentary call-to-action.

To facilitate the 'Reflect and Act' Peer Gallery Walk, students upload their final action projects, camp reflections, and KWL charts to Seesaw. They generate a Seesaw QR code for their digital portfolio and place it on their desks. Peers use iPads to scan the codes, review the multimedia projects at their own pace, and leave constructive, peer-to-peer feedback using Seesaw's audio or text commenting tools.

plannerText: This Year 6 IB PYP unit, under the theme 'Sharing the Planet,' explores the central idea that communities interact with the environment in different ways. The inquiry focuses on how community values shape natural environment management, the critical need for conservation, and how environmental stewardship drives change. Students begin with a provocation exploring diverse global environments before engaging in experiential learning at Camp Mallana, where they reflect on water ecosystems, biodiversity, and human impacts. Back in the classroom, they analyze environmental case studies, including urban pollution and Amazon conservation. A significant focus is placed on Indigenous stewardship, specifically comparing traditional Gunaikurnai land practices with modern management approaches. The unit culminates in an action-oriented project where students design and present initiatives?such as awareness videos or conservation posters?to promote environmental stewardship, sharing their learning through a peer gallery walk.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Significant events shape a place
lo: What is a significant event; Why these events occurred; Impact of significant events

s.d: Instead of a physical classroom map for the provocation lesson, use a Padlet set to the 'Map' layout. Year 6 students can drop digital pins on their family's countries of origin, attaching photos, short text descriptions, or audio recordings of family members sharing their migration stories. This creates a rich, interactive, and collaborative visual representation of the class's diverse backgrounds that can be easily referenced and updated throughout the unit.

During the lesson where students create a number line of significant migration dates to Australia, leverage Canva's collaborative whiteboard or timeline templates. Year 6 students can work in small groups to design visually engaging timelines, embedding primary source images, icons, and short summaries of push/pull factors for each era. This integrates Mathematics (scale and chronological ordering) with HASS and digital design skills.

Utilize Microsoft OneNote Class Notebook for the 'Migrant vs. Refugee' Venn diagram and the 'diverse/contributions' word investigation. Distribute a digital Venn diagram template to student sections where they can use digital inking or text boxes to categorize push and pull factors. The collaborative space can be used to co-construct the definitions of 'diverse' and 'contributions', allowing the teacher to monitor understanding in real-time and provide immediate feedback.

For the 5-6 lesson Personal Inquiry project, have students synthesize their research on a self-selected significant event (e.g., Cyclone Tracy, 2004 Tsunami) using Book Creator. Year 6 students can combine text, imported primary source images, embedded maps, and voice recordings to explain the event's causation and impact. This multimodal approach supports diverse learners and provides a highly engaging alternative to a standard written report.

For the Personal Inquiry project, students use the podcasting function to record a 'Live News' or 'Oral History' report on a significant event like Cyclone Tracy, focusing on interviewing 'witnesses' to capture the community's resilience.

plannerText: This Year 6 unit, under the theme "Where we are in place and time," explores the central idea that "Significant events shape a place." Students investigate the concepts of form, causation, and change through the lens of migration and historical milestones. The inquiry begins with a provocation where students map their own families' migration stories, leading into an exploration of push and pull factors. Students analyze the differences between migrants and refugees, investigate primary sources detailing waves of migration to Australia, and collaboratively define what makes an event historically significant. The unit culminates in a 5-6 lesson personal inquiry project where students exercise agency by researching a self-selected significant event (such as Cyclone Tracy or the 2004 Tsunami). They synthesize their findings into an informative presentation, demonstrating their understanding of how specific events have caused lasting impacts and shaped different places globally.

ci: Personal changes and influences contribute to our evolving sense of self.
lo: Physical, social, emotional and intellectual changes that occur throughout life; Influences that shape our identity; Developing a sense of self

s.d: To elevate the 'Superpower Showcase' activity where students design a superhero embodying an Approach to Learning (ATL), Year 6 students can use the 'Animate from Audio' feature in Adobe Express. They can record their own voices explaining their character's specific learning superpower (e.g., self-management or communication) and how it helps them overcome challenges. The resulting animated video can then be published directly to Seesaw for peer feedback.

Building on the planned 'All About Me' Canva activity, students can utilize Canva's video presentation and interactive tools to create a dynamic 'Influences Board'. They can embed short video reflections, use the recording studio feature to narrate how specific family or cultural influences have shaped their evolving sense of self, and hyperlink to safe media that represents their interests, sharing the final interactive link in Microsoft Teams.

Following the Cyber Safety workshops with Susan McClean, students can work in small groups using the podcasting equipment and Garageband to script and record a 'Cyber Safe Kids' mini-podcast. They can discuss the social and emotional impacts of online interactions, share tips on managing app age restrictions, and reflect on how their digital perceptions are influenced, demonstrating their understanding of responsible technology use.

To track their academic, social, and personal goals alongside their VIA Character Strengths, students can use a dedicated section in their Microsoft OneNote Class Notebook. They can create a multimedia goal-setting page where they insert their strength survey results, use the audio recording tool to verbally reflect on their 'You, At Your Best' narrative, and use digital inking to check off milestones as they grow throughout the term.

Microsoft Teams offers robust native video tools within its channels, allowing students to record and share video reflections on their personal changes, character strengths, and goal setting. This directly replaces Flip's video discussion functionality, enabling asynchronous peer-to-peer video responses and collaborative discussions on topics like emotional regulation, cyber safety, and evolving self-perception. Students can also present their 'superhero' personas via video and use Teams as a central hub for digital presentations and reflective journaling, fostering a dynamic and interactive environment for documenting their self-discovery journey.

plannerText: This Year 6 'Who We Are' unit focuses on the central idea that personal changes and influences contribute to an evolving sense of self. Throughout the 6-week inquiry, students explore the physical, social, emotional, and intellectual changes occurring during puberty and adolescence. Key learning experiences include identifying personal character strengths using the VIA classification, setting academic and personal goals, and creating 'superhero' personas to understand Approaches to Learning (ATLs). The unit incorporates specialized workshops on emotional regulation, puberty and reproductive health, and cyber safety. Students reflect on how their environments, relationships, and digital interactions shape their perceptions and behaviors, ultimately documenting their growth and self-discovery through collaborative discussions, digital presentations, and reflective journaling.

ci: Emotions influence our wellbeing.
lo: How our feelings and emotions affect us; How feelings and emotions are interpreted; How our emotions and behaviour affect relationships

s.d: Connecting to the L1 'Colour Monsters' activity, students can use the iPad camera to photograph their physical paint and wobbly-eye monster artworks. Using the Seesaw microphone tool, which is highly accessible for Prep students with limited writing skills, they can verbally record which emotion their monster represents and describe a time they felt that way, capturing their understanding of how feelings affect us.

To support the L2 focus on how emotions are interpreted and the creation of the 'emotions wall', students can draw an emotion face or select an emoji. Using Chatterpix Kids, they draw a simple line across the mouth and record a 10-second audio clip saying 'I look like this when I feel...'. This brings the static wall to life and allows pre-readers to practice facial recognition and verbal expression.

During the Music transdisciplinary connection where students listen to music and draw how it makes them feel, students can use Brushes Redux on iPads. The app's playback feature records their finger-painting strokes in real-time. Students can watch their drawing process replay, allowing teachers to discuss how their fast, slow, harsh, or gentle strokes matched the tempo of the music and their internal emotional state.

For the summative assessment, students can work in pairs using iPads to photograph each other acting out a specific emotion. They can import their photo into PicCollage alongside the teacher-provided reference photo of that emotion. Using fine motor skills, they can resize the images, add matching emoji stickers, and export the visual collage to Seesaw to answer the final reflection questions verbally.

To enhance the L3 mindfulness activities connected to 'The Lemonade Hurricane' book, teachers can use ClassVR Headsets to guide students through a 360-degree calming environment, such as a quiet beach or a gentle forest. This provides a concrete, immersive sensory experience of 'calmness', helping young Prep students tangibly understand emotional regulation and how finding a peaceful state can positively impact their behaviour.

plannerText: This year-long Prep unit under the theme 'How We Express Ourselves' focuses on the central idea that emotions influence our wellbeing. Students explore how feelings affect them, how emotions are interpreted by others, and how their behaviour impacts relationships. Key learning experiences include creating 'Colour Monsters' to identify pleasant and unpleasant feelings, using emojis to build an emotions wall, and reading picture books like 'The Lemonade Hurricane' to practice mindfulness. Transdisciplinary connections involve expressing emotions through Yayoi Kusama-inspired art and responding to music. The summative assessment requires students to photograph themselves mimicking specific emotions and reflect on why people might feel that way, fostering empathy, self-regulation, and interpersonal skills.

ci: The products people use go through a process of change.
lo: Where food comes from; How food can be changed for different purposes; An appreciation of food

s.d: Enhance the planned 'Making connections' activity where students link raw ingredients to final products. Provide a pre-made Seesaw template with draggable images of raw sources (cows, wheat, bees) and processed foods (cheese, bread, honey). Prep students use the drag-and-drop tool to match them, then use the microphone feature to verbally explain their reasoning (e.g., 'The bee makes the honey'), reducing writing load while capturing their understanding of the change process.

During the 'Finding out' phase where students create flowcharts for 'Making Honey' or 'From Peanut to Peanut Butter', have students draw the stages of the process on paper. They can photograph their favourite stage (e.g., a peanut being crushed), import it into Chatterpix Kids, draw a mouth, and record a short audio clip speaking from the perspective of the food explaining how it is changing. This makes procedural sequencing highly engaging and accessible for early years.

To document the 'Tuning In' seed planting activity, have students use iPads to take a weekly photo of their growing seed in the prep garden. At the end of the growth cycle, students compile their photos into a simple PicCollage grid. They can use the drawing tool to trace the stem and leaves, visually documenting the 'process of change' from seed to plant before it becomes a consumable product.

Prior to the 'Sorting out' Chesterfield Farm visit, use ClassVR Headsets to provide a guided, immersive 360-degree experience of a working dairy or crop farm. This front-loads vocabulary and context for Prep students, allowing them to safely observe large farm machinery and animals up close. It prepares them to ask better questions about 'where food comes from' during the actual excursion.

Connect to the 'Taking Action' focus on food waste and the classroom composting project. Create a large floor grid featuring pictures of different waste items (apple cores, plastic wrappers, sandwiches) and destination bins (compost, recycling, landfill). Prep students practice basic coding, directional language, and spatial reasoning by programming the Beebot to push the 'food waste' items to the correct 'compost' destination, reinforcing their learning about sustainable disposal.

plannerText: This Prep unit, under the theme 'How We Organise Ourselves,' explores the central idea that products people use go through a process of change. The inquiry focuses specifically on food: where it comes from, how it is processed for different purposes, and developing an appreciation for it. Key learning experiences include planting seeds to observe growth, matching raw ingredients to final products, and creating flowcharts for processes like making peanut butter or honey. Students also visit a local farm to see food production firsthand. The unit concludes with a focus on sustainability, where students take action by conducting lunchbox audits, learning about composting, and understanding how to manage food waste responsibly.

ci: The properties of materials influence how objects move and their use.
lo: Properties of materials; How objects move; How we use materials

s.d: During the 'Materials Gallery' learning experience, students can use iPads to take photos of the different material samples (sandpaper, foil, cling wrap). Using Seesaw, they can upload their photo and use the microphone tool to orally describe the observable properties (e.g., 'The foil is shiny and smooth'), removing the cognitive load of writing while capturing their understanding.

For the 'Toy sort' activity where students categorize toys by how they move, they can use PicCollage. Students take photos of the toys, import them into a simple two-column template (e.g., 'Slide' vs. 'Roll'), and use their fingers to drag and drop the images into the correct categories, developing fine motor skills and digital sorting capabilities.

Connecting to the 'Everyday photos' walk (observing features like grip strips on stairs), the class can collaboratively build a multimodal text in Book Creator. Students capture photos of purposeful materials around the school. The teacher compiles these onto pages where students can tap to add an audio recording explaining why that material was chosen for that specific job.

Seesaw's native video and audio recording features are perfect for capturing the hands-on learning and observations in this unit. Prep students can record themselves describing the properties of materials during the 'Mystery Bag' and 'Materials Gallery' activities, articulating what they see, feel, and predict. They can document their 'Slide and Roll' tests by recording their observations of how different materials influence movement. For the 'floater' design challenge, students can use video to explain their design choices, show their creation, and demonstrate its effectiveness. This allows for authentic student voice, immediate documentation of learning, and easy sharing of their discoveries about materials and their properties, directly replacing the interactive video response functionality of Flip.

To support the 'Taking action' exploration of materials used in cars, students can use Chatterpix Kids. They take a photo of a toy car part (e.g., a rubber tyre or glass window), draw a digital mouth on it, and record a 30-second voiceover pretending to be that material, stating its properties and purpose (e.g., 'I am rubber, I am grippy!').

plannerText: This Year Prep unit under the theme 'How the World Works' explores the central idea that the properties of materials influence how objects move and their use. Over 8 weeks, students identify various materials (wood, plastic, metal, etc.) and their observable properties (waterproof, flexible, smooth). Students engage in hands-on learning, including a 'Mystery Bag' sensory activity, a 'Materials Gallery,' and 'Slide and Roll' tests using ramps. They observe everyday materials in their environment, sort toys based on movement, and undertake a design challenge to build a 'floater' for water or air using items like yoghurt pots and foil. The unit concludes with an exploration of materials used in vehicles and creating sensory connections inspired by touchy-feely books.

ci: An inquiry into rights and responsibilities in the struggle to share finite resources with other people and with other living things.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded

s.d: Connecting to the 'Food Origin Stories' and flowchart activities (e.g., peanut to peanut butter), students can use Book Creator to document how food changes. Prep students can use the iPad camera to take photos of their hand-drawn flowcharts or raw ingredients, and then use the simple audio record button to verbally explain the process of change, reducing the cognitive load of writing.

During the 'Lunchbox Audit' action task, students can use Seesaw to document their food waste. They can take a photo of their lunchbox before and after eating, use the drawing tool to circle the leftover food or packaging, and use the microphone to record a reflection on whether the waste can go to the classroom compost bin or landfill.

To capture the 'process of change' during the Tuning In phase where students plant seeds in the Prep garden, set up an iPad on a tripod. Students can take one photo every few days using Stop Motion Studio. Over the unit, this will compile into a short time-lapse video, allowing Prep students to visually witness the slow growth of their plants.

Following the Chesterfield Farm visit, students can select a photo of a farm animal they observed. Using Chatterpix Kids, they simply swipe a line across the animal's mouth and record their voice in character, explaining what food product they provide (e.g., 'I am a bee, I collect nectar to make honey!'). This is highly engaging and perfectly suited for Prep fine motor skills.

To reinforce the 'Composting Project' and sorting waste, teachers can set up a floor map with images of various waste items (apple cores, plastic wrappers, sandwich crusts). Students use the screen-free colour tiles to program the Sphero indi car to navigate towards the compostable items and avoid the landfill items, blending early algorithmic thinking with sustainable action.

plannerText: This Prep unit explores the central idea that the products people use go through a process of change, closely aligning with the sustainable management of finite resources. The inquiry focuses on where food comes from, how it is transformed for different purposes, and developing an appreciation for food. Key learning experiences include planting seeds in the Prep garden to monitor growth, creating flowcharts to understand how raw ingredients like peanuts and honey are processed, and visiting Chesterfield Farm to observe food-producing animals. Students also engage in practical action to reduce environmental impact by conducting a lunchbox waste audit, weighing their rubbish, and setting up a classroom compost bin. Through these hands-on activities, Prep students build foundational understandings of food origins, the transformation of natural resources, and their personal responsibility in managing food waste sustainably.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Family histories shape who we are and what we value
lo: What makes a family; The people in your family; Your family history; What is important to your family

s.d: During the L3 lesson where students map out where their families come from, use Google Earth on iPads or the interactive whiteboard. The teacher can guide students to 'fly' from Australia to their family's country of origin. Students can take a screenshot of their destination to upload to their Seesaw portfolio, providing a highly visual and engaging way for Prep students to connect their personal history to global geography.

Following the L2 activity where students create a physical family picture with a house front, students can use iPads to photograph their artwork. Using Chatterpix Kids, they can draw a simple line over a family member's face or the house, and record their voice (up to 30 seconds) introducing who is in their family. This lowers the cognitive load for writing while powerfully supporting oral language and communication skills.

For the L4 activity comparing what children like to do on their own versus with friends, replace the physical class brainstorm with an interactive Seesaw activity. The teacher can provide a digital Venn diagram template. Prep students can use the camera tool to take photos of activities around the room, drag them into the correct circles, and use the microphone tool to verbally explain their sorting choices.

To capture the L2 provocation where parents visit to teach a childhood game, create a collaborative digital class book titled 'Games Our Families Play'. Teachers and students can use iPads to take photos or short videos of the class participating in the cultural games. These media assets can be added to Book Creator pages, where Prep students can simply tap the audio record button to reflect on what they enjoyed about each game.

Enhance the L3 Family Tree activity (using collected autumn leaves and templates) by having students animate the 'growth' of their family. Using iPads and Stop Motion Studio, students can take a photo of their bare tree template, add one leaf (representing a family member) at a time, and snap subsequent photos. Playing it back creates a magical animation of their family history coming together, combining fine motor skills with digital sequencing.

plannerText: This Prep unit, under the transdisciplinary theme 'Where We Are in Place and Time,' explores how family histories shape who we are and what we value. Over seven weeks, students inquire into the form and connections of families, examining what makes a family, who belongs to it, and their unique histories. Key learning experiences include creating physical family portraits, mapping family origins on a world map, and constructing family trees using autumn leaf rubbings. Students also investigate family values by sharing cultural games taught by parents, discussing important family celebrations, and comparing past and present family photographs. The unit integrates literacy through themed storybooks and mathematics by connecting celebrations to months of the year. Assessments are captured via Seesaw, culminating in a digital booklet that celebrates the diverse structures, traditions, and histories of the students' families.

ci: Schools are organised to help students learn and work together
lo: The organisation of the Junior School; Routines that allow developing independence; How to make the classroom and playground healthy and safe

s.d: Following the L1 campus tour, students use iPads to take photos of key school locations (e.g., library, Prep room, pool). These photos are placed under a transparent floor grid. Students then practice positional language by programming Beebots to navigate from the 'classroom' to other school areas, reinforcing their spatial understanding of Wesley College's organisation.

Directly supporting the L1 summative assessment, students use the iPad camera to photograph their hand-drawn pictures of four things they learned about Wesley College. They upload these images to Seesaw and use the microphone tool to verbally explain their drawings, removing writing barriers and allowing them to independently articulate their understanding of the school.

To reinforce the L3 Essential Agreement discussions around health and safety, students photograph objects that keep them safe (e.g., a soap dispenser, a sun hat, or a piece of fruit). Using Chatterpix Kids, they draw a mouth on the object and record a short, playful voiceover of the object stating its safety rule, which is then shared with the class.

Connecting to the L1 inquiry into 'Who do we know that works in the Junior School?', the class collaboratively builds a 'Who's Who at Wesley' digital book. Using a teacher-made template in Book Creator, students insert photos they have taken of different staff members and use the audio tool to record a sentence about how that person helps the school community.

Students can use Seesaw's camera to photograph different areas of the school during campus tours, then use the drawing tools to annotate maps or highlight key features, and record their voice explaining the layout and organisation. They can take photos of key staff members and record audio or video explaining their roles within the school community. To demonstrate independence and understanding of routines, students can record short videos of themselves performing daily classroom routines (e.g., borrowing readers, morning unpacking) and verbally explain the steps and their importance. For the Essential Agreement, students can photograph the co-created agreement and record their voice explaining specific actions or behaviours that keep their classroom and playground healthy and safe. Seesaw acts as a digital portfolio, allowing students to document their learning journey over the five weeks through a combination of drawings, photographs, and verbal explanations, directly aligning with the unit's assessment methods.

plannerText: This Prep unit of inquiry, under the theme 'Who We Are', explores the central idea that schools are organised to help students learn and work together. Over five weeks, students investigate the physical layout and organisation of the Wesley College Junior School through campus tours and mapping activities. They identify key staff members and their roles within the school community. A major focus is placed on developing independence through the establishment of daily classroom routines, such as borrowing readers and morning unpacking. Furthermore, students collaboratively co-create an Essential Agreement, exploring actions and behaviors that keep their classroom and playground healthy and safe, such as sun safety and hygiene. Formative and summative assessments involve drawing, photographing, and verbally explaining their understanding of the school environment and their responsibilities within it.

ci: People use imagination and materials to express stories, ideas and culture through performance.
lo: Different materials to make puppets; Types and uses of puppets by different cultures; Features and purposes of puppet performances

s.d: Students will use Seesaw's built-in video recording feature to capture their collaborative puppet show performances. This allows them to directly demonstrate their understanding of story structure, voice projection, and audience engagement. After recording, students can easily add audio or video reflections to their posts, explaining their creative choices and evaluating their performance. Peers can then provide constructive feedback directly on the video posts, fostering a rich environment for self and peer assessment, all within a platform they are already familiar with for reflection.

During the 'Mini puppet world tour' lesson, replace the static world map with Google Earth on iPads or the interactive whiteboard. As students learn about different cultural puppets (like Balinese shadow puppets or Italian marionettes), 'fly' to these countries. This highly visual experience helps Year 1 students build concrete spatial awareness and connects cultural storytelling to real-world geography.

As an extension to physical puppet play, introduce the Puppet Pals app. Students can take photos of the sock puppets they made during the incursion, import them into the app as custom characters, and digitally move them across the screen while recording their voices. This provides a low-stakes, highly engaging way to practice their dialogue and story sequencing before the live performance.

Instead of a paper-based 'story map template', students can use Book Creator to plan the beginning, middle, and end of their puppet shows. Year 1 students can use the drawing tool to sketch their scenes, add emojis for characters, and most importantly, use the audio recording tool to verbally draft their script, significantly reducing the barrier of written expression.

To address the teacher reflection suggesting the inclusion of shadow puppets, students can create black cardboard cut-out characters and perform with them in front of a Green Screen Kit. Using iMovie on iPads, teachers or students can replace the green background with vibrant digital settings (like a castle or forest), blending traditional shadow puppetry with modern digital storytelling.

plannerText: This Year 1 unit under the theme 'How We Express Ourselves' explores how people use imagination and materials to express stories, ideas, and culture through performance. Over five weeks, students investigate the features, purposes, and cultural significance of puppets. Learning experiences begin with provocations like a Year 6 finger puppet show and a sock-puppet-making incursion. Students then research different types of puppets (marionette, shadow, hand) and their global origins using a world map. They analyze the materials used to construct puppets and the structure of puppet performances. For their summative assessment, students work collaboratively in pairs to design a story map featuring a beginning, middle, and end. They then use the sock puppets they created to perform and record a short puppet show, demonstrating their understanding of story structure, voice projection, and audience engagement, followed by peer and self-reflection on Seesaw.

ci: Communities work together by creating systems to help people live and learn.
lo: The roles people play in a community; Systems and routines that help us stay organised; How communities work together to meet needs
