s.d: During the 'Partner research task' where students create a poster of a community helper, students can use Chatterpix Kids on their iPads. After drawing their community helper, they take a photo of it, draw a mouth, and record their voice sharing 1-2 facts they researched from the non-fiction texts. This video is then exported to their Seesaw portfolio, allowing Year 1 students to demonstrate their understanding verbally, reducing the cognitive load of writing.

To enhance the 'Mapping' lesson, students can use the collaborative 'Community mural' they created as a custom Beebot mat. Students program the Beebots to travel between different community buildings (e.g., from the police station to the hospital). A partner can use an iPad camera to record the Beebot's journey, verbally explaining the directional algorithm (forward, turn left) used to navigate the community, which is then uploaded to Seesaw.

For the 'Drawing your community' mind map activity, students can use Book Creator to build a multimodal digital book instead of a paper workbook. They can use the pen tool to draw their family and community helpers, use the iPad camera to take photos of their school environment, and use the audio record button to verbally explain who is in their community and how they connect, perfectly scaffolding the task for early writers.

During the 'Exploring non-fiction texts' scavenger hunt, students can utilize the Epic app to access a curated collection of 'Read-to-Me' books about community helpers, supporting varied Year 1 reading levels. Students can take screenshots of specific text features they find (like a table of contents or glossary), import them into a collaborative Freeform board, and use the markup tools to circle and label the features.

To elevate the 'Community mural' where students build a city out of paper, small groups can use Lego Spike Essential kits to design and build 3D, motorized community systems to place on their mural. Using the intuitive, icon-based coding app, Year 1 students can program simple systems like a spinning windmill, a moving community vehicle, or a boom gate, demonstrating how technology and systems help a community function.

plannerText: This Year 1 unit under the theme 'How We Organise Ourselves' investigates how communities work together by creating systems to help people live and learn. Students explore the various roles people play within a community, focusing on community helpers and the services they provide. Through hands-on activities, they identify systems and routines at home, school, and the wider neighbourhood that keep us organised and safe. Key learning experiences include researching a specific community helper using non-fiction texts, mapping directions using Beebots, and collaboratively constructing a 2D and 3D paper community mural. The unit integrates English through non-fiction text features and research, Mathematics through spatial awareness and mapping, and fosters social and self-management skills as students work together to understand their responsibilities and connections within their local community.

ci: Movement is dependent on different forces.
lo: Ways objects move; The influence different materials have on movement; How designs can be adapted and improved

s.d: For the 'Movement Video Reflection' task in the 'Going further' phase, students can use the Seesaw app on their iPads to record a short video of a peer performing a push, pull, or twist action in real life (e.g., opening a door or pushing a swing). To reduce cognitive load while demonstrating understanding, students can use Seesaw's drawing tool to draw an arrow over the video showing the direction of the force, and use the microphone tool to verbally reflect on what made it move.

During the 'Design a Moving Toy' activity in the 'Sorting out' phase, students can use Lego Spike Essential kits alongside recycled materials. Working in small groups, they can build a simple vehicle or spinning top. This allows Year 1 students to safely explore 'Form' and 'Function' by swapping out different wheel sizes or adding a simple motor block, directly testing how these design adaptations improve the way their toy moves.

At the 'Playdoh' movement station in the 'Finding out' phase, students can use iPads and Stop Motion Studio to document how a shape changes when pushed or pulled. By taking a sequence of photos as they slowly apply force to the Playdoh, students can play back the animation to clearly visualize the cause-and-effect relationship of their applied force over time, developing their ICT and creative-thinking skills.

For the 'Forces detective at home' homework task in the 'Making conclusions' phase, students can use the online version of Book Creator. Instead of drawing on a paper template, students can take photos of three things that move around their house. They can then use the simple audio recording tool to verbally explain the force used (e.g., 'I pulled the drawer open'), which perfectly accommodates the varying literacy levels of Year 1 students.

As an additional 'Movement station' during the 'Tuning in' phase, introduce Sphero indi cars. Year 1 students can explore causation in a screen-free, highly tactile way by placing different coloured silicone tiles on the floor and observing how the car's movement changes (speeding up, slowing down, turning left/right). This directly addresses the inquiry line 'Ways objects move' while introducing foundational computational thinking.

plannerText: This Year 1 unit under the transdisciplinary theme 'How the World Works' investigates the central idea that movement is dependent on different forces. Over six weeks, students explore the various ways objects move, how different materials and surfaces influence this movement, and how designs can be adapted to improve motion. Key day-to-day learning experiences include hands-on movement stations where students test pushing, pulling, rolling, and sliding using everyday toys, magnets, and Playdoh. Students engage in early scientific inquiry by building and testing their own ramps to measure travel distance, and they design moving toys using recycled materials. The unit also features a 'Craft Power' incursion where students build wooden cars, further connecting the concepts of form and function. Assessments involve sorting objects by movement type, recording real-life examples of forces in action, and reflecting on how variables impact moving objects.

ci: Invertebrates are essential to living things and the environment.
lo: The characteristics of invertebrates; The interdependence of invertebrates and their environment; Life cycles of invertebrates

s.d: Connecting to the 'Invertebrates drawings & labelled diagram' activity, students use the iPad camera to photograph their hand-drawn invertebrate. They then use Seesaw's drawing tool to digitally label key features (e.g., exoskeleton, antennae) and the microphone tool to verbally explain how these features help the bug survive. This reduces the cognitive load of writing while effectively capturing their understanding of the concept of 'Form'.

During the 'Life cycles' investigation, students use iPads and Stop Motion Studio to animate the metamorphosis of their chosen invertebrate (e.g., egg to caterpillar to butterfly). Using playdough or natural loose parts, they take sequential photos to demonstrate the stages of growth, reinforcing the key concept of 'Change' through hands-on, visual sequencing that is highly accessible for Year 1 students.

For the 'Information Report sharing' summative task, students publish their drafted reports using Book Creator. They can type simple sentences, import photos of their 'invertebrate art' made from natural materials, and use the audio tool to read their facts aloud. This creates an interactive, multimodal digital book that can be easily and authentically shared with their Year 2 peers.
For the 'Guess the Invertebrate' task, students animate their chosen bug to give clues about its structures (exoskeleton, wings, segments), providing a professional finish for their Year 2 audience.

Following the 'Bug Hunt in the Schoolyard', students use iPads to take photos of the actual invertebrates they find. They import these images into PicCollage to create a digital visual pictograph, sorting the bugs by type or physical characteristics (e.g., wings vs. no wings). This supports early mathematical data handling and scientific classification skills using a visually intuitive, drag-and-drop interface.

plannerText: This Year 1 Sharing the Planet unit explores the central idea that invertebrates are essential to living things and the environment. Over eight weeks, students inquire into the physical characteristics of invertebrates, their life cycles, and their vital roles within ecosystems. The unit begins with hands-on provocations, including a schoolyard bug hunt and an 'Animals of Oz' incursion, to spark curiosity. Students then engage in guided research using non-fiction texts and digital libraries like Epic to become an 'expert' on a specific invertebrate. Learning experiences include creating labelled diagrams, observing waterbugs, and mapping out life cycles of insects like butterflies and ladybugs. The unit culminates in students synthesizing their research into an information report and presenting their findings to Year 2 peers. Through these activities, students develop their research, communication, and thinking skills while fostering a sense of responsibility and care for the natural world.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: An understanding of history helps us to see how the past shapes the future.
lo: Ways in which children's lives have changed over time; Reasons for changes to children's lives over time

s.d: Connecting to the 'timeline of how children have changed' activity, students use Book Creator on iPads to build a multimodal personal timeline. They can import a baby and toddler photo, and take a current selfie. Using the audio recording tool, Year 1 students can verbally explain how they have grown, reducing the cognitive load of writing while authentically documenting their personal history.

For the 'Interview a grandparent or parent' homework task, students use the Seesaw video tool to record their interview at home. This allows Year 1 students to actively listen and engage in the conversation about past school and home life without the developmental barrier of transcribing complex answers, preserving a digital primary source for class discussion.

Following the Supreme Incursions toy exploration, students use iPad digital cameras to photograph different toys. They then use PicCollage to sort these images into 'Wood', 'Metal', and 'Plastic' categories. Dragging and dropping images develops digital fine motor skills while reinforcing the HASS and Science concepts of observable material properties.

Linking to the 'then and now' object comparison, students select an image of a historical artefact (e.g., a rotary phone or Walkman). Using Chatterpix Kids, they draw a mouth on the object and record their voice pretending to be the item, explaining how it was used in the past. This playful, low-barrier app highly engages Year 1s in historical perspective-taking.

Connecting to the 'Toy Sort' focusing on toys that move, students build a modern moving toy (like a simple car) using Lego Spike Essential and program it with early-years icon-based code. They then compare their modern, motor-driven creation to the traditional wooden push-toys from the incursion, prompting rich discussions on how technology has changed the way children play.

plannerText: This Year 1 unit, under the theme 'Where We Are in Place and Time', explores how an understanding of history helps us see how the past shapes the future. Students investigate how children's lives and toys have changed over time. Key learning experiences include exploring artefacts from the past (like Walkmans and old cameras), participating in a 'Toys Over Time' incursion, and categorising toys based on their materials (wood, metal, plastic). Students also create personal timelines showing their growth from baby to toddler to now, and interview parents or grandparents to compare past and present home and school lives. The unit culminates in sorting old and new toys and reflecting on how technological changes impact the way children play.

ci: Emotions, values, and relationships guide how we connect and collaborate
lo: The function of different parts of the brain; Situations that cause pleasant and unpleasant feelings; Actions that support wellbeing

s.d: Students animate their 'Colour Monster' to explain what triggers a 'feelings storm,' focusing on using an expressive tone to communicate the specific emotion.

During the 'Wheel of Choice' role-play lessons where students practice responding to tricky situations, students can use the video tool in Seesaw to record their partner acting out a conflict resolution strategy (e.g., walking away, taking deep breaths). They can then use the microphone and drawing tools over the video to circle which specific strategy from the Wheel of Choice they demonstrated, capturing their social skills in action.

To consolidate the 'Have you filled a bucket today?' and 'Random Acts of Kindness' lessons, the teacher can set up a collaborative class book in Book Creator. Each Year 1 student gets a single page to document how they are a bucket filler. They can use the iPad camera to take a photo of themselves doing a kind act, and use the simple audio recording button to verbally explain their action, reducing the cognitive load of writing while demonstrating understanding.

Linking to the Visual Arts integration where students create paper sculptures expressing a specific emotion, students can photograph their finished 3D sculptures. They can import this photo into PicCollage and use the text, drawing, and sticker tools to surround their sculpture with 'feelings vocabulary' (happy, calm, angry) that matches the bold lines and dynamic shapes they used, reinforcing emotional literacy.

Connecting the Music outcomes (how music makes them feel) with the 'feelings storm' discussions, students can use the Live Loops feature in Garageband to compose a short 10-second 'feelings soundtrack'. They can select slow, acoustic loops to represent the 'Calm/Green' monster, or fast, loud percussion to represent the 'Angry/Red' monster, helping them explore emotional regulation through auditory expression.

plannerText: This Year 1 'Who We Are' unit explores how thoughts, feelings, and actions impact relationships and wellbeing. Students investigate the function of the brain, situations that trigger pleasant and unpleasant emotions, and actions that support emotional regulation. Key learning experiences include reading texts like 'The Colour Monster' and 'First Day Jitters', creating a 'Wheel of Choice' for conflict resolution, and exploring the concept of being a 'bucket filler'. Transdisciplinary connections involve creating expressive paper sculptures in Visual Arts and exploring how music evokes feelings. The unit heavily emphasises social-emotional learning, empathy, and developing practical strategies for managing 'feelings storms'.

ci: Storytellers use different methods to convey meaning.
lo: Mediums and techniques artists use; Connections between story and illustrations; How ideas and information are communicated

s.d: Connecting to the author investigations of Dr. Seuss and Julia Donaldson, students can author their own multimodal digital picture books. Year 2 students can easily combine text, digital drawings, and use the audio recording feature to practice reading their stories with expressive tone, reducing the cognitive load of extensive handwriting while fulfilling the English curriculum link for creating imaginative texts.

Following the Axel Scheffler seascape provocation on how colour choices create meaning, students use iPads and Sketchbook to paint their own digital seascapes. The app allows Year 2 students to experiment with different digital brushes and easily switch between warm and cool colour palettes to demonstrate how colour impacts the mood and emotion of a visual story.

Linking to the Visual Arts exploration of Indigenous Australian symbols and storytelling, students can use ScratchJR to animate a short, symbol-based narrative. The icon-based block coding is developmentally perfect for Year 2, allowing them to program simple movements and interactions between characters (sprites) to communicate a story without needing advanced reading skills.

To support the Music and JS Production learning experiences focusing on facial expressions and singing tone, students can use the video recording tool in Seesaw. They can record themselves performing a short poem or song, then use the microphone and drawing tools to reflect on how their specific facial expressions and vocal dynamics helped convey the story's meaning to the audience.

Inspired by the 'Behind the scenes' look at how picture books are made, students can explore animation as a storytelling medium. Working in small groups with iPads, Year 2 students can use Stop Motion Studio to bring paper cut-outs or clay characters to life, learning how a sequence of images can communicate a narrative and convey meaning over time.

plannerText: This Year 2 unit explores the central idea that storytellers use different methods to convey meaning. Students investigate the diverse mediums and techniques artists and authors use to communicate ideas, focusing on the connections between stories and illustrations. Key learning experiences include author studies on Sarah Allen, Dr. Seuss, and Julia Donaldson, as well as examining Axel Scheffler's use of colour to create mood in seascapes. Transdisciplinary connections are strong, with students exploring Indigenous Australian art symbols in Visual Arts to tell stories connected to the land, and practicing facial expressions, tone, and dynamics in Music to convey narrative moods. The unit culminates in students creating their own imaginative and multimodal texts, applying their understanding of how different visual, auditory, and digital storytelling methods impact an audience.

ci: A sequence of processes enables a system to function.
lo: The systems in our lives; The purpose of various systems; How systems are made of many interdependent parts

s.d: For the 'Finding out' activity where students document their 'getting ready for school' system, students can use Book Creator on iPads. They can take photos of themselves acting out each step, sequence them chronologically across the pages, and use the audio recording tool to verbally explain how missing a step (like forgetting their school bag) would cause their morning system to break down, reducing cognitive load while practicing sequencing.

During the 'Sorting out' phase where students sequence picture prompts of different systems (like the steps of making bread), place the pictures on a Beebot grid. Students must program the Beebot with the correct sequence of directional commands to visit each step of the system in the correct chronological order, physically reinforcing how a sequence of processes (code) enables a system to function.

To support the 'Going further' investigation into locational and directional systems, students can use Sphero indi and its colored silicone tiles. Working in small groups, they must design a functional road system by sequencing the colored tiles correctly to guide the car from a starting point to a destination, learning hands-on how specific inputs create a functioning navigational system.

In the 'Making conclusions' phase, replace the paper blank flowchart with Freeform on iPads. Students can use the infinite canvas to map out the 'journey of a letter' or a personal system they know. They can easily drag and drop shapes, insert relevant emojis or photos, and draw arrows with their fingers or styluses to visually connect the interdependent parts of the system.

For the 'Taking action' summative task where students research and present a system (like a marble run, simple machine, or Rube Goldberg setup), students can use Seesaw's multimodal tools. They can record a video of their physical model in action, then use the drawing tool to add arrows over the video and the microphone to explain how the interdependent parts achieve the intended purpose.

plannerText: This Year 2 unit under the theme 'How We Organise Ourselves' investigates the central idea that a sequence of processes enables a system to function. Students explore the interconnectedness of human-made systems in their daily lives, such as school routines, lunch orders, and evacuation procedures. Through hands-on provocations like a 'morning of chaos' without standard routines, students quickly realize the purpose and necessity of systems. The inquiry progresses into mapping out personal routines, investigating locational systems like the Dewey Decimal system, and analyzing the journey of everyday items like milk or a posted letter. For their summative assessment, students research a specific system of their choice?such as a marble run, simple machine, or daily routine?and create flowcharts and multimodal presentations to demonstrate how interdependent parts work together to achieve an intended purpose.

ci: Changes to materials affect how we use and reuse them in daily life.
lo: Understanding material properties; Reuse and recycling through physical change; Sustainably managing materials

s.d: During the 'Finding out' phase where students manipulate materials (tearing, bending, crushing), they can use Seesaw to document the process. Taking before-and-after photos, students can use the microphone tool to verbally explain the physical changes and new properties, reducing the cognitive load of writing while capturing their scientific observations.

Students can use Seesaw's native video and audio recording features to document their learning journey. They can record themselves demonstrating the physical manipulation of materials (bending, twisting, stretching, breaking) and explaining their observations and findings. For the 'Odd One Out' game, students can record their game in action, articulating their classifications and reasoning. Crucially, Seesaw allows students to capture and share their 'Reuse Musical Orchestra' performance, showcasing their recycled instruments and explaining the sustainable practices involved. This provides a dynamic and accessible way for Year 2 students to present their hands-on work, verbalize their understanding, and share their creative projects, directly replacing the interactive video sharing aspect of Flip.

For the summative visual representation task, students can use Canva to create a digital chart or diagram. Using a simple graphic organizer template, Year 2 students can drag and drop photos of their manipulated materials and add basic text labels to demonstrate the relationship between physical changes and material properties.

To enhance the 'Reuse Musical Orchestra Concert' action piece, students can integrate Makey Makey kits with their recycled instruments. By attaching alligator clips to conductive recycled materials (like metal cans or foil), students can trigger digital sounds on their devices, blending their understanding of physical material properties with basic circuitry.

When exploring First Nations cultural practices like twisting yarn or weaving in the 'Going further' section, students can use Stop Motion Studio. They can animate the step-by-step physical transformation of a raw material into a functional object, helping them visually break down and understand the sequence of physical changes.

plannerText: This Year 2 'How the World Works' unit focuses on the properties of materials and how physical changes affect their use and reusability in daily life. Through a STEM lab focus, students explore how materials can be bent, twisted, stretched, and broken without altering their chemical composition. Learning experiences include hands-on manipulation of various materials (fabric, paper, rubber, metal), creating an 'Odd One Out' game to classify physical changes, and investigating First Nations Australians' traditional practices of material manipulation. For their summative assessments, students create a visual representation linking physical changes to material properties and participate in a 'Reuse Musical Orchestra,' where they design and play instruments made entirely from recycled materials, highlighting sustainable practices and environmental responsibility.

ci: Patterns in the sky help us understand our place in the universe.
lo: Earth's place in the solar system; Observable patterns in the sky; Tools and methods we use to observe the sky

s.d: To support the 'Wonder Wall' provocation, set up a Padlet board where Year 2 students can document their initial questions about space. To accommodate varying literacy levels, students can use the audio recording or drawing features to post their wonderings, ensuring all voices are captured without the cognitive load of typing.

For the 'Moon Diary' homework task, utilize Seesaw as a digital journal. Students can step outside with an iPad or home device, take a photo of the moon, and use the drawing tool to trace its shape. The microphone feature allows them to verbally explain the phase, supporting oral language development and making daily tracking interactive.

During the 'Sorting Out' phase exploring Earth's movements, students can use Lego Spike Essential kits to build a simple motorized model of the Earth and Sun. Using the intuitive, icon-based coding interface, Year 2s can program the motor to rotate, providing a concrete, hands-on representation of how rotation causes day and night.

For the 'Planet Research Projects', small groups can synthesize their findings using Book Creator. The platform's accessibility allows Year 2 students to easily combine text, hand-drawn illustrations of their planet, and voice recordings. This multimodal approach reduces writing barriers and lets them proudly present their informative texts.

Elevate the 'Weather Reporter Roleplay' action piece by setting up a Green Screen Kit. Students can film their daily sky and weather broadcasts on iPads, using iMovie to superimpose images of the current moon phase or weather patterns behind them. This highly engaging activity develops their communication skills and scientific vocabulary.

plannerText: This Year 2 IB PYP unit under the theme 'How the World Works' investigates the central idea that patterns in the sky help us understand our place in the universe. Over five weeks, students explore the Earth's place in the solar system, observable sky patterns like day/night, moon phases, and seasons, and the tools used to observe them. Learning experiences are highly interactive, including creating a digital Wonder Wall, tracking shadows, keeping a daily Moon Diary, and building papier-m?ch? solar system models. Students also engage in cultural astronomy, learning how First Nations Peoples use sky knowledge. The unit culminates in collaborative planet research projects and a 'Weather Reporter' roleplay, allowing students to demonstrate their understanding of celestial cycles, scientific vocabulary, and the cause-and-effect relationships between Earth's movements and our daily lives.

ci: Water is a limited resource that is essential to life.
lo: Unequal access to water; Communities affected by water access; Transferral of water from source to use; Responsibility as global citizens

s.d: During the 'Finding out' phase, students use the drawing and microphone tools in Seesaw to create a digital, interactive 'Water Word Wall'. For new vocabulary like 'source' or 'pollution', Year 2s can draw their understanding and record a short audio explanation, reducing writing cognitive load while accurately capturing their comprehension.

After watching the Water Cycle clip in the 'Sorting out' phase, students use iPads and Stop Motion Studio to animate the water cycle (evaporation, condensation, precipitation) using playdough or paper cutouts. This hands-on maker approach makes the abstract system concrete and visible for Year 2 learners.

To support the 'Taking Action' phase, students use Canva to design 'water wise' awareness posters for the Junior School. Using age-appropriate templates, Year 2s can drag and drop water-saving icons and type simple pledges (e.g., 'Turn off the tap!') to print and display around the campus.

Connecting to the playground water play area design action, students use Lego Spike Essential to prototype a simple, motorized water-saving feature (like a timed gate). This introduces basic engineering and coding while keeping fine motor requirements and cognitive load perfectly suited for Year 2.

Elevating the 'wetlands game board' student agency activity, students design a large grid map of a wetland ecosystem. They then program Beebots to navigate from a 'water source' to a 'community,' avoiding 'pollution' squares, seamlessly integrating directional maths and computational thinking into their inquiry.

plannerText: This Year 2 Sharing the Planet unit explores the central idea that water is a limited resource essential to life. Students investigate the water cycle as a system, learning how water transfers from its source to communities. Through provocations like 'The Lorax' and 'A Wash in Hope,' the inquiry highlights the unequal access to clean water globally and the impact this has on different communities. Students engage in hands-on learning by creating water cycle models, developing interactive word walls, and designing wetland game boards. The unit culminates in a strong 'Taking Action' component, where students apply their knowledge to advocate for sustainable practices. They create water-saving posters, formulate personal 'water wise' pledges, and even conceptualise a water-efficient play area for the school playground, fostering a sense of global and local responsibility.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploring the past helps us to understand the present.
lo: Evidence and its significance in a place; Connections between the past and present within a place; Personal history shapes the present

s.d: During the 'Sorting out' phase where students sequence the Wesley plaques they photographed on their Wonder Walk, provide a simple Microsoft PowerPoint template. Year 2 students can insert their iPad photos onto a digital timeline slide, practicing basic digital literacy skills like resizing images and dragging them into chronological order from oldest to newest.

To enhance the 'Finding out' visual thinking routine comparing historical and current Melbourne landmarks, use Google Earth on iPads. Students can search for the landmarks they are studying and use Street View to explore the modern-day surroundings, directly comparing the digital present-day environment with their printed historical photographs to identify changes in infrastructure and transport.
Use the podcasting feature to edit and present the oral histories gathered during the Campbell Place Aged Care visit, focusing on the connection between the residents' past and the students' present.

When investigating Wesley artefacts (such as the lion, mace, or old uniforms) in the 'Finding out' stage, have students use Chatterpix Kids. They can take a photo of an artefact, draw a digital mouth on it, and record a 30-second voiceover speaking from the perspective of the object, explaining its history and what changes it has 'witnessed' at the college over time.

For the final Artefact Summative Project, students can use Book Creator to design a multimodal digital portfolio of their chosen historical item. They can use the camera tool to photograph their artefact, the audio recording feature to verbally explain its connection to the past, and the pen tool to circle specific evidence on the image, catering perfectly to Year 2 literacy and fine motor levels.

plannerText: In this Year 2 unit under the theme 'Where We Are in Place and Time', students inquire into how exploring the past helps us understand the present. The unit focuses on historical evidence, connections between past and present, and personal histories. Learning experiences include a 'Wonder Walk' around the Wesley Glen Waverley campus to photograph historical plaques and statues, and an excursion to Como House to compare 1800s servant life with modern living. Students also analyze historical Melbourne landmarks, investigate Wesley College artefacts like the mace and old uniforms, and interview residents at Campbell Place Aged Care to gather oral histories. The unit culminates in an artefact summative project where students demonstrate their understanding of how personal and local history shapes their current world.

ci: Humans have a responsibility in their relationships to value the rights of others
lo: The difference between needs and wants; Everybody has rights and responsibilities; What is a respectful relationship

s.d: To support the 'Needs/Wants/Rights' sorting activity (L1 & L2), create a drag-and-drop template in Seesaw. Provide visual icons of various items (water, shelter, toys, candy). Year 2 students can drag the items into a T-chart and, crucially, use the microphone tool to verbally explain their reasoning. This reduces the writing load while allowing them to articulate their understanding of basic human rights versus wants.

For the 'Rights and Responsibility Role-play' (L2), students can use Puppet Pals on their iPads to animate characters acting out common playground conflicts (e.g., not sharing, excluding someone). By recording their voices to negotiate a fair, respectful solution through the digital puppets, students who might be shy about live role-playing can confidently demonstrate their conflict resolution skills.

Instead of a physical 'Respectful Relationships Mural' or 'Kindness Quilt' (L2/L3), use a grid-format Padlet to create a collaborative digital quilt. Students can use the iPad camera to take photos of respectful actions in the classroom or upload digital drawings of what kindness looks like. This creates a highly visual, accessible digital board that visually reinforces the unit's core values.

For the 'What being a friend means to me' activity (L1), have student pairs design a multimedia page in Book Creator. They can use the pen tool to draw their friend, type a simple sentence, and use the audio button to record themselves reading it. The teacher can then combine all pages into a collaborative class eBook to read together, celebrating their shared understanding of friendship.

Students can create a digital 'My Respect Pledge' book. Each page can focus on a different aspect of the pledge (empathy, inclusivity, active listening), allowing students to write their reflections, draw illustrations, and record themselves explaining what these concepts mean to them and how they will demonstrate them in their daily interactions. They can also embed short video clips of role-playing conflict resolution scenarios or demonstrating acts of kindness, providing a rich, multimodal artifact of their learning and commitment to valuing the rights of others.

plannerText: This Year 2 'Who We Are' unit focuses on the central idea that humans have a responsibility in their relationships to value the rights of others. Students explore the fundamental differences between needs and wants, and investigate the concept that everybody has inherent rights and responsibilities. Through engaging texts like 'A Little Spot of Kindness' and 'The Rainbow Fish,' students unpack what constitutes a respectful relationship and what being a good friend looks like. Key learning experiences include role-playing conflict resolution scenarios, creating a collaborative kindness quilt, and sorting needs versus wants. The unit culminates in a student-driven 'Respect Pledge,' empowering these young learners to take actionable steps in treating others with empathy, inclusivity, and active listening within their classroom and the broader global community.

ci: Creativity allows people to express their understanding of the world.
lo: The ways people use technology to express creativity; How texts and images work together; How technology influences our creativity

s.d: Directly supporting the core learning experience of the unit, students use the Stop Motion Studio app on iPads to animate their paper cut-out props. This develops fine motor skills and patience as they capture incremental movements. Once the visual sequence is complete, students export their video to iMovie to layer in voiceovers, sound effects, and the pentatonic scale music compositions they created in Music class, demonstrating how audio and visual technologies combine to communicate a message.

To explore how texts and images work together, students use the comic book layouts in Book Creator to design their graphic novels. They will use the iPad camera to photograph their physical storyboards or Hosier Lane-inspired artwork, importing these images into the app. Students will then apply digital comic conventions such as panels, speech bubbles, and 'emanata' to express their characters' emotions and perspectives, catering perfectly to Year 3 literacy and digital creation skills.

Following the 'That's not a box' provocation and gallery walk, students use Seesaw to document their creative thinking. They capture a photo of their repurposed cardboard creation and use the microphone tool to record an oral reflection explaining the function of their object and the message behind it. This provides an accessible, age-appropriate platform for Year 3 students to practice speaking skills and allows for peer feedback through audio or text comments.

To support the planning and drafting phase of their graphic novels, students use Canva to create a digital character profile or setting mood board. Drawing on their Visual Arts inquiry into Yayoi Kusama, students can search for and arrange geometric shapes, bold colours, and digital elements to visualize their characters' environments and perspectives before moving into the final Book Creator production.

Addressing the teacher reflection noting a missed opportunity to explore audio, students use Garageband to digitally record the pentatonic scale compositions they developed in Music class. Using Smart Instruments (like keyboards or strings), Year 3 students can easily experiment with tempo and pitch to create a custom soundtrack that reflects the emotional tone of their graphic novel or stop-motion animation, deepening their understanding of how audio influences creative expression.

plannerText: This Year 3 unit under the theme 'How We Express Ourselves' investigates the central idea that creativity allows people to express their understanding of the world. Students explore how technology influences creativity and how texts and images work together to communicate messages. Key learning experiences involve hands-on digital creation, specifically using Stop Motion animation and designing graphic novels. Students progress through the creative process?from storyboarding and creating physical props to utilizing digital tools to animate, add audio, and format comic panels. Transdisciplinary connections are made through music (composing pentatonic melodies) and visual arts (Yayoi Kusama-inspired patterns). The unit emphasizes student agency in choosing their creative messages and culminates in a parent workshop where students share their digital storytelling skills.

ci: Needs can be met with digital systems.
lo: What are digital systems; Ideas and information can be organised and shared in safe online environments; Being a responsible digital citizen

s.d: For the 'Technology Sort' provocation, students can use Canva to collaboratively drag and drop images of historical and modern technologies into a digital T-chart. This allows Year 3 students to easily manipulate visual data without the fine motor demands of cutting and pasting, while introducing them to basic digital organization skills.

To map out a desktop computer's hardware and software components, students can use Explain Everything. Year 3 students can import photos of computer peripherals, draw connecting lines, and use the microphone tool to verbally explain the function of each part, reducing the cognitive load of typing complex technical vocabulary.

During the 'Look inside a Sphero' activity, students can examine the clear casing of a Sphero BOLT to identify its internal hardware. They can then use Seesaw's camera and drawing tools to take a photo of the BOLT, annotate the visible input sensors and output displays, and record a short audio reflection explaining how these components work together as a digital system.

To visualize their personal digital footprint, students can use PicCollage to assemble a digital poster. They can search for and import safe icons representing the apps, games, and websites they frequently use, arranging them into the shape of a footprint. This provides a highly visual, age-appropriate way for 8-year-olds to grasp the abstract concept of an online identity.

For their summative assessment, students can publish their individual Digital Citizen Essential Agreements using Book Creator. They can dedicate a page to each key concept (e.g., consumption vs. creation, online etiquette), utilizing the comic book layouts, adding voice recordings for accessibility, and embedding their digital footprints, resulting in a multimodal digital portfolio of their learning.

plannerText: This Year 3 unit under the theme 'How We Organise Ourselves' explores the central idea that needs can be met with digital systems. Students investigate what digital systems are, focusing on hardware, software, and peripherals, and how they have evolved over time. They engage in hands-on activities like sorting 'then and now' technologies, mapping the components of a desktop computer, and looking inside a Sphero to understand inputs and outputs. A significant portion of the unit is dedicated to digital citizenship, where students track their screen time using a digital log, explore the concepts of public versus private online spaces, and analyze their own digital footprints. The unit culminates in students reflecting on their balance of digital consumption versus creation and collaboratively developing an Essential Agreement for responsible and safe digital technology use.

ci: Observing how materials change helps us make sense of the physical world.
lo: Observable properties of solids and liquids; How heating and cooling change state; Scientific thinking helps solve everyday problems

s.d: Connecting to Lesson 1 (Sunken shapes), students use Freeform on iPads to create a digital sorting board. They drag images of everyday materials into 'Solid' or 'Liquid' categories and use drawing tools to annotate observable properties (e.g., 'holds its shape' vs 'takes the shape of the container'). This visual approach reduces cognitive load while building foundational scientific vocabulary.

During Lesson 2 (Heat it up/Cool customers), students use iPads and Stop Motion Studio to capture a time-lapse of chocolate, butter, and ice melting. This allows Year 3s to visually analyze and replay the transformation at different speeds, making the abstract concept of 'causation' (heat adding/removing) highly concrete and observable.

For Lesson 5 (Sorting out), students use Book Creator to document how First Nations Australians use heat to change the state of beeswax and resins. They can insert images of traditional tools and use the audio recording feature to verbally explain the reversible changes, supporting Year 3 students who may have stronger oral than written communication skills.

Connecting to Lesson 6 (Break it up), students use Seesaw to capture 'before' and 'after' photos of their chocolate pieces when investigating how shape affects melting rates. They use the drawing and microphone tools to annotate their photos and verbally interpret their data, explaining why smaller pieces melted faster using target vocabulary.
Students record a 'Science News' podcast for their 'Taking Action' homework, explaining a real-world change of state (like melting or freezing) observed in their own kitchen.

plannerText: This Year 3 unit, under the theme 'How the World Works', focuses on the central idea that observing how materials change helps us make sense of the physical world. Students inquire into the observable properties of solids and liquids and investigate how heating and cooling cause reversible changes of state. Through hands-on experiments like melting chocolate, butter, and ice, and freezing water, students explore the concepts of form, change, and causation. The unit integrates First Nations perspectives by examining how Indigenous Australians use changes of state in materials like beeswax and resins for practical purposes. Students develop their scientific thinking, data collection, and communication skills by predicting outcomes, observing melting rates based on shape, and applying their understanding to everyday real-life scenarios, ultimately reflecting on how scientific knowledge helps solve everyday problems.

ci: The diversity of soils, rocks, and minerals shapes the way people value and use Earth's resources.
lo: Observable properties of soils, rocks and minerals; Earth's resources are vital and must be used responsibly; First Nations knowledge of soils and rocks

s.d: During the 'Local Soil Study' and 'Zoom In' routine, students use iPad cameras to take macro photos of their collected soil and rock samples. They upload these images to Seesaw, using the drawing and microphone tools to annotate observable properties like colour and grain size, and to identify components such as pebbles or plant matter.

Following the 'Rock and Mineral Hunt', students use Freeform to create a digital sorting board. They import photos of the local rocks they observed and physically group them on the infinite canvas based on properties like texture or crystal size, adding text labels to explain their classification criteria.

To support the 'Sorting Out' comparison of natural features, students use Google Earth to virtually explore the landforms and terrains of Australia and a neighbouring country like Indonesia. They capture screenshots of these environments and import them into a Canva Venn diagram template to compare climates, vegetation, and visible rock formations.

For the 'Cultural Connections' inquiry into First Nations Australians' use of rocks and minerals, students synthesize their research in Book Creator. They can embed images of traditional tools like grindstones, record audio explaining the cultural significance of ochre, and use the digital pen tool to create their own earth-toned illustrations.

For the 'Making Conclusions' summative assessment, students use Canva to design an infographic poster. They utilize Canva's visual elements to illustrate the journey of Earth's resources, showing the connection between raw minerals and everyday items (like graphite in pencils) to demonstrate why these materials are vital for living things and cultures.

plannerText: This Year 3 Sharing the Planet unit investigates how the diversity of soils, rocks, and minerals shapes the way people value and use Earth's resources. Students explore the observable properties of geological materials through hands-on soil studies and rock hunts around the school grounds. They investigate the vital role these resources play in everyday life and the importance of sustainable practices. The unit incorporates a strong cultural perspective, examining how First Nations Australians have historically used and valued rocks and minerals for tools, pigments, and food preparation. Additionally, students compare Australia's natural features and landforms with neighbouring countries. The unit culminates in a summative presentation where students synthesize their understanding of how geological resources connect living things, societies, and environments across different places.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Land and place are represented and valued in different ways.
lo: How Australia and neighbouring regions define places; Australia's states/territories have unique identities; First Nations connections to Country

s.d: For the pre-assessment activity where students draw what they know about the map of the world, Australia, and Melbourne, students can use Freeform on their iPads. They can use the Apple Pencil or their fingers to sketch their maps, and utilize the audio recording or text tools to annotate their drawings, providing teachers with a clear baseline of their spatial awareness and geographical vocabulary.

During the 'Going Further' phase where students locate Australia's neighbouring countries, they can use Google Earth. Year 3 students can virtually 'fly' from Melbourne to Pacific Island nations, New Zealand, and Indonesia. They can use the digital measurement tool to see how far these countries are from Australia and use Street View to observe the different natural and constructed features of these regions.

To compare the boundaries of Aboriginal Countries with Australian states and territories, students can use Canva. They can import the AIATSIS Map of Indigenous Australia and a standard political map of Australia into a single project. Using Canva's transparency sliders and digital drawing tools, students can overlay the maps to visually analyze and annotate the differences between colonial and First Nations representations of the land.

For the 'Australia Is...' poetry task, students can publish their poems in Book Creator. They can dedicate a page to each state or territory, typing their verses and adding relevant digital images or their own drawings of the landscapes and animals mentioned. They can also use the audio tool to record themselves reading the poem, practicing their oral presentation and fluency skills.

When creating their 'My Country, My Place' sensory poems exploring First Nations perspectives on connection to land, students can use Adobe Express to create a digital video story. They can select evocative, royalty-free images of Australian landscapes that match the mood of their free verse, record their voice narrating the poem, and add subtle background music to communicate the spiritual and environmental value of the land.

plannerText: This Year 3 unit, under the theme 'Where We Are in Place and Time,' explores how land and place are represented and valued in different ways. Students investigate the geography of Australia, identifying states, territories, and capital cities, while also locating neighbouring countries like New Zealand and Indonesia. A significant focus is placed on First Nations Australians' deep, ongoing spiritual and cultural connections to Country. Students compare standard political maps of Australia with the AIATSIS Map of Indigenous Australia to understand different geographical boundaries and historical perspectives. Through poetry creation, mapping activities, and research, students express how places are defined, how unique identities are formed, and what it means to belong to a place.

ci: The choices and actions we make contribute to our health and wellbeing.
lo: What it means to be healthy; Choices and their impact on health; Actions to maintain a healthy mind and body

s.d: During the 'Tuning In' phase, use Padlet to host the 10 health and wellbeing images for the 'See, Think, Wonder' routine. Students can use iPads or laptops to view the images and add their responses as comments under each column. For the 'Colour Symbol Image' routine, students can use Padlet's drawing tool to sketch their symbol and explain their colour choice via an audio recording, making their thinking visible and collaborative for the whole class.

To support the 'Finding Out' investigation into bones and muscles, students can use Sketchbook on their iPads after watching the 'Fuel for our Body' video. Providing a basic digital outline of a human body, students can use different digital brushes and layers to draw and colour the skeletal and muscular systems. They can then use the text tool to label key parts, developing both their anatomical understanding and digital fine motor skills.

For the 'Sorting Out' Maths statistics integration, students can use Canva to visualize their data investigations on healthy eating and sleep. After collecting tally data from their peers, students can input this information into Canva's chart templates to create digital bar graphs or pictographs. This allows Year 3 students to easily interpret data, customize colours, and add relevant icons (like fruits or beds) to make their mathematical representations clear and engaging.

Following the guest speaker visits from the Dietician and Doctor, students can use Seesaw to document their personal health action plans. They can utilize Seesaw's multimodal tools to take a photo of their written goal, use the microphone to orally explain why they chose this specific target (e.g., improving sleep or eating more vegetables), and use the drawing tool to illustrate what success looks like, creating an accessible reflection for their digital portfolio.

For the 'Making Conclusions' summative assessment, students can design their 'Health Advice Profiles' using Book Creator. They can synthesize their learning about nutrition, sleep, and exercise by creating a multimedia digital book for a fictional character. Year 3 students can type advice, use the camera to insert photos of the healthy lunchboxes they designed, and embed voice recordings to orally explain how the character can improve their wellbeing.

plannerText: This Year 3 unit, under the theme 'Who We Are', explores how daily choices and actions impact health and wellbeing. The central idea focuses on the connection between lifestyle habits and overall physical and mental health. Throughout the 6-week planner, students investigate what it means to be healthy by examining nutrition, exercise, sleep, and mindfulness. Key learning experiences include unpacking health vocabulary, analyzing the Australian Guide to Healthy Eating, tracking personal sleep and exercise data, and learning from guest experts like a dietician and doctor. Students integrate mathematics by graphing health data and culminate their learning by creating summative 'health advice profiles' to demonstrate their understanding of balanced lifestyle choices.

ci: Responding to different artistic expressions can promote thinking.
lo: Ways forms of expression convey messages; Creative and critical thinking skills; The language used to respond to The Arts

s.d: Students can leverage Microsoft Teams' native video tools to record and share their responses to various artistic expressions. After visiting the Acquisitive Art Prize and NGV, students can record short video reflections discussing the salience and composition of artworks, fostering critical thinking and developing specific art language. They can also record themselves reciting their original poetry (haikus, shape poems, limericks) or explaining their analysis of song lyrics. For the summative Year 4 Art Show, students can create video 'artist statements' explaining the mood, message, and media of their original artwork, which can be shared within a dedicated Teams channel. This channel can also facilitate peer feedback and discussion through video or text comments, promoting deeper engagement and collaborative learning around artistic responses.

During the poetry writing sessions (Weeks 6-8), students use Canva to publish their final poems, such as their shape poems or haikus. They must select appropriate typography, colors, and background imagery that visually represent the mood and figurative language of their poem, enhancing the message conveyed through their artistic expression.

After analyzing Bruno Mars song lyrics and comparing them to poetry, students use Garageband on iPads to compose a short backing track that matches the mood of one of their written poems. They record themselves performing their poem over the track, exploring how musical elements like tempo and pitch change the audience's interpretation of the text.

For the Taking Action component (Year 4 Art Show), instead of a traditional written plaque, students take a digital photo of their completed artwork and use Chatterpix Kids to make the artwork 'speak'. They record a 30-second artist statement explaining their inspiration, mood, and chosen media, which visitors can listen to via a Seesaw QR code displayed next to the physical art.

During the reading sessions focused on analyzing a painting's salience and composition, students import a photo of an artwork into Explain Everything. They use the digital pen and voice recording tools to annotate the image, circling the salient features, drawing lines to show visual flow, and verbally explaining how these elements guide the viewer's eye and promote thinking.

plannerText: This Year 4 unit under the theme 'How We Express Ourselves' focuses on the central idea that responding to different artistic expressions can promote thinking. Students explore how various forms of expression convey messages and develop the specific language needed to respond to the Arts. Key learning experiences include visiting the Acquisitive Art Prize and the National Gallery of Victoria (NGV) to analyze visual art, focusing specifically on salience and composition. Students also explore written and musical expression by analyzing song lyrics and writing various forms of poetry, including haikus, shape poems, and limericks. As a summative action, students plan, create, and exhibit their own original artwork in a Year 4 Art Show, complete with an artist plaque, demonstrating their understanding of mood, message, and media.

ci: Communities create rules, laws and services to help people live together fairly and safely.
lo: Effective and influential leadership; Strengths that help leaders guide others; How leadership impacts communities

s.d: During the tuning-in phase, students use Freeform on their iPads to create their initial 'looks like, sounds like, feels like' leadership mind maps. They can drop in icons, draw connections with the Apple Pencil, and add digital sticky notes to represent their thinking before watching the Kid President provocation video.

To demonstrate how a leader would solve the provided playground scenarios (such as a younger student having their hat taken), students use Puppet Pals to animate and record a short role-play. This allows Year 4 students to safely act out conflict resolution and practice leadership communication without the pressure of live acting.

While watching 'A Bug's Life', the teacher sets up a Padlet with columns for different characters like Flik and Princess Atta. Students post real-time observations of the leadership qualities and strengths demonstrated by each character, creating a collaborative digital template that the whole class can reference.

For the making conclusions activity, students use Seesaw to upload side-by-side photos of their initial and final leadership quality rankings. They then use the microphone tool to record an audio reflection explaining how and why their perspective on effective leadership has changed, directly addressing the central idea.

For the summative assessment, students can utilize Microsoft Teams' native video recording features to deliver their persuasive speeches. They can record their speeches directly within a Teams assignment or a dedicated channel, allowing peers to easily access and view all speeches. This facilitates the democratic voting process and enables teachers to provide feedback and assess the speeches directly within the Teams environment, perfectly replacing the video submission and peer viewing functionality of 'flip'.

plannerText: This Year 4 unit under the theme 'How We Organise Ourselves' focuses on the central idea that leadership demonstrates people's strengths to reflect and take action. The inquiry explores effective and influential leadership, the specific strengths leaders demonstrate, and the actions they take within a community. Students begin by unpacking their preconceptions of leadership through mind maps and ranking leadership qualities. They analyze leadership traits in media, such as the movie 'A Bug's Life', and apply problem-solving skills to real-world playground scenarios. A significant component of the unit involves a Leadership Day with guest speakers and team-building challenges to put leadership into practice. For their summative assessment, students reflect on their personal strengths, match them to specific Year 4 leadership roles, and write and deliver persuasive speeches to their peers, culminating in a democratic voting process.

ci: The design and use of products can have an impact on the natural world.
lo: How material properties determine their use; Material solutions for a sustainable future; How research solves problems in the natural world

s.d: During the 'Finding Out' phase where students start a science journal, they can use Book Creator to document their ongoing learning. Year 4 students can use the camera tool to capture photos of the 'Mystery Materials', use the audio recording feature to verbally explain their hypotheses (reducing cognitive load for reluctant writers), and use the pen tool to annotate images with key vocabulary like 'decomposing' or 'biodegradable'.

For the lesson where students find objects around the classroom made of different materials, they can complete a Seesaw scavenger hunt. Students take photos of various items (fibres, metals, glass, plastics) and use the microphone and drawing tools to label the material, explaining its properties (e.g., 'waterproof', 'strong') and summarizing its function directly onto the digital canvas.

Students can leverage Microsoft Teams' native video recording and sharing capabilities to document their hands-on science experiments (sink/float, magnetism, friction tests), explaining their observations and findings directly. They can record short video presentations to share their research on material properties, animal welfare, and sustainability concepts. For the summative action, students can create video demonstrations of their sustainable prototypes, articulating their design choices, the physical properties of materials used, and the long-term environmental impact. Teams channels can also facilitate asynchronous video discussions, allowing students to share reflections, provide feedback on prototypes, and engage in deeper conversations about the unit's themes, fostering a collaborative and reflective learning environment.

To support the 'Going Further' lesson comparing domesticated pets and wild animals, students can use Microsoft PowerPoint to create a digital Venn diagram. They can insert images of animals and their habitats, and use shapes and text boxes to map out similarities and differences in their enrichment needs, practicing their analytical thinking and digital formatting skills.

For the 'Taking Action' summative task where students design a prototype of a feeding machine or enrichment toy for a zoo animal, they can use Tinkercad to digitally model their design. This allows them to manipulate 3D shapes, consider the structural properties of their design, and optionally 3D print a scaled-down model to test its physical form and function.

plannerText: This Year 4 IB PYP unit, under the theme 'How the World Works', investigates how the design and use of products impact the natural world. Students explore the properties of various materials (such as fibres, metals, glass, and plastics) and how these characteristics determine their function and environmental footprint. Through hands-on science experiments?including sink/float, magnetism, and friction tests?students observe material behaviors and document their findings in science journals. The unit strongly connects to animal welfare and sustainability, prompting students to compare the needs of domesticated and wild animals. As a summative action, students apply their research to design and create a sustainable prototype of an enrichment toy or feeding machine for an animal in captivity, considering the physical properties of the materials used and their long-term environmental impact.

ci: Country, language and community are significant to the lives of First Nations people.
lo: Diversity of First Nations people; Importance of community, family and traditions; Importance of Country to First Nations people

s.d: During the Week 7 lesson unpacking the Central Idea, students use iPads to access Word Clouds by ABCya. They input key vocabulary discussed in class (Country, language, community, First Nations) and manipulate the layout, colors, and shapes. To deepen the connection to the unit, students can select a shape that represents an Australian animal or natural element, creating a visual representation of their initial understanding to save to their digital portfolios.

For the provocation involving Indigenous symbols and A3 artworks, students use iPads to photograph symbols they find around the classroom. They upload these images into a Canva 'Field Guide' template. In Canva, Year 4 students can add text boxes and arrows to annotate the photos, explaining the meaning of each symbol (e.g., meeting place, waterhole, animal tracks) and demonstrating their ability to decode traditional visual language.

When comparing the Aboriginal map of Australia to a standard world map, students use Google Earth to explore the diverse physical geography of Australia. They take screenshots of different terrains and import them into a Freeform board alongside the AIATSIS Aboriginal Australia map. Using Freeform's drawing and text tools, students annotate the board to highlight how First Nations language groups align with specific geographical features and natural boundaries.

For the 'Going Further' task where students create a Dreamtime-inspired story, they use Book Creator to draft, illustrate, and publish their narratives. Year 4 students can utilize the audio recording feature to narrate their stories, adding expressive reading for their Prep buddies. They can also use the pen tool to draw their own digital illustrations inspired by the traditional symbols they learned about earlier in the unit.

Students can leverage Microsoft Teams' native video capabilities to record and 'publish' their Dreamtime-inspired stories orally, honoring the rich tradition of Indigenous storytelling. This allows them to express their creativity and understanding through spoken word and visual presentation. Additionally, students can record video explanations of their physical dioramas, articulating their understanding of Indigenous culture through physical, cultural, and spiritual lenses, providing a dynamic and authentic assessment of their learning. Teams also facilitates video discussions for reflections on the CERES excursion or analysis of Indigenous symbols and maps, fostering collaborative learning.

plannerText: This Year 4 unit under the theme 'Sharing the Planet' explores how Country, language, and community are significant to First Nations people. Students investigate the diversity of Indigenous groups, the importance of traditions, and their deep connection to Country. Key learning experiences include unpacking the central idea using word clouds, comparing traditional Aboriginal maps with modern world maps, and analyzing Indigenous symbols in artworks. Students also participate in a CERES excursion to learn about bush tucker and traditional practices. For their summative assessments, students write and publish their own Dreamtime-inspired stories with a moral, and construct physical dioramas that demonstrate their understanding of Indigenous culture through physical, cultural, and spiritual lenses.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploration leads to discoveries, opportunities and new understandings.
lo: Reasons for exploration; How people have explored over time; Consequences of exploration

s.d: To enhance the 'What makes you say that?' provocation featuring historical navigational tools, students use ClassVR Headsets to immerse themselves in 360-degree environments of historical ships (like the Endeavour) or early exploration sites. This immersive experience builds crucial background knowledge and visual literacy, allowing Year 4 students to generate richer vocabulary and deeper wonderings before unpacking the central idea.

Connecting to the Middle School mapping activity, students translate their physical maps into a digital coding challenge. Using iPads, students program Sphero BOLTs to navigate a grid or drawn map on the classroom floor that represents their exploration route. This hands-on task reinforces spatial awareness, measurement, and algorithmic thinking while simulating the precise navigation required by early explorers.

For the First Fleet historical narrative task, students publish their writing from a convict's perspective using Book Creator. They can utilize the audio recording feature to narrate their story with expressive emotion, embed historical images they have researched, and use comic layouts to sequence the events of the journey, thereby developing their multimodal literacy skills.
Students animate a character as a modern-day explorer in Antarctica, reporting 'live' on the discovery of meteorites and the challenges of the extreme environment.

For their final 'Taking Action' summative assessment, students use Canva to design an interactive infographic or multimedia presentation about their independently researched explorer. They can embed video clips, construct historical timelines, and apply Year 4-appropriate visual design principles to effectively communicate the reasons for and consequences of their chosen explorer's journey.

plannerText: This Year 4 unit under the theme 'Where We Are in Place and Time' explores the central idea that exploration leads to discoveries, opportunities, and new understandings. Students investigate the reasons for exploration, how methods have changed over time, and the historical consequences of these journeys. Key learning experiences include a provocation using images of historical navigational tools, mapping the Middle School to understand spatial orientation, and researching significant explorers like Captain Cook and Burke and Wills. A major focus is placed on the First Fleet, where students analyze the journey from a convict's perspective and write historical narratives. The unit also bridges past and present by examining modern exploration, such as expeditions to Antarctica. For their summative assessment, students conduct independent research on an explorer of their choice and present their findings using various digital tools, demonstrating their understanding of how exploration drives human progress and adaptation.

ci: People's beliefs contribute to the diversity of human life.
lo: Spiritual or religious beliefs; Similarities and differences of spiritual beliefs; Contributions of different religions to human diversity

s.d: To support the planned Week 7 virtual tours of places of worship, utilize ClassVR Headsets. In their assigned groups of three, students can rotate roles: one wearing the headset to explore a Mosque, Synagogue, or Temple, one guiding the experience, and the third recording their 'thin and thick' questions into a shared Microsoft Word document. This immersive experience provides concrete visual context to abstract religious concepts, making them highly accessible for Year 4 students.

Digitize the physical 'Question Walls' using a Padlet board with a 'Columns' layout, dedicating a column to each religion (Buddhism, Hinduism, Judaism, Christianity, Sikhism, Islam). Year 4 students can post their questions and thoughts as digital notes. This creates a living, accessible space where students can continually answer each other's questions throughout the 5-week unit, supporting collaborative inquiry and ensuring physical post-it notes aren't lost.

Instead of a static document for the 'My Religion Fact File', students can use Book Creator to design an interactive, multimodal report. They can embed the Clickview videos they watched during their research, use the pen tool to carefully draw religious symbols, and record audio of themselves explaining sacred texts. This allows for differentiated expression and caters to varying Year 4 reading and writing levels.

For the sorting activity where students revisit fact files to categorize different symbols, sacred texts, and places of worship, they can use Canva to create a visual graphic organizer. Using a teacher-provided template, students can drag and drop images and text into categorized sections. This visual sorting method helps Year 4 students clearly identify the similarities and differences between spiritual beliefs, directly supporting the second Line of Inquiry.

Microsoft Teams will serve as the central hub for collaborative inquiry and sharing throughout the unit. Students can utilize Teams channels to create 'interactive question walls,' posting their inquiries about spiritual and religious beliefs and engaging in text or video responses with peers. For the 'Religion Fact Files,' students can collaborate on research documents within Teams, and then present their findings through short video presentations recorded directly in Teams or during live meetings, fostering public speaking skills. The native video tools will also facilitate virtual tours of places of worship and allow students to share reflections on mentor texts. Finally, students can present their 'cultural posters' in a dedicated channel or live meeting, using video to explain their choices and engage in discussions that promote open-mindedness and respect for global diversity, directly replacing the video discussion functionality of 'flip' with a more integrated collaborative environment.

plannerText: This Year 4 unit, under the theme 'Who We Are,' explores how people's beliefs contribute to the diversity of human life. Over five weeks, students inquire into various spiritual and religious beliefs, examining their similarities, differences, and impacts on human diversity. Learning experiences include virtual and physical tours of places of worship, gallery walks, and reading mentor texts. Students formulate questions on interactive question walls and conduct guided research using Clickview to create 'Religion Fact Files' on major world religions like Judaism, Buddhism, Christianity, and Hinduism. The unit culminates in students sorting religious symbols and texts, and taking action by creating cultural posters that reflect their own identities, languages, and beliefs, fostering open-mindedness and respect for global diversity.

ci: People design for purpose.
lo: How design thinking creates in various ways; Design thinking meets community needs; Effective ways to engage an audience

s.d: During the Week 3 and 4 'Finding Out' phase on bushfires and floods, students use Google Earth to explore the topography of affected areas like Lismore. They can use the measurement tools to calculate distances between infrastructure and hazard zones, and drop pins to identify high-risk areas, building their spatial awareness and understanding of geographical impacts.

In Week 5 'Sorting Out', as students review their research and choose a stakeholder, they use Canva to collaboratively design digital Empathy Maps. They can use built-in templates to visually organize what their stakeholder (e.g., a child experiencing a flood) says, thinks, does, and feels, integrating icons and images to deepen their connection to the UNICEF brief.

During the Week 6 'Going Further' prototyping phase, students transition their 'Crazy 8s' and storyboards into 3D models using Tinkercad. They can design physical solutions for their chosen natural disaster (e.g., an earthquake-resistant shelter component or flood-rescue device) and use the 3D Printers to create tangible prototypes, applying their mathematical measurement and geometry skills.

For the Week 7 'Making Conclusions' pitch writing, students use Book Creator to compile their design journey into an interactive pitch deck. They can embed photos of their physical prototypes, record audio of their persuasive arguments explaining their design decisions, and link their solution directly to the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child.

In Week 8 'Taking Action', students record their final UNICEF pitches using Green Screen Kits and iMovie. They can superimpose themselves over footage of the natural disaster they are addressing or their digital topographical maps, creating a professional, persuasive video presentation that clearly communicates how their design meets the needs of their community.

plannerText: This Year 5 unit, under the theme 'How We Express Ourselves,' explores the central idea that 'People design for purpose.' Over 10 weeks, students engage in a design thinking process prompted by a UNICEF brief focusing on natural disasters and the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child. During the 'Finding Out' phase, students investigate the causes and impacts of bushfires, earthquakes, and floods using mapping tools and data analysis. In the 'Sorting Out' and 'Going Further' stages, collaborative groups select a specific disaster and child right, develop empathy maps, and ideate solutions using storyboards and prototyping. Finally, students refine their designs and write persuasive pitches, presenting their innovative solutions to an audience. The unit heavily integrates science (natural hazards), geography, and mathematics (measurement, data representation) to support authentic, purpose-driven design.

ci: Economic systems are an integral part of human society.
lo: Wealth is more than money; Responsibilities when managing income; Income is earned and managed in many ways

s.d: During the Week 4 'Needs and Wants' lesson, students use Canva to design a visually engaging infographic categorising physiological/safety needs versus desires/luxuries. They can utilise Canva's extensive icon library to represent these items. Once downloaded, students upload their graphic to the required Seesaw portfolio task and use the microphone tool to verbally justify their categorisation choices, demonstrating their grasp of financial literacy and values.

For the Week 6 'Government Spending' inquiry, instead of a static paper map, students use Google Maps (Street View and Satellite) to virtually explore Glen Waverley and locate public facilities like schools, parks, and libraries. They take screenshots of these locations, paste them into a collaborative Microsoft OneNote Class Notebook page, and use the digital inking tools to circle and annotate the infrastructure they believe is funded by taxpayer money.

Canva provides an ideal platform for students to visually articulate their understanding and project outcomes. For the 'Ideal Community' design thinking project, students can collaborate to create detailed maps and visual representations of their communities, showcasing equitable resource distribution. They can design infographics to explain their $1 million tax budget allocation, justifying their decisions for government spending and community needs.
