This supports the literacy focus on distinguishing topic sentences from details while keeping the cognitive load manageable for Year 2 students.

For the 'Robot Coding' experience, students work in pairs to build and program a simple robot using Lego Spike Essential. They can code their creations to navigate across a physical 'ecosystem' mat on the classroom floor, providing a tangible, hands-on demonstration of how technology interacts with and moves through an environment.

To complete the 'Screens & Satellites' comic strip about the evolution of TV, students use Canva's drag-and-drop comic templates. They can search for safe graphics representing different eras of television and add speech bubbles containing their 'Because/but/so' sentences, seamlessly blending digital design skills with their specific grammar and sequencing objectives.

While reading 'The Wild Robot' class novel, students draw a picture of Roz the robot and import it into Chatterpix Kids. They record a 30-second audio clip speaking in character as Roz, explaining how she is adapting to the island's ecosystem. This fosters empathy, deepens reading comprehension, and connects directly to the unit's central idea.

Students can utilize Seesaw's native video and audio recording features to articulate their understanding of ecosystems and the impact of technology. They can record themselves presenting their 'Robot Fact Files,' explaining their robot coding projects, or narrating their comic strips on the evolution of technology. Crucially, Seesaw can serve as a platform for students to practice and deliver their persuasive arguments on whether robots should replace teachers, allowing them to develop their oral persuasion skills and demonstrate their understanding of ethical implications. This directly supports vocabulary development and provides a rich medium for formative assessment of their comprehension and communication skills, mirroring the interactive sharing capabilities of 'flip'.

plannerText: This Year 2 unit under the theme 'How We Organise Ourselves' explores the central idea that technology can influence the way ecosystems survive. Students investigate the form, function, and changing nature of ecosystems, alongside the impact of technology and robotics. The learning sequence begins with tuning into the concept of an ecosystem and creating a 'Robot Fact File' focusing on topic sentences and bullet points. Students then engage in hands-on robot coding and explore the evolution of screens and satellites through comic strips and sequencing activities. The class novel, 'The Wild Robot,' serves as a continuous thread to contextualize how technology and nature interact. Finally, students synthesize their learning by writing a persuasive piece on whether robots should replace teachers, evaluating the ethical and practical implications of technology in society. Formative and summative assessments focus heavily on persuasive writing, vocabulary development, and understanding technological impacts.

ci: People and the planet are reliant on different energy sources.
lo: Energy types (electricity, fossil fuels, solar); Energy helps us in daily life; Energy transformation; Responsibility to conserve energy and find sustainable sources

s.d: During the 'Building electric circuits' lesson, students can use Makey Makey kits connected to their laptops to explore open and closed circuits. By testing different classroom materials (like paperclips or playdough) to complete the circuit and trigger a sound on the computer, Year 2 students gain a hands-on, concrete understanding of how electrical energy flows and what causes a circuit to break.

For the 'Sorting task - different energy source' activity, design an interactive Seesaw canvas. Year 2 students can drag and drop images of various energy sources (solar panels, coal, wind turbines, batteries) into categories. Utilizing the microphone feature allows them to verbally justify their sorting choices, reducing the cognitive load of writing while providing rich formative assessment on their understanding of energy types.

To elevate the 'Solar Energy - WRITING EXEMPLAR' task, have students publish their explanations using Book Creator. Year 2 students can type their planned sentences about how solar energy works, use the pen tool to draw diagrams of the sun and solar panels, and record their voices reading the text. This multimodal approach supports diverse literacy levels and creates a shareable digital artifact.

Students can leverage Seesaw's native video and audio recording features to document and articulate their understanding throughout the unit. They can record themselves explaining tangible energy demonstrations, detailing the process and outcomes of building electric circuits, and presenting their research findings on various energy sources. For the 'Tug of War' debate, students can record their persuasive arguments, outlining the pros and cons of fossil fuels or advocating for renewable energy. This approach allows Year 2 students to verbally demonstrate their conceptual understanding, engage in spoken debate, and reflect on sustainability, providing rich multimodal evidence of learning that is easily accessible for teacher feedback.

Connect to the 'Energy Transformation' and 'Electric circuits' investigations by having students build simple motorized models (like a windmill or carousel) using Lego Spike Essential. This provides a tangible, age-appropriate way for Year 2 students to observe how electrical energy from the battery hub is transformed into kinetic energy (movement), solidifying abstract energy concepts through play-based STEM.

plannerText: This Year 2 'How the World Works' unit investigates the central idea that people and the planet rely on different energy sources. Students explore the forms and functions of energy, including electricity, fossil fuels, and solar power, while understanding how energy transforms from one state to another. The learning sequence begins with tangible demonstrations of energy and progresses into hands-on experiments building and breaking electric circuits. Students then research various energy sources, weighing the pros and cons of fossil fuels through a 'Tug of War' debate and persuasive writing tasks. Finally, the unit emphasizes sustainability and responsibility, guiding students to understand how solar energy works and why transitioning to cleaner, renewable energy sources is vital for the planet's future. Formative assessments include sorting tasks, circuit building, and writing exemplars using specific conjunctions.

ci: To sustain life, people and environments depend on one another.
lo: Interdependence of people and environments; How people's wants and needs affect the land; Responsibility to use sustainable resources; Connection between fossil fuels and environmental impact

s.d: Use Padlet as a collaborative digital space for the 'How is electricity made?' and fossil fuel brainstorms. Year 2 students can easily tap to add images or use the voice record feature to share items that use electricity, reducing the cognitive load of spelling complex words while visually mapping out their collective prior knowledge.

Seesaw's native video and audio recording features are ideal for capturing student voice and facilitating the 'Tug of War' debate. Students can record their arguments for and against fossil fuels versus sustainable alternatives, explaining their reasoning and engaging in critical thinking. They can also use video/audio to share their reflections on sustainable practices and human impact, articulate their understanding of how electricity is made, or demonstrate their grasp of music concepts. This allows for rich, multimodal expression and provides a direct platform for students to share their learning and perspectives, mirroring the communicative and reflective goals of the unit.

During the 'Star Trek V' listening activity, students can use Sketchbook on their iPads to digitally paint their drawing response. They can experiment with different digital brush strokes and colours to represent the tempo and dynamics they hear in the music, easily undoing mistakes and layering their imaginative ideas.

For the 'Go Further' final vote, students use the Seesaw drawing and audio recording tools to publish their 'Gist Statement' about fossil fuels. They can write their sentence using the 'BUT' conjunction, illustrate their stance, and record their voice reading it aloud, allowing teachers to assess both grammar and verbal fluency.

As a summative reflection on 'What is solar energy?', students can create a collaborative class book in Book Creator detailing the pros and cons of solar energy versus fossil fuels. Year 2 students can use the built-in voice-to-text, add photos of solar-powered devices, and embed audio recordings to share their learning about sustainable practices.

plannerText: The Year 2 'Sharing the Planet' unit focuses on the central idea that people and environments depend on one another to sustain life, with a specific lens on energy resources. Students explore how electricity is made and society's heavy reliance on fossil fuels. Through provocations like a 'Tug of War' debate, they weigh the pros and cons of fossil fuels versus sustainable alternatives like solar energy. The unit creatively integrates music and movement, using film scores from Star Wars and Star Trek to explore rhythm, tempo, and dynamics. Literacy and numeracy are woven throughout via informative writing, gist statements using conjunctions, and exploring seasonal patterns across hemispheres. The unit culminates in reflections on sustainable practices and how human choices impact the planet.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Journeys can lead to challenges and new understandings.
lo: Types of journeys (physical, learning, cultural); Challenges encountered on journeys; New understandings gained through journeys

s.d: During the 'Tune In' phase reading picture books like 'The Snail & the Whale' or 'I am Sangoel', use Google Earth on iPads. Students can visually track the physical journeys of the characters across the globe, helping them concretely understand the concept of a 'change of location' and the geographical scale of the challenges the characters faced.

For the 'Tune In' Photo Sorting activity, utilize Freeform on iPads. Provide students with a shared digital canvas pre-populated with images of various journeys. In small groups, students can use touch gestures to drag, drop, and group the photos, using the drawing tools to annotate their reasoning for how they categorized physical, learning, or cultural journeys.

Students can utilize Seesaw's native video and audio recording features to document various stages of their learning journey. They can record themselves asking their WWWWH interview questions, capture audio or video snippets of their family member recounting a significant journey (with consent), or record their own reflections on the challenges and personal growth discussed. Furthermore, students can use the drawing tools and photo upload features to create and explain their 'Wave Template' analysis, adding audio explanations of the highs and lows. Finally, they can record themselves reading their own journey narratives aloud, sharing their understanding and reflections with the class and their families. This allows for rich, multimodal storytelling and personal reflection, making the learning visible and accessible for Year 2 students.

For the 'Go Further' Journey Writing task, transition from standard PowerPoint to Book Creator. Year 2 students can author their own multimodal journey stories, combining typed text, digital drawings, and voice recordings. This lowers the cognitive load for developing writers by allowing them to verbally narrate the 'growth' and 'opportunities' their characters experienced.

In the 'Reflect and Act' phase, students can synthesize their learning about how journeys cause growth. They can draw a picture of a traveler and use Chatterpix Kids to animate the mouth, recording a short verbal reflection on the challenges and opportunities of a journey. This video can then be seamlessly uploaded to their Seesaw journal for assessment.

plannerText: This Year 2 unit under the theme 'Where We Are in Place and Time' explores the central idea that journeys can lead to challenges and new understandings. Students investigate the vocabulary of journeys and opportunities, recognizing that journeys can be physical, cultural, or learning-based. The learning sequence begins with picture storybooks like 'The Snail & the Whale' and photo sorting to provoke thinking. Students then develop WWWWH (Who, What, When, Where, How) interview questions to ask a family member about a significant personal journey, focusing on the challenges faced and the resulting personal growth. Through the 'Wave Template' and CSPACE discussions, they analyze the highs and lows of these experiences. Finally, students apply their knowledge by writing their own journey narratives and reflecting on how different types of journeys impact our lives and shape our understanding of the world.

ci: The way we interact with others is influenced by who we are
lo: Personal identity and strengths; How self-regulation supports learning; Building resilience through knowledge of ourselves

s.d: For the 'All About Me Selfie' and 'Wifi - Ready to learn' Tune In activities, students use the camera and drawing tools in Seesaw to capture their portraits. They can use the microphone feature to verbally explain their personal needs and what helps them feel ready to learn, reducing the cognitive load of writing while practicing oral communication.

During the 'Mind Monsters' and 'Frames of Strength' Sort Out activities, students can design a digital page in a collaborative class book. Year 2 students can easily use the pen tool to draw their 'mind monsters' and use the audio recording button to explain the self-regulation strategies they use to overcome them, supporting emotional literacy.

Students can leverage Seesaw's native video and audio recording features to articulate their understanding of self-awareness, personal strengths, and self-regulation strategies. For activities like 'self-portraits' and 'Frames of Strength,' students can upload images and record audio explanations of their choices and identified strengths. They can create short video reflections demonstrating or explaining 'Mind Monsters' and the 'Calm Palm' strategy, sharing how they apply these in real-life scenarios. Peer reflections on student-created games can be captured via video or audio feedback. Crucially, the culminating 'Letter to my Year 3 Teacher' can be recorded as a personal video or audio message, allowing students to authentically articulate their learning needs, strengths, and self-regulation strategies developed throughout the unit, directly fostering student voice and metacognition.

Connecting to the learning goal 'What are the perspectives we need to consider when creating a game', students work in pairs using Lego Spike Essential to build and program a simple interactive game. This hands-on task requires them to practice the UR STRONG friendship skills, negotiate rules, and self-regulate during the collaborative building process.

For the final 'Letter to my Year 3 Teacher' Reflect and Act task, students draft their reflections using Microsoft Word. To support Year 2 students with varying spelling and fine motor abilities, teachers can introduce the 'Dictate' (speech-to-text) feature. Students can verbally reflect on their growth mindset and personal goals, then practice basic formatting skills.

plannerText: This Year 2 'Who We Are' unit focuses on self-awareness, personal strengths, and self-regulation. Students explore how their needs and the qualities they admire influence their choices and interactions. Key learning experiences include creating self-portraits, identifying personal strengths through 'Frames of Strength', and learning cyber safety. Students investigate emotional regulation through concepts like 'Mind Monsters' and the 'Calm Palm' strategy. The unit heavily integrates the UR STRONG curriculum to help students navigate 'Friendship fires' and develop a growth mindset. Formative assessments involve peer reflections on student-created games and setting personal goals. The unit culminates in a reflective 'Letter to my Year 3 Teacher,' where students articulate their understanding of their own learning needs, strengths, and the self-regulation strategies they have developed to bounce forward when faced with challenges.

ci: Methods of communication have different features which impact their effectiveness
lo: The forms of verbal, non-verbal and visual communication; How the correct form enhances the effectiveness of a message; How people interpret messages and bring their own meaning to interactions

s.d: During the 'Tune In' phase where students match the best method of communication with different audiences (friends, newsreaders, overseas businesses), students can use a collaborative Canva Whiteboard. They can drag and drop digital icons representing various communication tools (letters, emails, social media, broadcasts) next to the corresponding audience, visually mapping out their reasoning and discussing why certain features make a method more effective for specific groups.

For the 'Find Out' exploration of Morse code, students can use block coding to program Micro:bits to transmit simple messages. By coding the LED matrix to flash dots and dashes, or using the radio feature to send signals to a partner's device, Year 3 students gain a concrete, hands-on understanding of how early non-verbal, symbol-based communication systems functioned and required a shared code to be effective.

To support the 'Sort Out' activity involving a tally of the forms of communication that Year 3 uses, students can collaboratively design a simple Microsoft Form. They can survey their peers about their preferred communication methods (e.g., talking, video calls, gaming chat), and then use the automatically generated charts in the 'Responses' tab to interpret the data, integrating digital graphing with their math and inquiry skills.

Connecting to the 'Sort Out' music integration where students arrange dynamics and tempo, students can use Garageband on iPads to compose a short instrumental loop. They can experiment with the tempo settings and volume automation to physically hear and see how increasing or decreasing speed and volume changes the emotion and message being communicated to the listener without using any words.

For the student-initiated action of creating a 'silent movie', students can use iMovie to edit their recorded performances. Because they cannot use spoken dialogue, they can apply vintage black-and-white filters and insert text-based title cards to provide context, forcing them to rely heavily on their recorded body language, facial expressions, and written symbols to effectively communicate the narrative to their audience.

plannerText: The Year 3 'How We Express Ourselves' unit explores the central idea that methods of communication have different features which impact their effectiveness. Students investigate verbal, non-verbal, and visual forms of communication, analyzing how perspective and persuasion shape human interactions. Learning experiences include exploring historical and specialized communication systems like Morse code, Braille, and Auslan. The unit heavily integrates the Arts, with students examining how composers use tempo, dynamics, and metre to convey emotion in music, and how body language and facial expressions communicate meaning in Dance and Drama. Students apply their learning by tallying class communication habits, matching communication tools to specific audiences, and culminating in a student-led project where they plan, rehearse, and edit their own silent movies to demonstrate effective non-verbal storytelling.

ci: Ethical decision making supports online choices.
lo: How choice of action and reaction affects others online; How informed choices can make a positive difference; Our right to feel safe online and the responsibility to make ethical decisions

s.d: Connecting to the 'Sort Out' phase where students categorise findings from their homework task, the teacher can set up a Padlet with columns for 'Communication', 'Information', and 'Recreation'. Year 3 students can easily add posts with text or images of the apps they use at home into the correct columns. This visual, collaborative space reduces cognitive load while allowing the class to instantly see trends in their collective digital habits.

During the creation of the 'Online Essential Agreement', students can use Canva to design a visual poster of their co-constructed rules. Using Canva's extensive library of safe, age-appropriate icons and templates, Year 3 students can visually represent abstract concepts like 'privacy' or 'respect'. These completed graphics can then be exported and set as the lock screen on their school iPads as a constant reminder of their ownership.

Students will collaboratively create a multi-page digital book to document their learning and develop their 'iPad Essential Agreement'. Each student or group can contribute pages outlining their categorisation of apps (communication, information, recreation), their understanding of online rights and responsibilities, and reflections on ethical decision-making. They can embed text, images, audio recordings of discussions about family digital habits and cyber safety, and even short videos demonstrating safe online choices. For the summative reflection, students can dedicate a page to their self-assessment using a traffic light system, explaining their rationale through text or audio, demonstrating their ownership and understanding of the co-created agreements and safe online behaviours.

In the 'Go Further' stage, where students apply cyber safety skills, they can use Puppet Pals to animate a short role-play demonstrating a safe online choice (like asking a parent before downloading an app or dealing with an unkind message). Puppet Pals is highly accessible for Year 3 fine motor skills, allowing them to focus on the dialogue and ethical choices rather than complex animation mechanics.

For the 'Reflect and Act' traffic light self-assessment, students can use the drawing and microphone tools in Seesaw. They can draw a red, yellow, or green circle to indicate how well they are following the iPad essential agreement, and use the microphone to verbally explain a specific example of a safe online choice they made that week, capturing their reflection for both the teacher and parents to hear.

plannerText: This Year 3 unit under the theme 'How We Organise Ourselves' focuses on the central idea that ethical decision-making supports appropriate online choices. Students explore the purpose of familiar apps and websites, categorising them into communication, information, and recreation. Through discussions about family digital habits and cyber safety lessons, students investigate the balance between online and offline experiences. A major component of the unit involves students collaboratively developing and taking ownership of an iPad Essential Agreement. They learn about their rights to feel safe online, the importance of respectful digital relationships, and their responsibility to make informed choices. Formative assessments include categorising homework findings, while summative reflections involve students using a traffic light system to self-assess how effectively they are following their co-created essential agreements and demonstrating safe online behaviours.

ci: Materials behave and interact in certain ways, which poses challenges and solutions
lo: The three states of matter and their properties; How matter changes states depending on particles, temperature and energy; Responsible and ethical use of materials like glass and plastic

s.d: During the 'Tune In' phase where students observe their favourite snack, they can use the Seesaw drawing and microphone tools to complete their five-senses T-chart. Students can take a photo of their snack using the iPad camera, import it onto the Seesaw canvas, and use the microphone to verbally record their observations, reducing the writing load and allowing them to focus on rich scientific description.

For the 'Find Out' activity requiring students to find and photograph examples of each state of matter, they can use Book Creator to build a digital science journal. Students can walk around the school to capture photos of solids, liquids, and gases, insert them into their book, and use the audio recording feature to verbally explain the particle behaviour that classifies each item into its specific state.

When students are tasked with engineering a solution to slow down glaciers from changing from a solid to a liquid, they can use Freeform on their iPads as an infinite digital whiteboard. Working collaboratively, they can pull in reference images of glaciers, use drawing tools to sketch their protective structures, and add digital sticky notes to label the materials they would use to insulate the ice.

In the 'Go Further' chocolate-making activity, students can use Canva to design a visual flow chart explaining the changing states of matter. Using Canva's extensive library of age-appropriate graphics, students can sequence images of cocoa beans, melting vats, and solid chocolate bars, adding arrows and short text boxes to explicitly identify where the matter changes from solid to liquid and back again.

To support the student-initiated action of collecting soft plastics, students can use Chatterpix Kids to create engaging, persuasive PSAs for the school. By taking a photo of a piece of soft plastic, drawing a 'mouth' on it, and recording a 30-second voiceover, students can make the rubbish 'talk' to explain the environmental importance of recycling, which can then be shared with other classes.

plannerText: This Year 3 'How the World Works' unit investigates the scientific principles of matter, focusing on how materials behave, interact, and change states. Students explore the three main states of matter (solids, liquids, and gases) and the conditions required for them to change, such as adding or removing heat. Through hands-on learning experiences like melting ice cubes, observing snacks, and exploring the chocolate-making process, students develop their scientific inquiry and observation skills. The unit also incorporates an engineering and sustainability lens, challenging students to design conceptual solutions for melting glaciers and to investigate the ethical use and disposal of materials like glass and plastics. As a student-initiated action, the cohort actively engages in collecting and recycling soft plastics, demonstrating their understanding of environmental responsibility and the real-world impact of material usage.

ci: People can respond to natural disasters with resilience
lo: The causes and types of natural disasters (tectonic events, weather events); How natural disasters impact the environment and people; How humans respond with resilience and connection

s.d: During the 'Find Out' phase on tectonic plates, students use Google Earth to locate and drop pins on famous earthquake and volcano sites discussed in class (e.g., Japan, Christchurch). This interactive mapping helps Year 3s visualize the 'Ring of Fire' and understand the spatial relationship between tectonic boundaries and disaster zones before drawing them on their paper world maps.

Connecting to the prompt to 'make a structure that is Earthquake resistant', students use Lego Spike Essential kits to build and code a simple motorized shake table. They construct various building designs and test their resilience against the simulated tectonic movements, observing and evaluating which structural features best withstand the impact.

For the 'Earthquake backpack sorting activity', teachers can create a drag-and-drop Seesaw template where students sort essential and non-essential survival items. Crucially, students use the microphone feature to orally justify their choices, demonstrating their understanding of disaster preparedness and resilience without being hindered by writing stamina.

To elevate the 'Newspaper Article Writing' task about events like Cyclone Tracy, students use Canva's educational newspaper templates. This allows Year 3 students to authentically format their research with headlines, columns, and imported images, teaching them basic graphic design and digital publishing skills while presenting their factual information.

Extending the newspaper report, students transform their written articles into a live news broadcast. Using iPads and Green Screen Kits, students act as on-the-scene reporters or meteorologists, overlaying images of natural disasters behind them in iMovie to report on the event's impact and the community's resilient response.

plannerText: This Year 3 Sharing the Planet unit explores the central idea that humans respond to natural disasters and strive to move forward with resilience. Students investigate the scientific causes behind various natural disasters, including tectonic events (earthquakes, tsunamis, volcanoes) and weather events (cyclones, floods, bushfires). Through inquiry, they examine how these catastrophic events impact both the environment and human communities. Learning experiences range from mapping tectonic plates and understanding the 'Ring of Fire' to conducting science experiments and designing earthquake-resistant structures. Students also focus on disaster preparedness, such as analyzing survival kits and studying Japan's earthquake readiness. The unit culminates in a research project where students synthesize their knowledge by writing a newspaper article about a historical natural disaster, such as Cyclone Tracy, highlighting the event's impact and the community's connection and resilience in the face of adversity.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Many factors shape the development of cities.
lo: The essential features of a city; The function of a city; The development of cities through time

s.d: To support the 'city sounds' learning experience, Year 3 students can use GarageBand on iPads to collaboratively record and layer audio tracks representing different city zones (e.g., transport hubs, quiet parks). They can export these soundscapes, upload them to Seesaw, and use Canva to generate a QR code. Sticking these QR codes onto their physical class city model allows peers to scan and listen to the auditory environment they engineered.

For the global city research and comic task, students can first use Google Earth to virtually navigate the streets and infrastructure of their chosen city, taking screenshots of key amenities. They can then import these images into Book Creator, utilizing the comic panel layouts and speech bubbles to draft their graphic page. This supports their literacy development and reduces the fine motor strain of hand-drawing complex cityscapes.

Connecting to the '3D building design to withstand Natural disasters' inquiry, students can use Tinkercad to digitally model a structure with specific disaster-resistant features (like a wide base for earthquakes or an aerodynamic roof for cyclones). This introduces them to basic CAD skills and spatial reasoning. Selected designs can then be manufactured using the school's 3D Printers to populate the collaborative class city.

To execute the 'travel agency with a name and logo' and brochure creation tasks, students can use Canva. They can design a professional-looking logo and use a trifold brochure template to organize their research. Canva's text tools easily support the integration of Pinyin and Chinese characters, allowing students to demonstrate their language skills while practicing digital layout and graphic design.

To enhance the 'Label the facilities they?ve learnt in Chinese' activity, students can take digital photographs of the specific buildings in their physical class city. Using Chatterpix Kids, they can draw a 'mouth' on the facility and record their own voice speaking the correct Chinese vocabulary and Pinyin. These short, engaging videos can be shared to their Seesaw portfolios for teacher assessment of their pronunciation.

plannerText: This Year 3 unit, under the theme 'Where We Are in Place and Time,' investigates the central idea that many factors shape the development of cities. Students explore how cities function, adapt, and change over time due to demographics, density, and social needs. Key learning experiences include designing a class city from scratch, understanding the role of transport and infrastructure, and exploring how buildings are structured to withstand natural disasters. The unit integrates Chinese language learning through vocabulary related to city facilities and transport, as well as visual arts and music by creating city soundscapes and examining how surroundings inspire composers. Students demonstrate agency by designing their own travel agencies, brochures, and 3D disaster-resistant buildings.

ci: Health and wellbeing are impacted by the choices we make
lo: How our body systems connect and function; How our choices affect physical, emotional and mental wellbeing; Strategies to measure and maintain the health of the human body

s.d: During the 'Sort Out' phase, students can use Book Creator to publish their Body Systems Information Report. They can use iPad cameras to photograph their hand-drawn diagrams and insert them into their digital book. The audio recording feature allows Year 3 students to read their reports aloud, supporting oral language development and providing an accessible format to share with Year 4 students.

Book Creator perfectly supports the unit's pedagogical goals by enabling students to synthesize their learning in a dynamic, multimedia format. For the 'Human Body Systems' component, students can create interactive digital information reports, integrating text, diagrams, images, and even embedded videos to explain organ functions. In Physical Education, students can design personal fitness journals within Book Creator, documenting their training goals, recording pre- and post-fitness test results, and reflecting on their progress using text, photos, and audio. For Social-Emotional Wellbeing, students can create digital stories or scenarios illustrating 'friendship fires,' demonstrating conflict resolution strategies, and distinguishing between normal conflict and mean-on-purpose behavior. Finally, for Digital Wellbeing, collaborative books can be created where students contribute to and illustrate essential agreements for cyber safety, promoting a healthy online/offline balance. This platform allows students to showcase their understanding of health and wellbeing choices through a cohesive, interactive portfolio.

When exploring the URStrong program and 'Friendship Fires' in the 'Find Out' stage, students can use Puppet Pals on iPads to animate social scenarios. This allows them to safely role-play conflict resolution and 'putting out the fire' without the cognitive load or anxiety of acting on camera, reinforcing their social-emotional learning in a developmentally appropriate way.

For the 'Tune In' cyber safety essential agreements, students can use Canva to design a visual poster of their personal online safety rules. Using Year 3-friendly templates and drag-and-drop graphics, they can visually represent concepts like online/offline balance and safe communication, which can then be exported and uploaded to their Seesaw portfolio for parent viewing.

During the 'Find Out' investigation of body systems, students can use Merge Cubes to hold and inspect 3D models of human organs (like the heart or lungs). This tactile, augmented reality experience helps 8-9 year olds concretely visualize how different parts of a system connect and function before they begin drafting their written information reports.

plannerText: This Year 3 'Who We Are' unit explores the central idea that health and wellbeing are impacted by the choices we make. The inquiry delves into the physical, social, and emotional components of a healthy lifestyle. Students investigate human body systems, learning how different organs connect and function, and synthesize this knowledge by writing an information report. Physical education is deeply integrated, with students identifying components of fitness, setting personal training goals, and measuring their progress through pre- and post-fitness testing. Social-emotional wellbeing is addressed through the URStrong program, where students learn to navigate 'friendship fires' and distinguish between normal conflict and mean-on-purpose behaviour. Additionally, the unit emphasizes digital wellbeing, guiding students through cyber safety lessons to create essential agreements that promote a healthy balance between online and offline activities.

ci: The creative process involves understanding and applying artistic elements to communicate ideas and emotions across disciplines
lo: How colour, movement and music communicate character and mood; The creative process of making a multi-disciplinary presentation; The effort and commitment required to produce a live performance

s.d: During the 'Sort Out' phase where students design their Beijing Opera masks, they can use Sketchbook on iPads to draft their concepts. Using the symmetry tool and various digital brushes, Year 4 students can experiment with different colour palettes to represent their character's personality traits (e.g., red for loyalty, white for villainy) before committing to their physical mask creation.

To address the Music learning goals of composing a piece with tempo and dynamics, students can use Garageband's Smart Instruments or Live Loops. They can compose an original soundtrack that matches the mood and personality of their specific Beijing Opera character, adjusting the tempo and volume to reflect actions like a 'greedy thief' sneaking around.

When students develop their character's background story (e.g., 'hiding behind trash cans'), they can film their silent mime vignettes in front of a Green Screen. Using iMovie, they can replace the green background with an appropriate digital setting they have sourced or drawn, enhancing the visual storytelling of their performance.

For the final vignette performance, students can use iMovie to edit their recorded body-acting scenes. They can overlay their Garageband musical compositions onto the video and add silent-film style title cards to introduce their characters (Clown, Warrior, Villain), effectively combining Drama, Music, and Digital Technologies.

To meet the Digital Technologies goal of curating their work, students can use Book Creator to build an interactive digital portfolio. They can embed photos of their physical masks, their typed English scripts, their Garageband audio files, and their final video performances, providing a comprehensive space for self-reflection on their creative process.

plannerText: This Year 4 unit under the theme 'How We Express Ourselves' explores the creative process through a multi-disciplinary lens, integrating Dance, Drama, Music, Visual Arts, and Chinese. Students investigate how artistic elements communicate ideas and emotions, specifically focusing on the traditions of the Beijing Opera. They explore the cultural meaning of colours to design character masks and compose original music using tempo and dynamics to reflect specific character traits. Working collaboratively, students write scripts and choreograph silent vignettes using exaggerated mime and body acting. The unit culminates in a curated digital portfolio where students combine their physical artworks, musical compositions, and recorded performances, reflecting on how their artistic choices effectively communicated their character's story and personality to an audience.

ci: Not-for-profit organisations exist to respond to a variety of needs within our community
lo: The function and purpose of not-for-profit organisations; How not-for-profit organisations respond to community needs; Our responsibility to take action and make a difference

s.d: During the 'Tune In' phase exploring 'Wants and Needs' scenarios, use Padlet to facilitate a visible thinking routine. Students can collaboratively post their prior knowledge and questions about not-for-profit organisations, and categorize different family scenario cards into 'wants' versus 'needs' on a shared digital canvas, allowing the teacher to easily assess prior understanding.

For the 'Find Out' research phase where partnerships combine into groups of four, utilize the Collaboration Space in Microsoft OneNote. Students can co-author their data charts in real-time, documenting the NFP's name, purpose, target demographic, and reach (local/national/global) in a shared table, fostering digital teamwork and organization.

In the 'Sort Out' stage where students identify the 'BIG ISSUE/S' across different organisations, have students copy and paste the mission statements and functions of their researched NFPs into Word Clouds by ABCya. This visual representation will help Year 4 students easily identify recurring themes and core community needs based on the most prominent words.

During the 'Go Further' stage, students can use Canva to design the promotional flyers and posters for their chosen student-initiated actions (e.g., Oz Harvest donations, St Kilda Mums collection). Canva's templates and intuitive design tools will allow them to create visually engaging, persuasive media to form their 'flyer quilt' for the Junior School.

Microsoft Teams will serve as the central collaborative platform for students throughout this unit. Groups will utilize shared documents and channels within Teams to collaboratively conduct research on NFP organizations, compile findings, and plan the logistics of their fundraising or collection drive. This fosters strong self-management and research skills. For the advocacy phase, students will leverage Teams' native video tools to record persuasive short videos or 'public service announcements' about their chosen cause, which can be shared digitally or played during their visits to other Junior School classrooms. Additionally, they can collaboratively design digital promotional materials and presentations using integrated tools like PowerPoint within Teams, enhancing their communication skills and demonstrating their knowledge and caring attributes as they advocate for community needs.

plannerText: This Year 4 unit under the theme 'How We Organise Ourselves' explores the central idea that not-for-profit (NFP) organisations exist to respond to community needs. Students begin by differentiating between 'wants' and 'needs' through family scenarios. They then collaboratively research specific NFPs (local, national, and global) to understand their functions, target demographics, and the 'big issues' they address. Moving into the action phase, students form interest-based groups to plan and execute a fundraising or collection drive (e.g., Oz Harvest, St Kilda Mums). They design promotional flyers and posters, and visit other Junior School classrooms to advocate for their chosen cause, developing their communication, research, and self-management skills while demonstrating the learner profile attributes of being caring and knowledgeable.

ci: Engineers design and build structures using specific features and materials to make them strong, stable and fit for purpose
lo: Different types of bridges and their design features; How materials and engineering principles contribute to structural strength and stability; Testing and refining designs through experimentation

s.d: During the 'Tune In - See Think Wonder' phase and when students 'select from 5 buildings to research', use Google Earth to locate and explore famous bridges globally in 3D. Year 4 students can zoom in to observe specific structural features (like trusses, arches, or suspension cables) in real-world contexts, using these digital observations to fuel their inquiry questions and 'See, Think, Wonder' routines.

For the 'Sort Out - Building Bridges Activity', have students use Tinkercad to digitally prototype their bridge designs before constructing physical models. This allows students to experiment with geometric shapes (such as triangles for trusses) and understand how specific structural features provide stability, reducing cognitive load and material waste before they begin their physical construction.

Students will leverage Microsoft Teams channels to document their entire inquiry journey. They can record short videos explaining their research findings on famous bridges and structural failures, showcasing their design iterations for bridge models, and demonstrating the testing process with coins. These videos, along with photos and written reflections, can be shared within dedicated team channels, fostering peer feedback and collaborative learning. For Student Led Conferences, students can curate their video portfolio within Teams to present their understanding of bridge construction and engineering principles to parents, leveraging Teams' native video and sharing capabilities for a rich, interactive showcase of their learning.

For the 'Reflect and Act' stage where students are 'teaching parents re building and structure of bridges' at Student Led Conferences (SLC), students can author an interactive manual in Book Creator. They can embed photos of their bridge iterations, audio recordings explaining the properties of their chosen materials, and text detailing how they improved the bridge's weight-bearing capacity.

To support the 'Student Agency: Voice' goal where 'Students generate a brief informational text', students can use Canva to design a professional infographic. They can synthesize their research on different bridge types (beam, truss, suspension) and material properties (weatherproof, flexible), visually demonstrating their understanding of how engineers make structures fit for purpose over time.

plannerText: This Year 4 'How the World Works' unit explores the central idea that engineers design and build structures using specific features and materials to make them strong, stable, and fit for purpose. Students investigate different types of bridges, focusing on how structural design and material properties contribute to stability and sustainability. Through the inquiry cycle, students research famous bridges, analyze structural failures like the Tacoma Bridge collapse, and engage in hands-on design challenges. A key learning experience involves students working collaboratively to design, build, and test their own physical bridge models, using coins to measure weight-bearing capacity. The unit emphasizes student agency, allowing them to choose buildings to research and make iterative improvements to their designs based on testing feedback. Finally, students reflect on their learning and share their understanding of bridge construction with parents during Student Led Conferences.

ci: First Nations people have a deep connection to Country, language and culture
lo: The diversity of language and culture across First Nations communities in Australia; The significance of connection to Country as custodial responsibility; Listening to and learning from Indigenous perspectives

s.d: During the 'Sort Out' phase where students create a soundscape representing a page in a book, they can use Garageband on iPads. Students can layer audio recordings of their own voices, environmental sounds, and use Live Loops to evoke the specific time of day, animals, and scenery of an Australian location, demonstrating their understanding of how sound creates a sense of place.

For the 'Reflect and Act' activity requiring students to compare two Indigenous countries and complete a Venn diagram, students can use Canva. They can select a Venn diagram template to visually organize their research on the similarities and differences in language, culture, and landscape, developing their digital design and data representation skills.

To assess the students' ability to retell the Bunjil creation story through writing and artworks, students can use Stop Motion Studio on iPads. They can animate their own hand-drawn First Nations-inspired symbols and characters, adding a recorded voiceover to narrate the story, which provides a multimodal approach to assessing their comprehension.

During the 'Tune In' phase where students equate the diversity of First Nations countries to countries in Europe, they can use Google Earth. By exploring the satellite imagery of Australia, students can visually investigate the diverse geographical landscapes, climates, and environments across the continent, helping them understand why different First Nations groups have distinct cultures and practices.

When students are tasked with creating their own symbols and artwork based on a personal story, they can first use the Sketchbook app to digitally draft and refine their designs. Once their final acrylic painting is complete, they can capture a photo and upload it to Seesaw, using the microphone tool to record an oral reflection explaining their artwork's storyline and symbol meanings for their Student Led Conference.

plannerText: This Year 4 Sharing the Planet unit focuses on First Nations people's deep connection to Country, language, and culture. Students explore 'Country' as a living entity encompassing physical, spiritual, and cultural elements. They investigate the diversity of First Nations groups across Australia, comparing this diversity to European countries. Key learning experiences include an excursion to the Melbourne Museum, creating collaborative musical soundscapes to represent Australian environments, and comparing two different Indigenous countries. Assessments involve retelling the Bunjil creation story, articulating the environmental importance of trees, and designing personal acrylic artworks using self-created symbols. Students exercise agency by presenting their learning through various digital mediums during Student Led Conferences.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploration and discovery have led to changes that shape the world we live in
lo: The age of exploration and the reasons explorers set out on their journeys; How Australia changed through European arrival; Different perspectives on the consequences of exploration

s.d: Enhance the 'Tune In' map ordering activity by using Freeform on iPads. Provide students with a shared digital canvas containing scattered historical maps. Students can collaboratively drag and drop the maps into chronological order, using the drawing tools to circle key differences in cartography and adding audio notes to justify their timeline predictions.

Replace the laminated map cards in the 'Find Out' phase with Google Earth projects. Students can plot the specific routes of their assigned explorers (e.g., Captain Cook) using the path tool, adding placemarks at key historical stops. They can embed their research notes directly into the placemarks, creating an interactive 3D visualization of the journey.

Utilize Microsoft OneNote Class Notebook for the explorer research and note-taking phase. Students can use a structured graphic organizer distributed by the teacher to record the 'who, what, when, and why'. They can leverage the Immersive Reader tool to assist with reading comprehension of complex historical texts and use the dictation feature to help draft their coherent paragraphs.

Leveraging Microsoft Teams' native video capabilities, students can record and submit their summative presentations directly within assignments. This allows them to synthesize their research into coherent video paragraphs, articulating their reflections on the positive and negative impacts of historical exploration on Australia. Crucially, the video format provides an authentic platform for students to express and share their understanding of multiple perspectives, particularly the Indigenous Australian perspective regarding Captain Cook's arrival and colonization, fostering deeper empathy and critical thinking. Teachers can provide direct video or text feedback on these submissions, facilitating a rich, asynchronous discussion that mirrors the reflective and communicative goals of the unit.

For the final assessment where students create a text showing what they have learnt, use the paid online version of Book Creator. Students can synthesize their written paragraphs, digital maps, and recorded perspectives into a multimedia interactive eBook. They can use the voice record feature to narrate their paragraphs, supporting diverse learners in sharing their understanding.

plannerText: This Year 4 IB PYP unit, under the theme 'Where We Are in Place and Time,' investigates how exploration and discovery have shaped the modern world, with a specific focus on Australian history. Students explore the Age of Exploration and examine the pre- and post-European arrival landscape of Australia. Key learning experiences include analyzing and chronologically ordering historical maps, researching specific explorers to trace their global movements, and developing structured note-taking skills to document the 'who, what, when, and why' of these journeys. Crucially, the unit challenges students to consider multiple perspectives, particularly the Indigenous Australian perspective regarding Captain Cook's arrival and colonization. As a summative task, students synthesize their research into coherent paragraphs and presentations, reflecting on both the positive and negative impacts of historical exploration on Australia's demographics, identity, and environment.

ci: Leaders can help a group to function effectively
lo: Roles and responsibilities of effective leadership; Purpose of leadership; The strengths and qualities of a leader

s.d: During the 'Find Out' session where students create lists of attributes on an outline of a person, they can use Canva Whiteboard collaboratively. In groups of three, students can insert a graphic of a person's silhouette and use digital sticky notes to add leadership attributes. This allows them to easily group similar traits, color-code them, and add icons or images that represent each attribute, enhancing their visual mapping of what makes an effective leader.

Students can leverage Canva's intuitive video editor and presentation tools to create their 'persuasive digital pitch' for a school leadership role. They can record themselves, integrate images, text, and graphics to visually articulate their understanding of effective leadership qualities and justify why their attributes make them suitable. Canva's design capabilities will enable them to produce a polished and engaging pitch, enhancing the persuasive element and allowing them to creatively demonstrate their learning from the unit.

In Session 5, where students are asked to show what behaviors like 'listening' look like in different contexts, they can use iPads to stage and capture photographs of these scenarios. They can then import these photos into PicCollage to create a visual poster. Students can use the app's text and drawing tools to add labels, arrows, and annotations pointing out specific body language and actions that demonstrate the ROAR essential agreements.

When completing their independent or partner reports on a leader from the 'Little People, Big Dreams' series, students can use Book Creator to design an interactive digital biography. They can type their report findings, import relevant images of the leader, and use the audio recording feature to narrate their text. This provides a multimodal way to present their research and allows for easy sharing with the class.
Students use the video editor to create a professional 'Leadership Pitch' for their portfolio application, layering their spoken goals over images of them demonstrating ROAR values.

plannerText: In this Year 4 'Who We Are' unit, students explore the central idea that leaders can help a group to function effectively. The inquiry focuses on identifying the qualities of effective leadership and justifying why these attributes make a leader successful. Students begin by brainstorming examples of leaders in their lives and the wider world, breaking down the specific traits that contribute to their effectiveness. Through collaborative activities, they map out leadership attributes and connect them to the school's 'ROAR' essential agreements, visualizing what behaviors like active listening look like in practice. Students also research significant historical figures using the 'Little People, Big Dreams' series to understand diverse leadership styles. The unit culminates in a practical application where students reflect on their own leadership potential, identify a school leadership portfolio they are interested in, and create a persuasive digital pitch to apply for that role.

ci: Social norms and stereotypes can influence the way people express themselves
lo: How stereotypes and social norms form and persist in society; The impact of discrimination, prejudice and assumptions on individuals and communities; How people overcome stereotypes to express themselves and inspire change

s.d: During the 'Tune In' Provocation Gallery Walk, replace physical posters with a digital Padlet board. Students can view provocation images representing various social norms and use the comment feature to document their 'See, Think, Wonder' thinking routine. This reduces cognitive load by keeping all media in one accessible space and allows for equitable, simultaneous participation where students can read and build upon their peers' perspectives.

Students can utilize Microsoft Teams' integrated video recording features (e.g., through Stream or directly within assignments) to record and share their 'infographic presentation.' This allows them to verbally articulate their research on a changemaker, explain how the individual challenged discrimination or prejudice, and reflect on their impact on humanity. Teachers can create a video submission assignment, enabling students to demonstrate their understanding through personal expression and facilitating peer feedback and discussion within a dedicated Teams channel, perfectly aligning with the unit's reflective assessment goals.

Connecting to the 'Sort Out' Graffiti Article and student inquiries about Banksy's art, students use Canva to design their own digital street art. They will synthesize their learning by creating a visual piece that challenges a specific stereotype (e.g., gender roles in sports or career assumptions), utilizing Canva's extensive graphic elements and fonts to express a powerful social message without the mess of physical paint.

To elevate the 'Go Further' Famous Person PowerPoint task, students use the Adobe Express 'Animate from Audio' feature. They can record a short, first-person narrative speaking as their chosen activist who overcame prejudice. This animated character video can then be embedded directly into their M365 PowerPoint template, adding a dynamic, multimodal layer to their research and demonstrating deeper empathy for the historical figure.

For the 'Reflect and Act' phase, consolidate the individual activist research into a collaborative class eBook using Book Creator. Students design a multimedia page detailing how their public figure overcame societal expectations, embedding voice recordings, text, and images. The published digital book link can then be shared via the planned Seesaw task, providing an authentic audience for peer and parent feedback.

plannerText: In this Year 5 'How We Express Ourselves' unit, students inquire into how social norms and stereotypes influence personal expression. The unit begins with a 'See, Think, Wonder' gallery walk provocation to unpack prior knowledge about societal expectations. During the 'Find Out' phase, students define key terms like prejudice and discrimination, analyzing real-world stereotype scenarios. They explore street art, specifically Banksy, through a graffiti article to understand how art conveys powerful messages challenging societal norms. For their independent inquiry, students research a famous public figure or rights activist who overcame discrimination or prejudice, gathering information to create an infographic presentation. The unit culminates in a reflective assessment where students share their findings on these changemakers via Seesaw, demonstrating their understanding of how individuals can challenge stereotypes, pave the way for others, and express themselves to positively impact humanity.

ci: Goods and services are exchanged within a marketplace
lo: The production of goods from raw materials through manufacturing to consumer; The exchange of currency for goods and services in a functioning economy; Ethics and responsible decision-making in the marketplace

s.d: During the 'Launch Suburb' phase, students use Tinkercad to collaboratively design the 3D floor plan of their suburb. They can map out streets, allocate zones for their student businesses, and design the physical layout of the marketplace, enhancing their spatial awareness and design thinking skills before trading begins.

For the 'Production Chains' activity, students use Stop Motion Studio on iPads to animate the lifecycle of their chosen product. Using craft materials or whiteboards, they visually demonstrate the transition from raw materials (primary stage) to manufacturing (secondary stage), retailing (final stage), and distribution, making abstract economic concepts concrete.

When setting up their student businesses, students use Canva to design professional logos and branding materials. They then embed these visual assets into a formal business proposal document created in Microsoft Word, outlining their target market, goods/services, and ethical considerations to pitch to their peers.

To support the 'Intro Spreadsheet/Budget' and 'Suburb Trading' lessons, students use Microsoft Excel to manage their business finances. By setting up basic formulas to track startup bonuses, fines, and daily trading profits or losses, Year 5 students develop crucial financial literacy and data management skills.

During the 'Ethics (collaborative research & role play)' lesson, students use the Rodecaster and podcasting microphones to record an 'Ethics in Business' interview. In character as business owners or consumers, they role-play scenarios discussing fair trade, inflation, and ethical decision-making, developing their oral communication and critical thinking skills.

plannerText: This Year 5 How We Organise Ourselves (HWOO) unit focuses on the central idea that goods and services are exchanged within a marketplace. Students explore economic fundamentals, including supply and demand, production chains, and the ethical responsibilities of businesses and consumers. The inquiry cycle begins with tuning into sustainability and resource management games. During the 'Find Out' phase, students investigate production chains from raw materials to distribution and analyze the cause and effect of inflation and cost of living. The unit culminates in a highly engaging 'Launch Suburb' simulation where students design a town floor plan, apply for jobs, create business proposals, and design logos. Using spreadsheets, they manage budgets, track profits and losses, and engage in active suburb trading. This hands-on simulation deeply integrates financial mathematics, civics, and digital literacy, allowing students to experience the real-world dynamics of running an ethical business in a functioning economy.

ci: Inventions and initiatives are a response to the changing needs of society
lo: How inventions have changed over time to meet the demands of society; The UN Sustainable Development Goals and the global problems they address; Using the design thinking process to create innovations that respond to real-world needs

s.d: During the 'What's That Invention' provocation, students use ClassVR Headsets to virtually explore historical museums or environments. This immersive experience lowers cognitive load by providing concrete visual context, helping Year 5s hypothesize the purpose and features of past inventions before writing their answers.

For the 'Changed Over Time' inquiry, students use Book Creator to design an interactive timeline of their chosen modern invention. They can embed images from each decade and use the audio recording tool to verbally explain the three significant changes, supporting diverse literacy levels and multimodal communication.

During the 'SDGs further investigation', students utilize OneNote as a digital workbook to construct their T-Charts. They can collaboratively gather facts, figures, and targets about their top 3 global goals, using drawing tools to annotate infographics and seamlessly organizing their research for the upcoming design process.

In the 'Go Further' prototype stage, students elevate their Canva AI concepts by designing functional 3D models of their SDG inventions in Tinkercad. Printing these models on 3D Printers provides a tangible artifact for the Future Thinkers' Forum, developing spatial awareness and engineering skills appropriate for Year 5.

Microsoft Teams provides a robust, integrated platform that perfectly supports the collaborative, research-driven, and design thinking goals of this unit. Students can form dedicated 'Teams' for their SDG groups, using channels for structured communication, file sharing (for research on global problems and design thinking documentation), and co-authoring documents. The native video tools are ideal for students to record their 'Empathise' interviews, 'Define' problem statements, 'Ideate' brainstorming sessions, and most importantly, to present their 'Prototypes' and final innovative solutions to the community at the Future Thinkers' Forum. This fosters deep collaboration, critical thinking, and allows students to document and share their entire design thinking journey, promoting student agency and global citizenship.

plannerText: This Year 5 unit under the theme 'How the World Works' explores how inventions and initiatives respond to society's changing needs. Students begin by investigating past inventions and tracking how modern household or technological items have evolved over decades to meet new demands. Integrating science, students explore elements like light and steam. The unit heavily focuses on the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), where students research global problems and use the design thinking process (Empathise, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test) to create their own solutions. Working in collaborative groups based on shared SDG passions, students develop prototypes and present their innovative ideas to the community at the Future Thinkers' Forum, fostering student agency, critical thinking, and global citizenship.

ci: Communities interact with the environment in different ways
lo: How community values shape the management of the natural environment; The need for conservation and environmental stewardship; Ways in which First Nations land management practices can drive positive environmental change

s.d: During the 'Find Out' and 'Ongoing Assessment' phases focusing on world biomes, students can use Google Earth's creation tools to build interactive maps. They can drop pins on different global habitats, adding text, images, and their own observations about how specific communities interact with these unique environments.

For the 'Go Further' activity where students write emails to Melbourne City Trees, they can draft their correspondence in Microsoft Word. Using the Editor tool and built-in accessibility features like Immersive Reader, Year 5 students can refine their persuasive language and ensure their messages about stewardship are clear and impactful.

In the 'Sort Out' phase focusing on Indigenous Land Management, students can use Book Creator to synthesize their learning. They can design a multimedia digital book that combines text, imported images of native flora, and embedded audio recordings of their reflections on First Nations peoples' enduring connection to Country.

To support the peer assessment opportunity where students create posters to share environmental awareness, Canva provides an ideal platform. Students can utilize Canva's extensive design elements to visually communicate strong messages about conservation and stewardship, applying visual literacy skills appropriate for Year 5.

For the 'Student-initiated action' involving the design of native garden beds around the Year 5 'Rose Garden', students can use Tinkercad. This 3D modeling tool allows them to spatially plan their garden layouts, scale different native plant species, and present a digital prototype of their stewardship project to the Environment Prefects.

plannerText: The Year 5 'Sharing the Planet' unit focuses on the central idea that communities interact with the environment in different ways. Students explore concepts of connection, perspective, responsibility, and conservation. The learning sequence begins with unpacking environmental vocabulary and exploring global habitats and biomes. Students then investigate conservation and stewardship, with a specific focus on Indigenous land management practices and First Nations peoples' connection to Country. Key learning experiences include a mapping task of world biomes, writing emails to Melbourne City Trees to advocate for nature, and participating in an excursion to Chum Creek and ECOSS. Formative assessments involve creating awareness posters and reflecting on human impacts on the environment. The unit culminates in student-initiated action, where students apply their learning about native species and stewardship to collaboratively design and plant native garden beds around the Year 5 'Rose Garden' alongside Environment Prefects.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Colonisation has had a lasting impact on different countries and their people
lo: The reasons, methods and impact of colonisation; How colonisation transformed society and culture; The responsibility of colonisers and the perspectives of those colonised

s.d: During the 'Tune In' activity for 'The Rabbits', students can use Canva to digitally design their 'sunglasses lenses'. Instead of drawing on paper, they can search for and manipulate symbolic graphics representing the contrasting perspectives of the colonisers and the Indigenous population. They can then use the text tools to type their paragraphs regarding motivation and experience directly alongside their digital artwork, creating a visually impactful multimedia poster.

For the 'Breaking down the Central Idea' activity, utilize Microsoft OneNote Class Notebook as a digital interactive workbook. Students can use the digital inking tools to highlight significant words in the Central Idea. They can then leverage the 'Insert Online Pictures' feature to safely search for and paste images directly connected to their highlighted words, using the infinite canvas to map out their initial understandings and brainstorms.

For the 'Find Out' research phase, host the required Australian History video on Clickview. Teachers can utilize Clickview's interactive video feature to embed formative comprehension questions at key moments regarding the methods and impacts of colonisation. This ensures active viewing and checks for understanding before students move on to complete their summarizing templates.

To elevate the summative assessment where students create a PowerPoint on a chosen country, integrate Green Screen Kits. Students can design their PowerPoint slides featuring historical maps and cultural images of their researched country. They can then record their oral presentation standing in front of a green screen, superimposing themselves into their slides like a historical documentary or news report, increasing engagement and presentation skills.

During the 'Reflect and Act' and 'Go Further' stages, students can use Adobe Express to create a short podcast episode. They can script and record an audio reflection that draws connections between the themes of displacement in their class novel 'Convict Boy' and the modern examples of invasion they researched. This provides a powerful, alternative medium for students to articulate complex conceptual connections.

plannerText: This Year 5 unit under the theme 'Where We Are in Place and Time' explores the central idea that colonisation has a lasting impact on countries and their people. Students investigate the reasons, methods, and impacts of colonisation, focusing on the transformation of societies and cultures. Learning experiences begin with analyzing perspectives using the picture book 'The Rabbits' and breaking down the central idea. Students then conduct guided research on British colonisation in Australia, utilizing videos and templates to build their understanding. They extend their learning by examining modern examples of invasion and making connections to their class novel, 'Convict Boy.' For their summative assessment, students exercise agency by choosing a specific country to research, ultimately creating and delivering a presentation to their peers that demonstrates their understanding of colonisation's historical and ongoing impacts.

ci: Values, beliefs and actions contribute to a diverse society
lo: How our values and beliefs shape our identity and interactions; Our responsibility to manage emotions and be safe online and in person; The importance of resilience, respect and positive relationships in a diverse community

s.d: During the 'Tune In' phase, instead of a physical craft, students use Canva to design a digital 'T-shirt About Me'. They can leverage Canva's extensive library of graphics, fonts, and templates to visually represent their core values, cultural background, and personal beliefs. These digital designs can be downloaded and shared on a class display or Padlet, allowing Year 5 students to celebrate their diverse identities while developing digital design skills.

To explore the 'Grow Your Mind: Meet the animals in your brain' lesson, students use Adobe Express Character Animations. They select a digital avatar (representing the 'guard dog' amygdala or 'wise owl' prefrontal cortex) and record their voice explaining how this part of the brain reacts during a stressful moment or 'friendship fire'. This provides a highly engaging, low-anxiety way for students to articulate complex emotional regulation strategies.

As part of the 'Find Out' phase covering the 16 lessons of CyberSafety, students collaborate to author a 'CyberSafety Survival Guide' in Book Creator. Small groups take on specific inquiry questions (e.g., 'How to be an upstander' or 'Recognising fake friends') and use Book Creator's multimodal tools to insert text, audio recordings, and comic-style scenarios, synthesizing their learning into a professional digital handbook for their peers.

During the 'Sort Out' phase focusing on the URStrong program, students use Puppet Pals on their iPads to role-play resolving 'friendship fires'. They animate characters navigating a social misunderstanding versus purposeful hurtful behaviour, recording dialogue that demonstrates conflict resolution and upstander behaviour. This provides a safe, low-stakes digital environment for 10-11 year olds to practice difficult social conversations.

Microsoft Teams provides a robust platform for students to record and share short video reflections, directly replacing the functionality of Flip. Within dedicated channels, students can record videos explaining their 't-shirts about me', reflecting on the 'animals in their brain' (neuroscience of emotions), or demonstrating how to be an upstander in various scenarios. They can also present their 'golden rule' for cyber safety through video. The native video tools allow for easy creation, and the channel structure facilitates peer viewing, commenting, and discussion, fostering relationship building, diverse perspectives, and collaborative problem-solving around friendship fires and cyber safety. Teachers can use Teams for wellbeing check-ins, setting collaborative goals, and facilitating live discussions or role-playing, ensuring a secure and integrated environment for all unit activities.

plannerText: This Year 5 'Who We Are' unit focuses on how values, beliefs, and actions contribute to a diverse society, with a strong emphasis on student wellbeing, cyber safety, and relationship building. Through programs like URStrong, the Cyber Safety Project, and Grow Your Mind, students explore what it means to be responsible and safe both in person and online. Key learning experiences include creating 't-shirts about me', understanding the 'animals in your brain' (neuroscience of emotions), and learning how to be an upstander rather than a bystander. Students investigate how to manage their own emotions, navigate friendship fires, and recognise fake friends. Formative and summative assessments involve goal setting, self-reflection, and creating a 'golden rule' for cyber safety, ultimately empowering students to apply resilience strategies and interact positively within their school and digital communities.

ci: Understanding global issues and using effective communication empowers us to raise awareness and inspire action.
lo: Global issues are caused by interconnected factors that require collective solutions (Causation) ? Presentations in the media can reflect the perspective of the author rather than the truth (Perspective) ? We must make responsible choices when responding to and creating media (Responsibility) ? Messages can be expressed through a range of mediums and elements can be adjusted to enhance effectiveness (Communication) ? Design choices contribute to the effectiveness of a message (Influence)

s.d: During the 'Find Out' phase, students use Padlet's canvas format to collaboratively build their Lotus Diagrams and Root Cause Analyses for their chosen global issue. They can link articles, images, and videos directly to the nodes, allowing for a dynamic, shared workspace that captures their initial MISO research and helps them formulate their Central Idea.

Connecting to the visual arts integration, students use Canva's design suite to draft their PYP Exhibition logos, applying principles like color, line, and emphasis to represent their global issue. They then export their designs and upload them to Seesaw, using the microphone tool to record a peer-assessment reflection on how their design choices effectively communicate their message.

In the 'Sort Out' phase, students use Microsoft Word's built-in 'References' tab to correctly format their bibliographies from library databases. They then synthesize their research onto collaborative Microsoft PowerPoint 'Sorting Out' slides (color-coded yellow, blue, and green as per the planner) to organize their findings for their three lines of inquiry.

For their 'Action' advocacy piece, students script and film a persuasive media campaign using Green Screen Kits to place themselves in environments relevant to their global issue (e.g., a deforested area). They edit the footage in iMovie, adding voiceovers, transitions, and royalty-free backing tracks to enhance the emotional impact of their message for the Exhibition.

Students opting for an audio-based media campaign utilize the Rodecaster and professional mics to record an investigative podcast about their global issue.
